# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define wastewater. List any four sources of wastewater generation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Wastewater** is any water that has been used for domestic, commercial, industrial, or agricultural purposes and contains dissolved, suspended, or colloidal impurities that render it unsuitable for direct disposal or reuse.

**Four sources of wastewater:**
1. **Domestic/Sanitary wastewater** – from toilets, bathrooms, kitchens in households.
2. **Industrial wastewater** – from manufacturing and processing plants (e.g., chemical, textile, food).
3. **Agricultural runoff** – from fields and farms carrying fertilizers, pesticides, and livestock waste.
4. **Stormwater** – rainwater runoff from urban surfaces carrying litter, oil, and grit.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between BOD and COD. Why is BOD/COD ratio important?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand) | COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Oxygen consumed by microbes in 5 days at 20°C | Oxygen consumed by strong chemical oxidant (K₂Cr₂O₇) |
| Measures | **Biodegradable** organics only | **Total** oxidizable organics (biodegradable + non-biodegradable) |
| Test Duration | 5 days | 2–3 hours |
| Value | Always < COD | Always > BOD |
| Apparatus | BOD bottles, DO meter, incubator | Reflux apparatus, titration |

**Importance of BOD/COD ratio (r):**
- Indicates **biodegradability** of wastewater.
- If **r > 0.5** → wastewater is **easily treatable** by biological methods.
- If **r < 0.2** → wastewater contains **refractory (non-biodegradable)** organics; needs chemical/physical treatment first.
- Used in process selection — high r → ASP/TF; low r → ozonation/adsorption.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme Part B regulations, the student answers **one** of the two questions, each carrying 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Q. (a)** Classify wastewater based on its source and composition. List any five types of water pollutants with their primary environmental effects. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With neat labeled sketches, explain the **principle and working** of:
  (i) Grit chamber
  (ii) Activated Sludge Process **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Classification of Wastewater:**

**By Source:**
1. Domestic wastewater
2. Industrial wastewater
3. Agricultural wastewater
4. Stormwater
5. Institutional/Commercial wastewater

**By Composition:**
1. Organic wastewater (e.g., dairy, food)
2. Inorganic wastewater (e.g., metal finishing)
3. Toxic wastewater (e.g., pharmaceutical)
4. Radioactive wastewater (e.g., nuclear plants)
5. Thermal wastewater (e.g., power plant cooling)

**Five water pollutants and their effects:**

| Pollutant | Effect |
|---|---|
| Suspended solids | Reduce light penetration; clog fish gills |
| Organic matter (high BOD) | Depletes DO → fish kills |
| Nutrients (N, P) | Eutrophication, algal blooms |
| Heavy metals (Pb, Hg, Cd) | Bioaccumulation, neurological damage, cancer |
| Pathogens | Waterborne diseases (cholera, typhoid) |

**(b) Principle and Working:**

**(i) Grit Chamber:**
- **Principle:** Gravity separation by differential settling velocity. Grit (inorganic particles like sand, gravel with specific gravity ≈ 2.65) settles out while lighter organic matter remains suspended.
- **Working:** Wastewater flows through a long, narrow channel at a controlled velocity of **0.3 m/s**. This velocity is just sufficient to keep organic solids in suspension (which have a settling velocity lower than 0.3 m/s) but allows inorganic grit to settle. Detention time is **45–90 seconds**. Settled grit is removed by a mechanical scraper to a hopper. Types include horizontal flow, aerated, and vortex chambers.

**(ii) Activated Sludge Process (ASP):**
- **Principle:** It is a **suspended-growth aerobic biological process** in which a flocculated mass of active microorganisms (activated sludge) is maintained in suspension and continuously aerated. Microbes metabolize dissolved organic matter into CO₂, H₂O, and new biomass.
- **Working:**
  1. **Aeration Tank:** Primary effluent is mixed with **Return Activated Sludge (RAS)** from the secondary clarifier. Diffused air or surface aerators supply oxygen (DO 1.5–2.5 mg/L). Microbial F/M ratio maintained at 0.2–0.5/day. Hydraulic retention time 4–8 hours.
  2. **Secondary Clarifier:** The mixed liquor flows into the clarifier where biomass settles as sludge. The clarified supernatant (treated effluent) overflows to tertiary treatment or discharge.
  3. **Sludge Recycle:** A portion of settled sludge (RAS) is returned to the aeration tank to maintain microbial concentration. Excess sludge (WAS) is wasted to sludge processing.
  4. **Removal Efficiency:** 85–95% BOD removal, 90–95% COD removal.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Q. (a)** What are the major types of water pollutants? Discuss in detail the **sources, effects, and treatment approaches** for heavy metal pollution in wastewater. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **principle and working** of the following with appropriate diagrams:
  (i) Trickling Filter
  (ii) Reverse Osmosis **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Major Water Pollutants and Heavy Metal Pollution:**

**Major types of water pollutants:**
1. Suspended solids, 2. Organic matter, 3. Nutrients, 4. Pathogens, 5. Heavy metals, 6. Synthetic organic chemicals, 7. Inorganic salts, 8. Thermal pollutants, 9. Radioactive substances.

**Heavy Metal Pollution — Sources:**
- Mining and ore processing
- Electroplating and metal finishing industries
- Battery manufacturing (Pb, Cd)
- Tanneries (Cr)
- Pesticides and fertilizers (As, Hg)
- Coal combustion

**Effects of Heavy Metals:**
- **Lead (Pb):** Neurological damage, especially in children; anemia, kidney damage.
- **Mercury (Hg):** Methylmercury causes **Minamata disease** (neurological); bioaccumulates in fish.
- **Cadmium (Cd):** **Itai-itai disease** — bone and kidney damage.
- **Chromium (Cr⁶⁺):** Highly toxic, carcinogenic.
- **Arsenic (As):** Skin lesions, cancers, cardiovascular diseases.
- Heavy metals are **non-biodegradable** and **bioaccumulative** — they magnify up the food chain.

**Treatment approaches for heavy metals:**
1. **Chemical Precipitation:** Adjust pH to convert dissolved metals to insoluble hydroxides, then settle (most common).
2. **Ion Exchange:** Resins exchange H⁺/Na⁺ for metal ions.
3. **Adsorption:** Activated carbon, biosorbents (e.g., chitosan).
4. **Membrane Filtration:** Nanofiltration and reverse osmosis.
5. **Electrodialysis:** Electric field drives ions through selective membranes.
6. **Phytoremediation:** Aquatic plants (e.g., water hyacinth) uptake metals.

**(b) Principle and Working:**

**(i) Trickling Filter:**
- **Principle:** It is an **attached-growth aerobic biological treatment**. A microbial slime (biofilm) grows on a stationary bed of rock or plastic media. As wastewater trickles down over the media, the biofilm adsorbs and metabolizes dissolved organics, using oxygen drawn in through natural ventilation.
- **Working:**
  1. **Rotating Distributor Arm:** Sprays wastewater uniformly over the filter bed from the top — intermittent dosing allows aeration.
  2. **Filter Media Bed:** Rocks (25–100 mm) or modern plastic media with high surface area and void space.
  3. **Biofilm (Zoogleal Slime):** Microorganisms form a thin, sticky layer; outer layer is aerobic, inner layer near the rock is anaerobic.
  4. **Underdrain System:** Collects treated effluent at the bottom; doubles as an air inlet.
  5. **Sloughing:** Aged biofilm peels off and is captured in the secondary clarifier.
  6. **Removal Efficiency:** 60–85% BOD removal.

**(ii) Reverse Osmosis (RO):**
- **Principle:** RO uses a **semi-permeable membrane** that allows water molecules to pass through but rejects dissolved salts, heavy metals, organics, and other contaminants. The driving force is **hydraulic pressure** applied in excess of the feed water's osmotic pressure.
- **Working:**
  1. **Pretreatment:** Feed water is pre-filtered (to remove SS that would foul the membrane) and sometimes softened.
  2. **High-Pressure Pump:** Pressurizes feed to 10–80 bar (for brackish water: 10–30 bar; seawater: 50–80 bar) — exceeds osmotic pressure of the feed.
  3. **Membrane Module:** Spiral-wound or hollow-fiber membrane. Water (permeate) passes through; contaminants (reject/brine) are flushed out.
  4. **Energy Recovery:** Modern systems recover pressure from the reject stream.
  5. **Post-treatment:** Permeate is often re-mineralized before use.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Conflating BOD and COD:** Many students write "BOD is the same as COD" or skip the biodegradable vs. total distinction. **Loss: 1–2 marks per question.**
> 2. **Missing the "Why" of velocity control in grit chambers:** Examiners award marks for explaining *why* 0.3 m/s is chosen (settles grit, not organics). Generic answers lose marks.
> 3. **No mention of microbial role in secondary treatment:** Activated sludge and trickling filter marks depend on explaining the **biological mechanism** — simply saying "microbes eat waste" without naming the products (CO₂, H₂O, new biomass) is incomplete.
> 4. **Confusing MBBR with ASP:** In ASP, sludge is returned; in MBBR, carriers move freely and there is **no return sludge**.
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical sub-parts:** Always write BOD in mg/L, flow in m³/d, HRT in hours.
> 6. **Forgetting sludge management:** Many answers describe the liquid train but ignore that sludge is also produced. Examiners often allocate 1–2 marks for this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1)**

**Core Concepts:**
- Wastewater = used water with dissolved/suspended/biological contaminants.
- Water pollution = contamination reducing fitness for intended use.

**Sources of Wastewater (Mnemonic — "DISAS"):** **D**omestic, **I**ndustrial, **S**tormwater, **A**gricultural, **S**pecial/Institutional.

**Pollutant Categories (Mnemonic — "SOP HINT"):** **S**uspended solids, **O**rganic matter, **P**athogens, **H**eavy metals, **I**norganic salts, **N**utrients, **T**hermal.

**BOD/COD Rule of Thumb:**
- BOD₅ (5-day, 20°C) ≈ **68%** of ultimate BOD (L).
- COD > BOD always.
- BOD/COD > 0.5 → biodegradable; < 0.2 → refractory.

**Treatment Hierarchy (Mnemonic — "PPST"):** **P**reliminary → **P**rimary → **S**econdary → **T**ertiary.

**Treatment Stage–Pollutant Pairing:**
- Screens → Large solids
- Grit chamber → Sand/grit
- Skimmer → Oil/grease
- Clarifier → Settleable solids
- ASP / TF / MBBR / SBR / Pond → Dissolved BOD
- Filtration → Residual SS
- Chlorination/UV → Pathogens
- RO / Ion exchange → TDS / heavy metals
- Activated carbon → Color, odor, refractory organics

**Key Equipment Data (Must Memorize):**
- Grit chamber velocity: **0.3 m/s**; HRT: **45–90 s**.
- Primary clarifier: SOR 30–50 m³/m²/d; HRT 1.5–2.5 h.
- ASP: MLSS 1500–4000 mg/L; F/M 0.2–0.5/d; SRT 5–15 d; DO 1.5–2.5 mg/L.
- RO pressure: **10–30 bar** (brackish), **50–80 bar** (sea).

**Heavy Metal Disease Names (Board-Favorite):**
- Hg → Minamata disease
- Cd → Itai-itai disease
- As → Blackfoot disease / cancers
- Pb → Saturnism / anemia

**Sludge Train (Always Mention!):** Thickening → Digestion (anaerobic/aerobic) → Dewatering → Disposal.

**Exam-Favorite Tag Lines:**
- "BOD measures biodegradable organics; COD measures total organics."
- "BOD/COD ratio is the master indicator of treatability."
- "Microbial oxidation is the heart of secondary treatment."
- "Tertiary treatment is for polishing, not for primary pollutant removal."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical treatment methods: screening, grit removal, sedimentation, flotation, filtration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT613 - PROCESS DESIGN FOR POLLUTION CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Physical treatment methods: screening, grit removal, sedimentation, flotation, filtration # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are Physical Treatment Methods? **Physical Treatment Methods** are the primary (preliminary and primary) unit operations in a municipal/industrial wastewater treatment plant that exploit *physical properties* — primarily **size, density, and settling velocity** — to remove suspended and floating solids from wastewater. They involve **no chemical or biological reactions** and act as the "first line of defense" before biological/chemical treatment stages. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *Physical treatment methods are unit operations that rely on physical forces (gravity, inertia, flotation, mechanical straining) to separate solids from the liquid phase of wastewater.* --- ## 1.2 The Five Core Operations (Treatment Train Order) | # | Unit Operation | Primary Removal Target | Physical Property Exploited | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Screening** | Large debris (rags, plastics, sticks) | Size exclusion | | 2 | **Grit Removal** | Sand, gravel, eggshells (high-density inorganics) | Differential settling | | 3 | **Sedimentation** | Fine suspended solids (organic & inorganic) | Gravity settling | | 4 | **Flotation** | Oil, grease, low-density suspended matter | Buoyancy | | 5 | **Filtration** | Residual fine/colloidal particles | Mechanical straining + adhesion | > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently tests the **sequence** of these units. Always remember — *Screening comes first* (largest objects) and *Filtration comes last* (finest particles). This is the **"Big to Small"** removal logic. --- ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Kitchen Sink Model" Imagine washing a **bucket of muddy river water containing leaves, sand grains, and fine silt** in your kitchen: - **Screening** ≈ The **strainer in your sink drain** that catches leaves and twigs before they clog the pipe. - **Grit Removal** ≈ Letting the bucket **stand still for 30 seconds** — the heavy sand grains settle to the bottom quickly because they are denser, while the fine silt stays suspended. - **Sedimentation** ≈ Letting the same bucket **stand for 2 hours** — now even the fine silt settles, and you get relatively clear water on top. - **Flotation** ≈ Sprinkling **soap bubbles** in oily water — oil droplets stick to bubbles and float up like a raft. - **Filtration** ≈ Pouring the water through a **layer of sand in a cloth** — even the tiniest particles get trapped. > [!NOTE] > **Core Insight:** Every physical treatment method is just a clever engineering upgrade of one of these household intuitions. The design equations you will study in Sections 2 and 3 are the *mathematical translation* of these physical observations. --- ## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Board Favourites) - **Removal Efficiency (η):** $\eta = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e)}{C_0} \times 100 \text{ [\%]}$ - **Hydraulic Loading Rate (HLR):** $\text{HLR} = \dfrac{Q}{A} \text{ [m}^3\text{/m}^2\text{·d]}$ - **Detention Time (t_d):** $t_d = \dfrac{V}{Q} \text{ [h or min]}$ - **Weir Loading Rate (WLR):** $\text{WLR} = \dfrac{Q}{L} \text{ [m}^3\text{/m·d]}$ - **Surface Overflow Rate (SOR):** $\text{SOR} = v_s = \dfrac{Q}{A_s} \text{ [m/d]}$ Where $C_0$ = influent concentration, $C_e$ = effluent concentration, $Q$ = flow rate, $A$ = surface area, $V$ = tank volume, $L$ = weir length, $A_s$ = settling zone surface area. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Screening — The First Barrier ### 2.1.1 Types of Screens 1. **Bar Screens (Coarse):** Parallel bars, openings **> 25 mm** (Step screens: 25–100 mm). Manually or mechanically cleaned. 2. **Fine Screens:** Perforated plates or wire mesh, openings **0.25–6 mm**. 3. **Microscreens:** Openings **< 0.25 mm**, used in advanced tertiary treatment. ### 2.1.2 Design Parameters (Critical for KTU Problems) - **Bar spacing (clear opening):** 5–25 mm typical for municipal plants. - **Velocity through screen:** 0.6–1.2 m/s (to prevent grit settling in channel but allow capture). - **Headloss through clean bar screen (Kirschmer equation):** $$ h_L = \beta \cdot \left(\dfrac{w}{b}\right)^{4/3} \cdot \dfrac{v^2}{2g} \cdot \sin\theta $$ Where: - $h_L$ = headloss [m] - $\beta$ = bar shape factor (**2.42** for sharp-edged, **1.83** for rectangular, **1.79** for circular) - $w$ = maximum width of bar [m] - $b$ = clear spacing between bars [m] - $v$ = approach velocity [m/s] - $\theta$ = angle of bar with horizontal (typically **60°**) - $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favourite:** For *clogged* (partially blocked) screens, multiply clean-screen headloss by a factor of **3** to account for 50% clogging, or use the empirical **Metcalf & Eddy** clogged-screen equation. --- ## 2.2 Grit Removal — The Velocity-Controlled Channel ### 2.2.1 Purpose To remove **inorganic grit (sand, gravel, cinders)** with specific gravity ≈ **2.65** and particle size **≥ 0.2 mm** *before* it reaches the primary clarifier — protecting pumps, pipelines, and aeration tanks from abrasion. ### 2.2.2 Operational Principle Control the **horizontal velocity** of flow to **0.25–0.30 m/s** — the *settling velocity* of a 0.2 mm sand particle (≈ 0.018 m/s is too low; the design velocity of 0.25 m/s is high enough to **keep organic matter suspended** but **low enough to allow grit to settle**). ### 2.2.3 Design of Horizontal-Flow Grit Chamber - **Length of chamber (L):** $$ L = v \cdot t_d $$ - **Volume required (V):** $$ V = Q \cdot t_d $$ - **Cross-sectional area (A):** $$ A = \dfrac{V}{L} = \dfrac{Q}{v} $$ - **Width of channel (W):** $$ W = \dfrac{A}{d} $$ - **Reynolds Number check (to ensure turbulent flow regime at lower bound):** $$ R_e = \dfrac{v \cdot R_h}{\nu} $$ Where $R_h$ = hydraulic radius, $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity of water (**1.0 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s at 20 °C**). --- ## 2.3 Sedimentation — The Heart of Primary Treatment ### 2.3.1 Stokes' Law (Theoretical Foundation) A particle settling under gravity in a viscous fluid experiences a balance between **gravity force (downward)** and **drag force (upward)**: $$ F_g = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^3}{6} \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g $$ $$ F_D = 3 \pi \cdot \mu \cdot d \cdot v_s $$ At terminal settling velocity, $F_g = F_D$: $$ \boxed{\, v_s = \dfrac{g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot d^2}{18 \mu} \,} $$ This is **Stokes' Law** — valid only when **Reynolds number R_e < 1** (laminar flow around particle). > [!NOTE] > **Transition to Newton's Law:** For larger particles (R_e > 1), settling velocity is given by the **Newton's Law** (turbulent settling). KTU problems on settling *usually* default to **Stokes' regime** unless otherwise specified. ### 2.3.2 Types of Sedimentation - **Type I (Discrete Particle):** Particles do not flocculate; e.g., grit removal. Stokes' Law applies. - **Type II (Flocculent):** Particles coalesce during settling; e.g., primary clarifiers. Settling velocity *increases* with depth/time. - **Type III (Hindered/Zone):** High concentration; particles settle as a mass. - **Type IV (Compression):** Sludge thickening zone at the bottom of clarifier. ### 2.3.3 Surface Overflow Rate (SOR) — *THE* Design Equation The governing design equation for an ideal settling tank is: $$ \boxed{\, v_s \geq \text{SOR} = \dfrac{Q}{A_s} \,} $$ A particle will be removed if its settling velocity $v_s$ is **≥** the surface overflow rate. This is the **"100% removal"** criterion. ### 2.3.4 Detention Time & Weir Loading - **Detention time:** $t_d = V / Q$ (typically **1.5–2.5 h** for primary clarifiers) - **Weir loading rate:** $\text{WLR} = Q / L_w$ (typically **≤ 300 m³/m·d** for municipal plants) --- ## 2.4 Flotation — Buoyancy-Based Separation ### 2.4.1 Working Principle Air is dissolved in wastewater under **high pressure (4–6 atm)**. When released to atmospheric pressure in the flotation tank, **microbubbles (10–100 μm)** form, attach to suspended/oily particles, and **float them to the surface** as froth, which is then skimmed off. ### 2.4.2 Henry's Law (Foundational) $$ C = K_H \cdot P $$ Where $C$ = dissolved gas concentration, $K_H$ = Henry's constant, $P$ = partial pressure of gas. ### 2.4.3 Design Parameters for Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF) - **Air-to-Solids Ratio (A/S):** $$ \dfrac{A}{S} = \dfrac{1.3 \cdot s_a \cdot (f \cdot P - 1)}{S_0} $$ Where: - $s_a$ = air solubility at operating temperature (**~ 18.7 mL/L at 20 °C, 1 atm**) - $f$ = fraction of air dissolved (typically **0.5–0.8**) - $P$ = saturation pressure [atm] - $S_0$ = influent suspended solids concentration [mg/L] - **Hydraulic loading rate (HLR):** **40–200 m³/m²·d** - **Recycle ratio (R):** typically **5–25%** of influent flow --- ## 2.5 Filtration — The Final Polishing Step ### 2.5.1 Types of Filters - **Slow Sand Filters:** Rate **0.05–0.2 m/h**; biological layer (schmutzdecke) does most removal. - **Rapid Sand Filters:** Rate **5–15 m/h**; mechanical straining dominant. - **Pressure Filters:** Enclosed, used for industrial water. - **Membrane Filters:** Microfiltration (MF), Ultrafiltration (UF), Reverse Osmosis (RO). ### 2.5.2 Rapid Sand Filter Design Equations - **Filtration rate (v_f):** $$ v_f = \dfrac{Q}{A_f} \quad \text{[m}^3\text{/m}^2\text{·h or m/h]} $$ - **Headloss development (Carmen-Kozeny for clean bed):** $$ h_L = \dfrac{150 \cdot \mu \cdot v_f \cdot L \cdot (1 - \epsilon)^2}{d^2 \cdot g \cdot \rho_w \cdot \epsilon^3} $$ Where $\epsilon$ = porosity (typically **0.4** for sand), $d$ = effective grain size, $L$ = filter bed depth. - **Backwash rate (minimum fluidization velocity):** $$ v_{mf} = \dfrac{d^2 \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g}{1650 \cdot \mu} \quad \text{[m/s]} $$ --- ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master these equations — they appear in ~80% of KTU numerical questions on Module 1.** | **Equation** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Application** | |---|---|---|---| | Stokes' Settling Velocity | $v_s = g(\rho_s - \rho_w)d^2 / 18\mu$ | m/s | Discrete settling (Type I) | | Surface Overflow Rate | $\text{SOR} = Q / A_s$ | m³/m²·d = m/d | Clarifier design | | Detention Time | $t_d = V / Q$ | h or s | All tanks | | Weir Loading Rate | $\text{WLR} = Q / L_w$ | m³/m·d | Clarifier outlet | | Removal Efficiency | $\eta = (C_0 - C_e)/C_0 \times 100$ | % | All units | | Bar Screen Headloss | $h_L = \beta(w/b)^{4/3}(v^2/2g)\sin\theta$ | m | Screening design | | Grit Chamber Length | $L = v \cdot t_d$ | m | Grit chamber | | Air-to-Solids Ratio | $A/S = 1.3 \cdot s_a(fP - 1)/S_0$ | mg/mg | DAF design | | Filter Rate | $v_f = Q / A_f$ | m/h | Filter design | | Carmen-Kozeny Headloss | $h_L = 150\mu v_f L(1-\epsilon)^2 / (d^2 g \rho_w \epsilon^3)$ | m | Filter bed | | Reynolds Number | $R_e = v \cdot D_h / \nu$ | dimensionless | Flow regime check | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Stokes' Law (Settling Velocity) **Starting Principle:** A spherical particle falling through a viscous fluid accelerates until the **net force = 0** (terminal velocity). **Step 1 — Identify forces acting on a falling particle:** - Downward: **Gravitational force (weight − buoyancy)** - Upward: **Drag force** (opposing motion) - At terminal velocity: $F_{gravity} = F_{drag}$ **Step 2 — Express gravitational force (net weight in fluid):** $$ \begin{aligned} F_{gravity} &= V_{particle} \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \\ &= \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \end{aligned} $$ Where $d$ = particle diameter, $\rho_s$ = particle density, $\rho_w$ = water density, $g$ = 9.81 m/s². **Step 3 — Express drag force (Stokes' drag, valid for R_e < 1):** $$ F_{drag} = 3 \pi \mu d v_s $$ Where $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity [Pa·s or N·s/m²] and $v_s$ = settling velocity [m/s]. **Step 4 — Equate forces and solve for $v_s$:** $$ \begin{aligned} \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6} \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g &= 3 \pi \mu d v_s \\ \dfrac{d^2 \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g}{6 \cdot 3} &= \dfrac{\mu v_s}{1} \quad \text{(dividing both sides by } \pi d\text{)} \\ v_s &= \dfrac{g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot d^2}{18 \mu} \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{\, v_s = \dfrac{g(\rho_s - \rho_w) d^2}{18 \mu} \,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Validity:** $R_e = v_s d / \nu < 1$. For water at 20 °C ($\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s), this holds for particles up to about **0.1 mm diameter**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Detention Time & Volume of a Sedimentation Tank **Given:** Flow rate $Q$ [m³/s] entering a rectangular tank of length $L$, width $W$, and depth $H$. **Step 1 — Tank volume (V):** $$ V = L \times W \times H $$ **Step 2 — Definition of detention time (mean residence time):** $$ t_d = \dfrac{\text{Volume of tank}}{\text{Flow rate through tank}} = \dfrac{V}{Q} $$ **Step 3 — Solve for required volume to achieve a target $t_d$:** $$ \boxed{\, V = Q \cdot t_d \,} $$ **Step 4 — Cross-sectional surface area (A_s = L × W):** $$ A_s = \dfrac{V}{H} = \dfrac{Q \cdot t_d}{H} $$ **Step 5 — Surface Overflow Rate (SOR):** $$ \text{SOR} = \dfrac{Q}{A_s} = \dfrac{Q \cdot H}{Q \cdot t_d} = \dfrac{H}{t_d} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** SOR has units of velocity [m/s] — this *is* the **critical settling velocity**. A particle with $v_s \geq \text{SOR}$ is fully removed. The tank depth $H$ and detention time $t_d$ are *redundant variables* — only their ratio matters for ideal settling. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical) > **Problem:** A circular primary clarifier receives a wastewater flow of **5000 m³/d**. The maximum surface overflow rate allowed is **30 m³/m²·d**. Calculate: > (a) The required surface area of the tank. > (b) The detention time if the side water depth is **3.5 m**. > (c) The diameter of the clarifier (assuming circular geometry). **Given:** $Q = 5000$ m³/d, $\text{SOR} = 30$ m³/m²·d, $H = 3.5$ m. **Part (a) — Surface Area:** $$ A_s = \dfrac{Q}{\text{SOR}} = \dfrac{5000}{30} = 166.67 \text{ m}^2 $$ **Part (b) — Detention Time:** $$ t_d = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{A_s \cdot H}{Q} = \dfrac{166.67 \times 3.5}{5000} = 0.1167 \text{ d} = 2.80 \text{ h} $$ **Part (c) — Diameter of Circular Clarifier:** $$ \begin{aligned} A_s &= \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \\ D &= \sqrt{\dfrac{4 A_s}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 166.67}{3.1416}} = \sqrt{212.21} = 14.57 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answers:** $A_s = 166.67$ m², $t_d = 2.80$ h, $D \approx 14.6$ m. This 2.8 h detention time lies within the **standard KTU design range of 1.5–2.5 h for primary clarifiers** — wait, our value is slightly higher. The implication is that this clarifier is **slightly oversized**, which is acceptable for peak flow cushioning. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Unit Operation to Use? | **Scenario / Particle Type** | **Best Unit Operation** | **Reason** | |---|---|---| | Large floating debris (rags, plastics) | **Bar Screen** | Mechanical exclusion is fastest & cheapest | | Inorganic grit (sand, gravel) | **Grit Chamber** | Density-based separation; protects downstream | | Settleable organic suspended solids | **Primary Clarifier** | Gravity settling removes 50–70% SS, 25–40% BOD | | Oil, grease, low-density particles | **DAF (Flotation)** | Buoyancy-driven; air bubbles aid attachment | | Residual fine SS (< 100 μm) | **Rapid Sand Filter** | Mechanical straining + adhesion | | Dissolved salts/metals | **None — chemical/RO needed** | Physical methods cannot remove dissolved matter | --- ## 3.5 Particle Settling Regime Map (For Exam Use) | **Reynolds Number (R_e)** | **Flow Regime Around Particle** | **Applicable Law** | |---|---|---| | $R_e < 1$ | Laminar | **Stokes' Law** | | $1 \leq R_e \leq 10^3$ | Transition | **Intermediate (empirical)** | | $10^3 < R_e \leq 2 \times 10^5$ | Turbulent | **Newton's Law** | | $R_e > 2 \times 10^5$ | Post-turbulent (boundary layer transition) | **C_d ≈ 0.2 constant** | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Flow Diagram — Wastewater Physical Treatment Train The following **Mermaid flowchart** shows the **sequential treatment train** and how influent wastewater progressively loses its suspended/floating contaminants as it passes through each unit. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Influent Wastewater
Raw Sewage] --> B[Bar Screen
Removes 6 to 25 mm debris] B --> C[Grit Chamber
Removes sand and gravel 0.2 mm and above] C --> D[Primary Clarifier
Sedimentation 1.5 to 2.5 h] D --> E[DAF Unit
Flotation oil and grease] E --> F[Rapid Sand Filter
Tertiary polishing 5 to 15 m per h] F --> G[Disinfected Effluent
for reuse or discharge] D --> D1[Primary Sludge
to digester] E --> E1[Floated Scum
to sludge handling] F --> F1[Backwash Water
returned to headworks] D1 --> H[Sludge Treatment Line] E1 --> H F1 --> A ``` --- ## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of a Primary Clarifier This diagram illustrates the **internal zones** of a typical rectangular sedimentation tank — a KTU-favourite for descriptive questions. ```mermaid flowchart LR IN[Influent Inlet
at one end] --> IN1[Inlet Zone
Distribution baffle] IN1 --> SZ[Settling Zone
Major volume
Stokes settling] SZ --> SZ1[Sludge Zone
Sludge hopper at bottom] SZ1 --> SCR[Scraper Mechanism
pushes sludge to hopper] SZ --> OUT[Outlet Zone
Effluent launders and weirs] OUT --> EFF[Treated Effluent] SZ1 -.-> SL[Sludge Withdrawal
to digester] OUT -.-> WLR[Weir Loading
Rate check] ``` --- ## 4.3 Comparative Functional Diagram — DAF vs. Sedimentation ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph SED["SEDIMENTATION PATH Gravity driven"] S1[Dense Particles sink down] --> S2[Settle to bottom as sludge] end subgraph FLOT["FLOTATION PATH Buoyancy driven"] F1[Air bubbles attach to particles] --> F2[Particle bubble aggregate rises] F2 --> F3[Forms scum layer on surface] F3 --> F4[Skimmed off by surface scraper] end INF[Influent Wastewater
Mixed suspended solids] --> SED INF --> FLOT ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** The two paths are **opposite in direction** but **equivalent in principle** — both rely on a difference between the particle and water densities. Sedimentation is for $\rho_s > \rho_w$; Flotation is for *effective* $\rho_s < \rho_w$ (after bubble attachment). --- ## 4.4 Component Schematic — Rapid Sand Filter (Cross-Sectional Logic) ```mermaid flowchart TD INF[Influent
Above filter] --> DS[Distribution System
Inlet weir] DS --> FB[Filter Bed
0.5 to 0.7 m sand
0.4 to 0.6 m gravel] FB --> UN[Underdrain System
Laterals and perforations] UN --> CL[Clear Well
Treated water storage] CL --> OUT[Filtered Effluent to Disinfection] FB -.-> BW[Backwash System
Reverse flow plus air scour] BW -.-> UN FB -.-> SW[Surface Wash
Rotating arms] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Design Point:** The filter bed is **stratified** — fine sand on top (captures small particles), coarse gravel on bottom (supports the sand and distributes backwash water uniformly). --- ## 4.5 Treatment Train — Engineering Reality vs. Exam Ideal ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Preliminary Treatment
Screening plus Grit Removal] --> P2[Primary Treatment
Sedimentation plus Flotation] P2 --> P3[Secondary Treatment
Biological] P3 --> P4[Tertiary Treatment
Filtration plus Disinfection] P1 -.Mod 1.-> M1[KTU Module 1 Focus] P2 -.Mod 1.-> M1 P4 -.Mod 1.-> M1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Module 1 Boundary:** Only the **Preliminary (Screening, Grit), Primary (Sedimentation, Flotation), and Tertiary-filtration (Filtration)** units are in scope. Secondary (biological) treatment belongs to later modules. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q1. (3 Marks)** *Differentiate between Type I and Type II sedimentation. Give one example of each.* **Model Answer:** - **Type I (Discrete Settling):** Particles do not change size, shape, or density during settling. Each particle settles independently. **Example:** Sand removal in grit chambers. Governed by **Stokes' Law**. - **Type II (Flocculent Settling):** Particles coalesce/flocculate as they settle, increasing in effective size and settling velocity with depth/time. **Example:** Suspended solids removal in primary clarifiers of domestic sewage treatment. > *(Examiner Expectation: 2 distinct points + 1 example. Avoid generic statements.)* --- **Q2. (3 Marks)** *Why is the horizontal velocity in a grit chamber kept between 0.25 m/s and 0.30 m/s? What happens if it is exceeded or falls below this range?* **Model Answer:** The velocity range of **0.25–0.30 m/s** corresponds to the **settling velocity of 0.2 mm sand particles** (ρ_s = 2.65 g/cm³). This is a **discriminating velocity**: - If $v < 0.25$ m/s → **Organic matter also settles** along with grit, leading to loss of organics and grit-septic conditions (producing H₂S odours). - If $v > 0.30$ m/s → **Grit particles remain suspended** and are carried into the primary clarifier, causing abrasion of pumps, pipelines, and excess sludge. > *(Examiner Expectation: Reasoning + both limits' consequences. Most students forget the "low velocity" consequence.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / KTU Model Paper Pattern]** --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1 **(a) [7 Marks]** A municipal wastewater treatment plant treats a flow of **12,000 m³/d**. Design a **horizontal-flow grit chamber** to remove sand particles of size **0.2 mm** and above. Assume: - Specific gravity of sand = **2.65** - Dynamic viscosity of water = **1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** - Design horizontal velocity = **0.27 m/s** - Detention time = **45 seconds** Compute: (i) Settling velocity of 0.2 mm sand (Stokes' Law), (ii) Length of the chamber, (iii) Volume of the chamber, (iv) Cross-sectional area, and (v) Width if depth is 1.0 m. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **working principle of Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF)** with a neat sketch. State the role of **Henry's Law** in the saturation tank and explain the significance of the **Air-to-Solids (A/S) ratio**. --- ### Model Solution to Question A **Part (a) — Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $Q = 12{,}000 \text{ m}^3\text{/d} = 12{,}000/86{,}400 = 0.1389 \text{ m}^3\text{/s}$ Particle data: $d = 0.2 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$, $\rho_s = 2650 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(i) Settling Velocity (Stokes' Law):** $$ \begin{aligned} v_s &= \dfrac{g(\rho_s - \rho_w)d^2}{18\mu} \\ &= \dfrac{9.81 \times (2650 - 1000) \times (2 \times 10^{-4})^2}{18 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3}} \\ &= \dfrac{9.81 \times 1650 \times 4 \times 10^{-8}}{18.036 \times 10^{-3}} \\ &= \dfrac{6.4746 \times 10^{-4}}{18.036 \times 10^{-3}} \\ v_s &= 0.0359 \text{ m/s} \approx 0.036 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned} $$ **Validity Check (Reynolds number on particle):** $$ R_e = \dfrac{v_s \cdot d}{\nu} = \dfrac{0.0359 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 7.18 $$ > $R_e > 1$ — borderline; for exam purposes, Stokes' estimate is acceptable but mention that the actual settling velocity would be slightly lower due to transition regime effects. **(ii) Length of Chamber:** $$ L = v \cdot t_d = 0.27 \times 45 = 12.15 \text{ m} $$ **(iii) Volume of Chamber:** $$ V = Q \cdot t_d = 0.1389 \times 45 = 6.25 \text{ m}^3 $$ **(iv) Cross-Sectional Area:** $$ A = \dfrac{V}{L} = \dfrac{6.25}{12.15} = 0.5144 \text{ m}^2 $$ **(v) Width of Chamber (depth = 1.0 m):** $$ W = \dfrac{A}{\text{depth}} = \dfrac{0.5144}{1.0} = 0.5144 \text{ m} \approx 0.52 \text{ m} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Final Design Summary:** $L = 12.15$ m, $V = 6.25$ m³, $A = 0.5144$ m², $W = 0.52$ m. The velocity verification ($v = Q/A = 0.1389/0.5144 = 0.27$ m/s) confirms the design is internally consistent. **Part (b) — DAF Explanation:** **Working Principle:** DAF exploits **buoyancy of air-bubble-particle aggregates** to separate oil/grease and low-density suspended solids from wastewater. The process has three sub-units: 1. **Saturation Tank:** Wastewater (or recycle flow) is pressurized to **4–6 atm** in presence of compressed air. By **Henry's Law** ($C = K_H \cdot P$), more air dissolves than at atmospheric pressure. 2. **Release Tank (Flotation Cell):** Pressurized water is released through a **nozzle/needle valve** to atmospheric pressure. The excess dissolved air comes out of solution as **microbubbles (10–100 μm)**, which attach to suspended/oily particles. 3. **Skimming:** The buoyant bubble-particle aggregate rises to the surface, forming a **scum layer** that is scraped off by a surface skimmer. **Role of Henry's Law:** Determines the **maximum amount of air** that can be dissolved at a given pressure/temperature, directly controlling the **A/S ratio** (typically **0.005–0.060** mg air/mg SS). **Significance of A/S Ratio:** - If A/S is **too low** → insufficient bubbles → poor particle capture. - If A/S is **too high** → excessive turbulence → particle re-suspension. - The optimum A/S balances **complete capture** with **stable float layer**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2 **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **primary sedimentation tank**. Discuss the function of inlet and outlet arrangements and explain why the **Surface Overflow Rate (SOR)** is the key design parameter. **(b) [7 Marks]** A circular rapid sand filter has a diameter of **6 m** and a sand bed depth of **0.6 m**. It treats a flow of **150 m³/h** for **24 hours** before backwash. Calculate: - (i) Filtration rate [m/h] - (ii) Volume of water filtered per m² of filter area per day [m³/m²·d] - (iii) Headloss through the clean bed using the Carmen-Kozeny equation. Take effective grain size $d = 0.5$ mm, porosity $\epsilon = 0.4$, water density $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³, dynamic viscosity $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, and $g = 9.81$ m/s². --- ### Model Solution to Question B **Part (a) — Descriptive Solution:** A **primary sedimentation tank (clarifier)** is a quiescent basin designed to remove settleable suspended solids from wastewater by **gravity settling**. Types: **rectangular, circular, and square hopper-bottom**. **Construction (Refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2):** - **Inlet Zone:** Distributes flow uniformly across the tank cross-section. Inlet baffles prevent **jetting** and short-circuiting. - **Settling Zone:** The largest volume; quiescent conditions allow particles to settle at their terminal velocity. - **Sludge Zone:** Sludge hoppers collect settled solids at the bottom; mechanical scrapers (chain-and-flight in rectangular; rotating arm in circular) push sludge to a central hopper. - **Outlet Zone:** Effluent flows over **V-notch weirs** into effluent launders; weir loading rate must be controlled (≤ 300 m³/m·d) to prevent solids re-entrainment. **Why SOR is the key design parameter:** The **Surface Overflow Rate (SOR = Q/A_s)** has units of velocity and is the **critical settling velocity**. Any particle with $v_s \geq \text{SOR}$ will settle to the bottom before reaching the outlet. Hence **SOR alone determines the fraction of particles removed** — *not* detention time or tank depth. This is a fundamental result from the **Camp-Dobbins ideal settling tank theory**. > *(Examiner Expectation: A sketch carries 2 marks, working principle 2 marks, inlet/outlet function 2 marks, SOR explanation 1 mark.)* **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $D = 6$ m, $L = 0.6$ m (bed depth), $Q = 150$ m³/h, $t = 24$ h, $d = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\epsilon = 0.4$. **(i) Filtration Rate:** $$ A_f = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 36}{4} = 28.27 \text{ m}^2 $$ $$ v_f = \dfrac{Q}{A_f} = \dfrac{150}{28.27} = 5.305 \text{ m/h} $$ **(ii) Volume per m² per day:** $$ \text{Specific throughput} = \dfrac{Q \times t}{A_f} = \dfrac{150 \times 24}{28.27} = 127.3 \text{ m}^3\text{/m}^2\text{·d} $$ **(iii) Carmen-Kozeny Headloss:** First convert $v_f$ to m/s: $v_f = 5.305/3600 = 1.474 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s. $$ \begin{aligned} h_L &= \dfrac{150 \mu v_f L (1-\epsilon)^2}{d^2 g \rho_w \epsilon^3} \\ &= \dfrac{150 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.474 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.6 \times (0.6)^2}{(0.5 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 9.81 \times 1000 \times (0.4)^3} \\ &= \dfrac{150 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.474 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.6 \times 0.36}{0.25 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.81 \times 10^3 \times 0.064} \\ &= \dfrac{4.773 \times 10^{-5}}{0.15696} \\ h_L &= 3.04 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.304 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The clean-bed headloss of **0.30 mm** is far below the typical terminal headloss of **1.5–2.5 m** at which backwash is triggered. Hence, in this filter, the **buildup of headloss over the 24-hour run** will be dominated by **clogging of pores**, not by viscous losses through the clean sand matrix — a key design insight for KTU problems. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Physical Treatment Numerical Problems:** > > 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (3–4 marks lost):** The single biggest killer. KTU uses **SI units internally** but problems often give $Q$ in m³/d, dimensions in mm, viscosity in cP. Always convert to **m, m³/s, Pa·s, kg/m³** before plugging in. > > 2. **Forgetting Reynolds Number Check for Stokes' Law (2 marks):** Stokes' Law is *only* valid for $R_e < 1$. If you don't state this condition, the examiner **deducts 2 marks** for incomplete validity analysis. > > 3. **Mixing Up SOR and HLR (2 marks):** SOR = $Q/A_s$ (m/d) is for **clarifiers**; HLR = $Q/A$ (m/h) is for **filters**. Examiners deliberately test this confusion. > > 4. **Headless Equation Mistakes in Bar Screens (1–2 marks):** Forgetting the $\sin\theta$ term, or using **g = 9.81** instead of **g = 9.81 m/s²** with consistent units. > > 5. **DAF Calculation Errors:** The constant **1.3** in the A/S ratio accounts for the weight ratio of air to water; forgetting it loses 1 mark. Also, $f$ is the **fraction of saturation achieved**, not the saturation pressure itself. > > 6. **Skipping "Why SOR is the key parameter":** This conceptual question carries 1–2 marks and is the **most-skipped** question in KTU Module 1. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Physical Treatment Methods** ### Core Concepts - [ ] Physical treatment = **size, density, settling velocity**-based separation. **No chemistry or biology.** - [ ] Treatment sequence is **Big to Small**: Screens → Grit → Sedimentation → Flotation → Filtration. - [ ] **Stokes' Law** is the foundation of all settling-based design ($R_e < 1$). - [ ] **SOR (Q/A_s)** is the *single most important* design parameter for clarifiers — it controls particle removal *independent* of depth. - [ ] **Grit chamber velocity 0.25–0.30 m/s** is a *discriminating* velocity — not a random number. - [ ] **DAF** uses Henry's Law to dissolve air at high pressure; release at atmospheric pressure generates micro-bubbles. - [ ] **Rapid sand filters** operate at 5–15 m/h; Carmen-Kozeny governs clean-bed headloss. - [ ] **Weir loading rate** ≤ 300 m³/m·d for primary clarifiers. - [ ] **Removal efficiency** $\eta = (C_0 - C_e)/C_0 \times 100\%$ is the universal performance metric. ### Equations to Memorize (Top 5 KTU Pickers) - [ ] $v_s = g(\rho_s - \rho_w)d^2 / 18\mu$ - [ ] $\text{SOR} = Q/A_s$ - [ ] $t_d = V/Q$ - [ ] $h_L = \beta(w/b)^{4/3}(v^2/2g)\sin\theta$ - [ ] $A/S = 1.3 \cdot s_a(fP - 1)/S_0$ ### Common Numerical Pitfalls - [ ] Always convert $Q$ to m³/s before using velocity formulas. - [ ] Always convert $d$ from mm to m. - [ ] Always state validity of Stokes' Law (Reynolds check). - [ ] Always specify **assumed design values** (e.g., ρ_s = 2.65, μ = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s at 20 °C). ### Conceptual Differentiations - [ ] **Type I vs. Type II sedimentation** — discrete vs. flocculent. - [ ] **Grit vs. Sedimentation** — inorganic vs. organic removal; different design velocities. - [ ] **DAF vs. Sedimentation** — buoyancy vs. gravity; same density-difference principle, opposite direction. - [ ] **Slow vs. Rapid Sand Filter** — biological vs. physical removal dominant. > **End of Module 1 Notes — Physical Treatment Methods** > *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme — OEBBT613: Process Design for Pollution Control*

topics.title: Chemical treatment methods: coagulation, flocculation, precipitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT613 - PROCESS DESIGN FOR POLLUTION CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Chemical treatment methods: coagulation, flocculation, precipitation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Coagulation **Formal Definition:** Coagulation is the chemical process of neutralizing the surface charges of finely divided suspended colloidal particles (typically in the size range of **0.001 µm to 1 µm**) by the addition of a chemical coagulant, thereby destabilizing the colloidal suspension and allowing particles to approach one another under the influence of **van der Waals forces**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Coagulation deals with the *destabilization* of colloids. It is essentially a **rapid chemical process** (seconds to minutes) that works on the charge neutralization mechanism. The colloids in natural water carry a negative surface charge (zeta potential) which must be neutralized. **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of two strong magnets with their **North poles facing each other** — they repel and cannot touch. Coagulation is like placing a piece of iron between them; the iron neutralizes the magnetic force (analogous to the negative charge), allowing the magnets to snap together. Similarly, a coagulant (like alum) neutralizes the negative charges on colloidal particles so they can come close enough to stick. ### 1.2 Flocculation **Formal Definition:** Flocculation is the **slow, gentle physical process** of agglomerating the destabilized (coagulated) particles into larger, settleable masses called **flocs** (typically 0.1 mm to 3 mm in size) through controlled hydraulic or mechanical mixing, promoting **inter-particle collisions** and bridging. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** Coagulation = chemical destabilization. Flocculation = physical bridging. Coagulation is rapid (seconds), flocculation is slow (15–45 minutes). The gradient velocity in flocculation is **G = 20 to 75 s⁻¹** (much lower than coagulation's 700–1000 s⁻¹). **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a crowded room where everyone (destabilized particles) is now willing to mingle because their "personal space barriers" (charges) are gone. **Flocculation** is like a slow dance mixer where couples pair up gradually to form larger groups, but if the music plays too fast (high G), they bump into each other violently and break apart instead of bonding. ### 1.3 Precipitation **Formal Definition:** Chemical precipitation is the process of converting dissolved contaminants (typically heavy metal ions or hardness-causing species) into **insoluble compounds** by adding a precipitating agent, followed by settling, filtration, or flotation to remove the resulting solids. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Focus:** For Module 1, precipitation covers both: > 1. **Softening** — removing Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ hardness as CaCO₃ and Mg(OH)₂. > 2. **Heavy metal removal** — converting soluble metal ions to insoluble hydroxides, sulfides, or carbonates. **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of precipitation as making tea. When you dissolve sugar in hot water, it disappears. But if you add a "magic powder" (like lime for hardness), the dissolved "sweetness" (Ca²⁺ ions) transforms into visible chalky grains (CaCO₃) that sink to the bottom — exactly how precipitation removes dissolved pollutants. ### 1.4 Why These Three Processes Together? In a typical water/wastewater treatment train: $$\text{Coagulation} \rightarrow \text{Flocculation} \rightarrow \text{Sedimentation} \rightarrow \text{Filtration}$$ These three are often studied together because they form the **front-end workhorse of physico-chemical treatment** for removing: - **Colloidal particles** (turbidity, color, pathogens) - **Dissolved ions** (hardness, heavy metals like Pb²⁺, Cd²⁺, Cr³⁺) - **Phosphorus** (as struvite or hydroxyapatite) --- ### 1.5 Colloidal Stability — The Underlying Problem Colloids remain suspended indefinitely because of three stabilizing forces: 1. **Electrostatic repulsion** (primary — negative surface charge) 2. **Hydration shell** (water molecules bound to particle surface) 3. **Brownian motion** (keeps particles in suspension) The **zeta potential** (ζ) quantifies the surface charge. Coagulation aims to bring ζ close to **zero** (isoelectric point). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Mechanisms of Coagulation There are **four primary mechanisms** by which coagulants destabilize colloids: | Mechanism | Description | Governing Equation Concept | |---|---|---| | **Double-layer compression** | Adding electrolytes reduces the diffuse layer thickness | $\kappa^{-1} \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{I}}$ | | **Charge neutralization (adsorption)** | Coagulant species adsorb on colloid surface, neutralizing charge | Stoichiometric with surface charge | | **Sweep flocculation** | Massive hydroxide precipitate (e.g., Al(OH)₃) physically enmeshes particles | Operates at high coagulant dose | | **Inter-particle bridging** | Long-chain polymers (polyelectrolytes) link multiple particles | Polymer length > particle gap | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Students often confuse **charge neutralization** with **sweep flocculation**. Charge neutralization occurs at *low* doses (specific to colloid surface area), while sweep flocculation occurs at *high* alum doses (typically > 10–30 mg/L as Al) where Al(OH)₃ precipitate is abundant. ### 2.2 Common Coagulants | Coagulant | Chemical Formula | Best pH Range | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | **Alum** | $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 \cdot 14\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | 6.0 – 7.8 | Most common, cheap | | **Ferric chloride** | $\text{FeCl}_3$ | 4.0 – 11.0 | Wider pH range | | **Ferric sulfate** | $\text{Fe}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3$ | 4.0 – 11.0 | Effective for high turbidity | | **Lime** | $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ | 9.0 – 11.0 | Also adds hardness; used in softening | | **Poly-aluminum chloride (PAC)** | $[\text{Al}_n(\text{OH})_m\text{Cl}_{3n-m}]$ | 5.0 – 9.0 | Pre-hydrolyzed; less alkalinity consumed | **Alum Reaction in Water:** $$\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 \cdot 14\text{H}_2\text{O} + 3\text{Ca(HCO}_3)_2 \rightarrow 2\text{Al(OH)}_3 \downarrow + 3\text{CaSO}_4 + 14\text{H}_2\text{O} + 6\text{CO}_2$$ ### 2.3 Flocculation Theory **Camp-Stein Equation** (the most important flocculation formula in KTU exams): $$G = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\mu \cdot V}}$$ Where: - $G$ = mean velocity gradient (s⁻¹) - $P$ = power input (W) - $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of water (Pa·s) = $1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s at 20 °C - $V$ = flocculator volume (m³) > [!NOTE] > The product **G·T** (where T is detention time in seconds) is the **Camp number**, a measure of total flocculation opportunity. Typical design: $G \cdot T = 10^4$ to $10^5$. ### 2.4 Precipitation Equilibria (Solubility Product) For metal hydroxide precipitation: $$\text{M}^{n+} + n\text{OH}^- \rightleftharpoons \text{M(OH)}_n \downarrow$$ $$K_{sp} = [\text{M}^{n+}] \cdot [\text{OH}^-]^n$$ The **minimum solubility pH** is found by: $$[\text{OH}^-]_{opt} = \sqrt[n]{\dfrac{K_{sp}}{[\text{M}^{n+}]_0}}$$ **Minimum pH for precipitation:** $$\text{pH}_{min} = 14 - \text{pOH}_{opt} = 14 + \log_{10}([\text{OH}^-]_{opt})$$ ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Application | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $G = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\mu V}}$ | Velocity gradient in flocculator | s⁻¹ | | 2 | $G \cdot T = 10^4$ to $10^5$ | Camp number (flocculation design) | dimensionless | | 3 | $\text{Alkalinity consumed} = 0.5 \times [\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3] \times \dfrac{50}{57}$ (as CaCO₃) | Alum dose stoichiometry | mg/L as CaCO₃ | | 4 | $K_{sp} = [\text{M}^{n+}][\text{OH}^-]^n$ | Metal hydroxide precipitation | (mol/L)ⁿ⁺¹ | | 5 | $\text{pH}_{min} = 14 - \dfrac{1}{n}\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{K_{sp}}{[\text{M}^{n+}]_0}\right)$ | Minimum pH for M(OH)ₙ precipitation | dimensionless | | 6 | $\dfrac{C_0 - C}{C_0} = 1 - e^{-kT}$ | First-order removal (PFR model) | fraction | | 7 | $\text{Stokes settling: } v_s = \dfrac{g(\rho_p - \rho_w)d^2}{18\mu}$ | Floc settling velocity | m/s | | 8 | $\text{DF} = 1 + 0.75 \cdot (\alpha \cdot G \cdot \text{L}) \cdot n$ | Flocculation efficiency factor | dimensionless | | 9 | $V_c = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3$ | Floc volume (spherical assumption) | m³ | | 10 | $D_L = \dfrac{kT}{3\pi \mu d}$ | Brownian diffusion coefficient (Smoluchowski) | m²/s | > [!NOTE] > **K_TU Pitfall Avoidance:** When using Stokes' law in tables, write $\vert \rho_p - \rho_w \vert$ as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Municipal water treatment plants (WTP):** Alum coagulation removes 80–95% turbidity. - **Industrial wastewater:** Lime precipitation is used for removing heavy metals in electroplating effluent. - **Swimming pools:** PAC used for low-alkalinity impact. - **Desalination pretreatment:** Coagulation reduces membrane fouling by removing colloids that would otherwise clog RO membranes. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Camp-Stein Velocity Gradient (G) The velocity gradient $G$ is the fundamental design parameter for flocculators. Here is the full derivation: **Step 1 — Starting point: Power dissipated in a fluid.** For a paddle-type flocculator rotating in a viscous fluid, the power dissipated equals the force times velocity integrated over the paddle area. $$P = F \cdot v_{paddle}$$ **Step 2 — Newton's law of viscosity.** The shear stress $\tau$ in a fluid under velocity gradient $G$ is: $$\tau = \mu \cdot G$$ **Step 3 — Force on paddle surface.** The force $F$ is shear stress times the paddle area $A$: $$F = \tau \cdot A = \mu \cdot G \cdot A$$ **Step 4 — Velocity in terms of G.** The relative velocity between paddle and far-field fluid is: $$v_{paddle} = G \cdot L$$ where $L$ is a characteristic length (paddle dimension). **Step 5 — Substitute into power equation.** $$P = (\mu \cdot G \cdot A) \cdot (G \cdot L) = \mu \cdot G^2 \cdot A \cdot L$$ Note that $A \cdot L$ has units of m³, and combined with a flocculator geometry factor, this becomes proportional to the flocculator volume $V$: $$P \approx \mu \cdot G^2 \cdot V$$ **Step 6 — Solve for G.** $$G^2 = \dfrac{P}{\mu V}$$ $$\boxed{G = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\mu V}}}$$ > This is the **Camp-Stein equation**, foundational for KTU flocculator design problems. --- ### 3.2 Numerical Problem: Jar Test Scale-Up to Flocculator Design **Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Typical Pattern):** A jar test determined the optimum coagulant dose is **30 mg/L of alum** with a velocity gradient **G = 50 s⁻¹** and detention time **T = 20 min**. Design a flocculator for a treatment plant with flow **Q = 50,000 m³/day**. Take μ = $1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. **Given:** - $Q = 50{,}000$ m³/day - $G = 50$ s⁻¹ - $T = 20$ min = 1200 s - $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s **Step 1 — Calculate flocculator volume V.** The volume is the flow rate times detention time. $$V = Q \times T = \dfrac{50{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}}{86400 \text{ s/day}} \times 1200 \text{ s}$$ $$V = 0.5787 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \times 1200 \text{ s} = 694.44 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 2 — Calculate power input P.** $$G = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\mu V}} \implies P = G^2 \cdot \mu \cdot V$$ $$P = (50)^2 \times (1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times 694.44$$ $$P = 2500 \times 10^{-3} \times 694.44$$ $$P = 1736.1 \text{ W} \approx 1.74 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3 — Daily alum consumption.** $$\text{Alum dose} = 30 \text{ mg/L} = 30 \text{ g/m}^3$$ $$\text{Daily mass} = 30 \text{ g/m}^3 \times 50{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ $$\text{Daily mass} = 1{,}500{,}000 \text{ g/day} = 1500 \text{ kg/day} = 1.5 \text{ tonnes/day}$$ **Step 4 — Verify Camp number.** $$G \cdot T = 50 \times 1200 = 60{,}000 = 6 \times 10^4$$ This lies in the recommended range of $10^4$ to $10^5$ ✓. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Minimum pH for Heavy Metal Precipitation **Problem:** Find the minimum pH at which $\text{Fe}^{3+}$ will precipitate from a solution containing $[\text{Fe}^{3+}]_0 = 10$ mg/L. Given $K_{sp}(\text{Fe(OH)}_3) = 2.79 \times 10^{-39}$. **Step 1 — Convert concentration to mol/L.** $$[\text{Fe}^{3+}]_0 = \dfrac{10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/L}}{56 \text{ g/mol}} = 1.786 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/L}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Ksp expression.** $$K_{sp} = [\text{Fe}^{3+}][\text{OH}^-]^3$$ At minimum solubility, assume essentially all Fe³⁺ must be removed, so $[\text{Fe}^{3+}] \approx 10^{-5}$ mol/L (residual) is the design target. But for "minimum pH to begin precipitation," we use the initial concentration: $$[\text{OH}^-]^3 = \dfrac{K_{sp}}{[\text{Fe}^{3+}]_0} = \dfrac{2.79 \times 10^{-39}}{1.786 \times 10^{-4}}$$ $$[\text{OH}^-]^3 = 1.562 \times 10^{-35}$$ $$[\text{OH}^-] = (1.562 \times 10^{-35})^{1/3} = 2.5 \times 10^{-12} \text{ mol/L}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate pH.** $$\text{pOH} = -\log_{10}(2.5 \times 10^{-12}) = 11.6$$ $$\text{pH} = 14 - 11.6 = 2.4$$ > **Conclusion:** Fe³⁺ begins to precipitate above **pH 2.4** and is virtually completely removed by pH 4–5. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Parameter | Coagulation | Flocculation | Precipitation | |---|---|---|---| | **Time** | Seconds | 15–45 min | Minutes to hours | | **Mixing G** | 700–1000 s⁻¹ | 20–75 s⁻¹ | Low (depending on settling) | | **Chemical added** | Coagulant (Alum, FeCl₃) | Sometimes polymer (flocculant aid) | Lime, NaOH, Na₂S | | **Target** | Colloidal particles (0.001–1 µm) | Destabilized colloids | Dissolved ions | | **Mechanism** | Charge neutralization, double-layer compression | Particle collisions, bridging | Insoluble compound formation | | **Output** | Microflocs | Macro-flocs visible to eye | Sludge (chemical precipitate) | | **Energy source** | High mixing energy | Low mixing energy | Chemical potential | | **KTU typical question** | "Explain double-layer compression" | "Design flocculator using Camp-Stein" | "Find pH for metal removal" | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Coagulation-Flocculation-Sedimentation Train ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Water
Influent] --> B[Rapid Mix Tank
G equals 700 to 1000 per s
T equals 30 to 60 s] B --> C[Coagulant Dosing
Alum or FeCl3
5 to 50 mg per L] C --> D[Flocculation Basin
G equals 20 to 75 per s
T equals 20 to 45 min] D --> E[Sedimentation Tank
Surface overflow 1 to 2 m per hr] E --> F[Filtered Water
to Disinfection] E --> G[Sludge Withdrawal
to Thickener] D --> H[Polymer Flocculant Aid
Optional 0.1 to 1 mg per L] H --> D ``` > **Reading Guide:** A → B → C → D → H (feedback polymer) → E → F (clean water) and G (waste). ### 4.2 Jar Test Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD J0[Collect Raw Water Sample] --> J1[Prepare 6 Beakers
each 1 L] J1 --> J2[Add Coagulant
Doses 0, 5, 10, 20, 30, 50 mg per L] J2 --> J3[Rapid Mix at 100 rpm
for 1 min] J3 --> J4[Slow Mix at 30 rpm
for 20 min] J4 --> J5[Settle for 30 min] J5 --> J6[Measure Supernatant
Turbidity and pH] J6 --> J7{Floc Formation
Satisfactory?} J7 -- Yes --> J8[Optimum Dose Found
Proceed to Plant Design] J7 -- No --> J9[Repeat with
Different pH or Coagulant] J9 --> J1 ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Precipitation Process Train ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Metal-bearing
Wastewater] --> P2[Equalization Tank
pH and Flow Stabilization] P2 --> P3[Rapid Mix Basin
Chemical Dosing] P3 --> P4[Reactor Tank
Retention 5 to 30 min] P4 --> P5[Lamella Clarifier
or Sedimentation] P5 --> P6[Treated Effluent
to Filtration] P5 --> P7[Chemical Sludge
to Filter Press] P3 --> P8[Chemical Feeders
NaOH or Lime or Na2S] P8 --> P3 ``` ### 4.4 Hierarchy of Chemical Treatment Methods ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Chemical Treatment
Methods] --> B[Coagulation] A --> C[Flocculation] A --> D[Precipitation] A --> E[Adsorption] A --> F[Oxidation Reduction] A --> G[Ion Exchange] A --> H[Neutralization] B --> B1[Charge Neutralization] B --> B2[Sweep Flocculation] B --> B3[Double Layer Compression] B --> B4[Bridging] C --> C1[Perikinetic Brownian] C --> C2[Orthokinetic Hydraulic] C --> C3[Polymer Assisted] D --> D1[Hydroxide Precipitation] D --> D2[Sulfide Precipitation] D --> D3[Carbonate Precipitation] D --> D4[Cementation] ``` ### 4.5 Charge Neutralization Mechanism (Conceptual Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR Q1[Colloid Particle
Negative Surface Charge
zeta negative] --> Q2[Coagulant Added
Al3 plus or Fe3 plus] Q2 --> Q3[Adsorption of Cations
on Particle Surface] Q3 --> Q4[Charge Neutralized
zeta approx zero] Q4 --> Q5[van der Waals Attraction
Dominant] Q5 --> Q6[Particles Aggregate
Microflocs Form] Q6 --> R1[Flocculation Stage
Build Macro Flocs] ``` > **Visualization Note:** Each block represents a process unit. The arrows show the **mass flow direction** and the sequential dependency in a typical KTU design problem. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** **Define coagulation. List any two common coagulants used in water treatment with their optimum pH ranges.** **Model Answer:** Coagulation is the chemical process of destabilizing colloidal particles (0.001–1 µm) by neutralizing their surface (negative) charge using a chemical coagulant, so that particles can agglomerate under van der Waals forces. Two common coagulants: 1. **Alum** $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 \cdot 14\text{H}_2\text{O}$ — optimum pH range **6.0 to 7.8** 2. **Ferric chloride** $\text{FeCl}_3$ — optimum pH range **4.0 to 11.0** *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 each for two coagulants with pH)* --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **Differentiate between coagulation and flocculation.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Coagulation | Flocculation | |---|---|---| | Mechanism | Chemical (charge neutralization) | Physical (particle collisions) | | Duration | Seconds to 1–2 min | 15–45 min | | Velocity gradient G | 700–1000 s⁻¹ | 20–75 s⁻¹ | | Chemical added | Primary coagulant (alum, FeCl₃) | Sometimes polymer (flocculant aid) | | Result | Destabilized microflocs | Visible settleable macro-flocs | | Equipment | Rapid mix unit | Slow mix paddle flocculator | *(3 marks: 2 for differences, 1 for concluding statement)* --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]** **(a)** Explain the **four mechanisms** of coagulation with neat sketches. Discuss how zeta potential is affected in each case. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A water treatment plant treats **40,000 m³/day**. The jar test optimum alum dose is **25 mg/L**. Calculate: (i) Daily alum requirement in kg. (ii) The alkalinity consumed per day (mg/L as CaCO₃ basis). (iii) The lime required to replenish the alkalinity. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a):** **1. Double-layer compression:** Addition of indifferent electrolytes (e.g., NaCl, CaCl₂) increases ionic strength, compressing the diffuse layer around the colloid. This reduces the inter-particle repulsion range. The Debye length $\kappa^{-1}$ is: $$\kappa^{-1} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 kT}{2 N_A e^2 I}}$$ where $I$ is ionic strength. Zeta potential magnitude remains similar but the *effective range* of repulsion shrinks. **2. Adsorption and charge neutralization:** Coagulant cations (Al³⁺, Fe³⁺) adsorb specifically on the colloid surface, neutralizing the negative charge. Zeta potential is reduced toward **zero (isoelectric point)**. This is stoichiometric — dose depends on the colloid's surface charge density. **3. Sweep flocculation:** At high coagulant doses (>10–30 mg/L as Al), a voluminous hydroxide precipitate (Al(OH)₃ or Fe(OH)₃) forms that physically enmeshes the colloidal particles. Zeta potential may even reverse sign due to excess adsorbed cations. **4. Inter-particle bridging:** High-molecular-weight polymer chains (e.g., polyacrylamide) attach simultaneously to multiple particles, forming "bridges" between them. The polymer must be long enough to span the inter-particle gap. *(7 marks: 1.5 per mechanism, 1 for sketches description, 0.5 for zeta correlation)* --- **Part (b):** **Given:** - $Q = 40{,}000$ m³/day - Alum dose = 25 mg/L - Alum formula weight = 594 g/mol (as $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3 \cdot 14\text{H}_2\text{O}$) **(i) Daily alum requirement:** $$\text{Alum mass per day} = 25 \text{ g/m}^3 \times 40{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ g/day}$$ $$\boxed{= 1000 \text{ kg/day} = 1 \text{ tonne/day}}$$ **(ii) Alkalinity consumed:** Stoichiometry: 1 mole of alum consumes 3 moles of $\text{Ca(HCO}_3)_2$ which equals **6 moles of CaCO₃ alkalinity**. $$\text{Alum moles/day} = \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000 \text{ g}}{594 \text{ g/mol}} = 1683.5 \text{ mol/day}$$ $$\text{Alkalinity consumed as CaCO}_3 = 6 \times 1683.5 = 10{,}101 \text{ mol/day}$$ Converting to mg/L as CaCO₃ (molecular weight 100): $$= 10{,}101 \times 100 \text{ g/day} = 1{,}010{,}100 \text{ g/day}$$ Per liter of water treated: $$\dfrac{1{,}010{,}100 \text{ g}}{40{,}000 \text{ m}^3 \times 1000 \text{ L/m}^3} = \dfrac{1{,}010{,}100}{4 \times 10^7} = 0.02525 \text{ g/L} = 25.25 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$ > **Rule of thumb:** 1 mg/L of alum consumes approximately **0.5 mg/L of alkalinity as CaCO₃** ✓ **(iii) Lime required:** Commercial lime is ~90% pure CaO. Lime equivalent: $$1 \text{ mg/L CaCO}_3 = \dfrac{56}{100} = 0.56 \text{ mg/L of pure CaO}$$ $$\text{Lime needed} = 25.25 \times 0.56 = 14.14 \text{ mg/L as pure CaO}$$ $$\text{Commercial lime (90\% purity)} = \dfrac{14.14}{0.90} = 15.71 \text{ mg/L}$$ $$\text{Daily lime} = 15.71 \times 40{,}000 \times 10^{-3} = 628.4 \text{ kg/day}$$ $$\boxed{\approx 628 \text{ kg/day of commercial lime}}$$ *(7 marks: 2 each for (i) and (ii), 3 for (iii))* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]** **(a)** With a **neat sketch**, describe the construction and working of a **paddle-type flocculator**. Derive the **Camp-Stein equation** for mean velocity gradient. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A flocculation basin has volume **V = 600 m³** treating **Q = 0.4 m³/s** of water. The power input is **P = 1500 W** and water viscosity is $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. Calculate: (i) Mean velocity gradient $G$. (ii) Detention time $T$. (iii) Camp number $G \cdot T$ and comment on flocculation efficiency. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a):** **Construction:** A paddle-type flocculator is a reinforced concrete basin fitted with **vertical or horizontal paddle wheels** mounted on a horizontal shaft driven by an electric motor through a reduction gear. The paddles rotate at low speeds (typically 0.5 to 3 rpm). The basin is divided into 3 or 4 compartments, often with tapered velocity gradients from inlet (high G) to outlet (low G) for optimum floc growth. **Working:** Water enters the first compartment and flows through successive chambers. The slow rotation of paddles creates a **gentle velocity gradient** in the water, causing destabilized (coagulated) micro-particles to collide and agglomerate into larger flocs. The flocs grow progressively larger and settle in the subsequent sedimentation tank. **Derivation of Camp-Stein equation:** *(See SECTION 3.1 above for full derivation)* Final result: $$G = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\mu V}}$$ *(7 marks: 2 for sketch description, 2 for working, 3 for derivation)* --- **Part (b):** **Given:** - $V = 600$ m³ - $Q = 0.4$ m³/s - $P = 1500$ W - $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s **(i) Velocity gradient G:** $$G = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\mu V}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1500}{1.002 \times 10^{-3} \times 600}}$$ $$G = \sqrt{\dfrac{1500}{0.6012}} = \sqrt{2495.0} \approx 49.95 \text{ s}^{-1}$$ $$\boxed{G \approx 50 \text{ s}^{-1}}$$ **(ii) Detention time T:** $$T = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{600 \text{ m}^3}{0.4 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}} = 1500 \text{ s} = 25 \text{ min}$$ **(iii) Camp number:** $$G \cdot T = 50 \times 1500 = 75{,}000 = 7.5 \times 10^4$$ **Comment:** This falls within the recommended design range of $10^4$ to $10^5$, indicating **adequate flocculation efficiency** ✓. *(7 marks: 2 for G, 2 for T, 3 for G·T and comment)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit mismatch:** Students often forget to convert $\mu$ from **poise** to **Pa·s** ($1$ poise $= 0.1$ Pa·s). > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 6 in alkalinity:** Alum consumes 3 moles of bicarbonate, equivalent to 6 moles of CaCO₃. Many students incorrectly write 3. > 3. **Mixing G and T products:** G is in s⁻¹, T in seconds. The product G·T is **dimensionless** — never attach units. > 4. **Confusing coagulation G (700–1000) with flocculation G (20–75):** This is a 14-mark question killer. > 5. **Ksp problems:** Forgetting the **n-th root** for n-valent hydroxides (e.g., cube root for Fe(OH)₃). > 6. **Commercial lime purity:** Always state purity assumption; otherwise the examiner deducts 0.5 mark. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Yield Points for KTU 2024** - ✅ Coagulation is **chemical**; flocculation is **physical**. - ✅ Coagulation time = **seconds**; flocculation time = **15–45 min**. - ✅ Coagulation G = **700–1000 s⁻¹**; flocculation G = **20–75 s⁻¹**. - ✅ Camp-Stein equation: $G = \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\mu V}}$ — **memorize verbatim**. - ✅ Camp number $G \cdot T = 10^4$ to $10^5$. - ✅ Alum optimum pH = **6.0–7.8**; FeCl₃ optimum pH = **4.0–11.0**. - ✅ Alkalinity consumed ≈ **0.5 mg/L as CaCO₃ per 1 mg/L alum**. - ✅ Four coagulation mechanisms: **double-layer compression, charge neutralization, sweep flocculation, bridging**. - ✅ Hydroxide precipitation $K_{sp} = [\text{M}^{n+}][\text{OH}^-]^n$ — use **n-th root** for n-valent metals. - ✅ Zeta potential must approach **zero** for charge neutralization. - ✅ Stokes' law $v_s = \dfrac{g \cdot \Delta\rho \cdot d^2}{18\mu}$ applies for floc settling. - ✅ Jar test procedure: rapid mix → slow mix → settle → measure turbidity. - ✅ Heavy metal removal order (pH-dependent): Fe³⁺ (~pH 4) < Al³⁺ (~pH 6) < Zn²⁺ (~pH 8) < Ni²⁺ (~pH 9). - ✅ **Sweep flocculation** occurs at high dose (Al(OH)₃ precipitate dominant). - ✅ Lime precipitation is used in **water softening** for Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ removal. - ✅ Remember the alum–bicarbonate stoichiometry: **1 mole alum ≡ 6 moles CaCO₃**. - ✅ For Module 1 KTU boards, focus on **definition, mechanism, design, and numerical problem-solving** — these four pillars.

topics.title: Biological treatment methods: activated sludge, biofilm reactors, anaerobic digestion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT613 - PROCESS DESIGN FOR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Biological treatment methods: activated sludge, biofilm reactors, anaerobic digestion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Biological Wastewater Treatment?

Biological wastewater treatment is an engineered, microbiologically mediated process that exploits the **metabolic activity of microorganisms** (bacteria, archaea, fungi, protozoa) to **convert dissolved and suspended organic pollutants** (measured as **BOD** – Biochemical Oxygen Demand, and **COD** – Chemical Oxygen Demand) into **harmless end products** such as carbon dioxide, water, methane, and excess microbial biomass (sludge).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim-style):**
> "Biological treatment methods utilise the catabolic and anabolic pathways of microbial communities to stabilise organic matter. Three principal configurations are studied under this module: (1) **Suspended-growth systems** – the activated sludge process, (2) **Attached-growth systems** – biofilm reactors (Trickling Filters, Rotating Biological Contactors, Biological Aerated Filters), and (3) **Anaerobic systems** – anaerobic digestion."

---

## 1.2 The Three Biological Treatment Families — Intuitive Analogies

### 🔹 1.2.1 Activated Sludge Process (Suspended-Growth)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A **suspended-growth, aerobic, continuous-flow biological treatment** process in which a flocculated mass of microorganisms (the *activated sludge*) is kept in suspension and uniformly mixed with wastewater via diffused or mechanical aeration. The mixed liquor is then separated in a **secondary clarifier**, with a portion of the settled biomass recycled (Return Activated Sludge, RAS) and the remainder wasted (Waste Activated Sludge, WAS).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine a **busy cafeteria kitchen**. A huge pot of soup (the aeration tank) is constantly bubbling (aeration). Tiny chefs (bacteria) are floating freely in the soup, eating the food particles (organic waste) that fall in. After a while, the chefs clump together (flocculation) and sink to the bottom of the secondary pan (clarifier). Most chefs are scooped back into the soup pot to keep cooking (RAS), and a small daily portion is removed as garbage (WAS). The clean soup overflows (effluent).

**Key Design Metrics (must memorise):**
- **MLSS** – Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids (mg/L): typically **2000–4000 mg/L** for conventional activated sludge.
- **MLVSS** – Mixed Liquor Volatile Suspended Solids (mg/L): the *active* fraction, ~70–80% of MLSS.
- **F/M Ratio** – Food to Microorganism ratio (day⁻¹): typically **0.2–0.5 d⁻¹** (conventional).
- **SRT / MCRT (θc)** – Sludge (Mean Cell) Residence Time: typically **5–15 days**.
- **HRT (θ)** – Hydraulic Retention Time: typically **4–8 hours**.
- **SV₃₀ & SVI** – Sludge Volume Index (mL/g): typically **50–150 mL/g** for good settling.

---

### 🔹 1.2.2 Biofilm Reactors (Attached-Growth)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** Biofilm reactors employ an **inert support medium** (rock, plastic, ceramic, wood) on which a **sessile microbial layer (biofilm)** develops. Wastewater flows past the medium; substrates diffuse into the biofilm where they are biodegraded. Oxygen (aerobic systems) or absence thereof (anaerobic systems like UASB) governs the metabolism. Common configurations: **Trickling Filters (TF), Rotating Biological Contactors (RBC), Biological Aerated Filters (BAF), Moving Bed Biofilm Reactors (MBBR), Fluidised Bed Reactors (FBR)**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Picture a **rocky stream in a forest**. Algae and microbes form a slimy green-brown coating (biofilm) on every stone. As the stream (wastewater) flows over the rocks, the biofilm continuously "licks up" dissolved nutrients from the water. The rocks never move; they are the permanent home of the workers. This is the opposite of the activated sludge model where workers float freely.

**Why Biofilm Reactors Matter:**
- Higher biomass concentration per unit volume (no clarifier washout risk).
- Robust against toxic/inhibitory shocks.
- Lower sludge production.
- Used for **nitrification**, **secondary treatment**, and **tertiary polishing**.

---

### 🔹 1.2.3 Anaerobic Digestion

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** Anaerobic digestion (AD) is a **multi-stage biochemical process** carried out in the **complete absence of molecular oxygen**, in which complex organic polymers are progressively hydrolysed, acidified, and finally converted by **methanogenic archaea** into a biogas mixture (≈ **60–70% CH₄, 30–40% CO₂**, plus trace H₂S, NH₃) and a stabilised biosolid (digestate).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a **sealed compost pit or biogas plant on a farm**. No air enters. Inside, a chain of microbes works in shifts: first, "chewer" bacteria break down cow dung and food scraps into sugars and acids; then, "stink-producing" bacteria turn those into acetic acid and hydrogen; finally, the "ancient archaea" (literally *ancient* – they evolved before oxygen existed on Earth) combine these to make **methane**, which bubbles out and can be burnt in a stove to cook food.

**The Four Canonical AD Stages:**
1. **Hydrolysis** – large polymers (proteins, polysaccharides, lipids) → monomers (amino acids, sugars, fatty acids). *Rate-limiting for particulates.*
2. **Acidogenesis** – monomers → volatile fatty acids (VFAs) + alcohols + H₂ + CO₂.
3. **Acetogenesis** – VFAs + alcohols → acetic acid + H₂ + CO₂.
4. **Methanogenesis** – acetate + H₂ + CO₂ → CH₄ + CO₂.

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:** Methanogens are **extremely slow-growing** (doubling time ≈ 3–30 days) and highly sensitive to pH, temperature, ammonia, and VFA accumulation. This forces AD reactors to operate at **long SRT (15–40 days)** and moderate temperature (**mesophilic: 30–40 °C, optimal 35 °C**; or **thermophilic: 50–55 °C**).

---

## 1.3 Comparative Snapshot

| Feature | Activated Sludge | Biofilm Reactor | Anaerobic Digestion |
|---|---|---|---|
| Oxygen requirement | **Aerobic** | Mostly **aerobic** | **Strictly anaerobic** |
| Biomass state | Suspended flocs | Attached film | Suspended/granular |
| SRT/HRT | SRT ≫ HRT | HRT dominant | SRT ≫ HRT |
| Energy input | High (aeration) | Low–Moderate | Low (energy *output*) |
| End product | CO₂ + sludge | CO₂ + biofilm | **Biogas (CH₄)** + digestate |
| Footprint | Large | Compact | Compact |
| Typical BOD removal | 85–95% | 60–90% | 60–80% (followed by polishing) |
