# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Activated Sludge — Process Architecture & Operating Theory

The **conventional activated sludge process (CASP)** consists of the following unit operations in series:

```
Influent → Screening → Grit Removal → Primary Clarifier
        → Aeration Tank (with RAS) → Secondary Clarifier
        → Disinfection → Effluent
        → WAS → Sludge Thickening → Sludge Handling
```

### 2.1.1 Microbiology and Floc Formation

The biomass in the aeration tank is a **mixed-culture, predominantly bacterial**, consortium dominated by:
- **Heterotrophic floc-formers** – *Zoogloea ramigera*, *Flavobacterium*, *Pseudomonas* (remove carbonaceous BOD).
- **Nitrifiers** – *Nitrosomonas* (NH₄⁺ → NO₂⁻), *Nitrobacter* (NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻) — grow slowly.
- **Protozoa & Rotifers** – graze on dispersed bacteria and clarify the effluent.

These organisms embed themselves in **extracellular polymeric substances (EPS)** — a sticky matrix of polysaccharides, proteins, and DNA — to form **flocs** (50–500 µm). Flocculation is critical because dispersed (pin-point) flocs escape the clarifier and degrade effluent quality.

### 2.1.2 Mass Balance Framework (Steady-State CSTR)

For a single completely-mixed aeration tank, the **substrate mass balance** around the reactor gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Accumulation} &= \text{In} - \text{Out} - \text{Consumed by biomass} \\
0 &= Q S_0 - Q S_e - r_{su} V \\
r_{su} V &= Q(S_0 - S_e)
\end{aligned}
$$

The **biomass mass balance** around the entire system (reactor + clarifier with recycle/waste) gives the classical design equation for the **Mean Cell Residence Time (MCRT, θc)**:

$$
\theta_c = \frac{V X}{Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e}
$$

Where:
- $V$ = aeration tank volume (m³)
- $X$ = MLVSS concentration (mg/L)
- $Q_w$ = waste sludge flow rate (m³/d)
- $X_w$ = wasted sludge concentration (mg/L)
- $Q_e$ = effluent flow rate (m³/d)
- $X_e$ = effluent suspended solids concentration (mg/L)

### 2.1.3 The F/M Ratio and Its Operational Significance

$$
F/M = \frac{Q S_0}{V X} = \frac{S_0}{\theta X}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operational Rule of Thumb (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - **High F/M (>0.5 d⁻¹):** "Rate of feeding is high relative to biomass" → poor BOD removal, dispersed flocs, *poor settling*. (e.g., Modified Aeration)
> - **Conventional F/M (0.2–0.5 d⁻¹):** Balanced operation, excellent BOD removal.
> - **Low F/M (0.05–0.15 d⁻¹):** Extended aeration, very low sludge yield, near-complete mineralisation.

### 2.1.4 Sludge Quality Indicators

$$
\text{SVI} = \frac{\text{Settled Sludge Volume (mL/L)}}{\text{MLSS (g/L)}} \times 1000 \;\; (\text{mL/g})
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Trap:** Some students confuse **SVI** with **SV₃₀** (settled volume after 30 minutes). SV₃₀ has units mL/L; SVI normalises it to mL/g. Always divide by MLSS in g/L.

---

## 2.2 Biofilm Reactors — Theory of Operation

### 2.2.1 The Biofilm Concept

A **biofilm** is a structured, gel-like microbial community embedded in EPS, irreversibly attached to a substratum. Substrate transport is governed by **molecular diffusion** through the biofilm matrix.

The **steady-state biofilm thickness, Lf**, is determined by the dynamic equilibrium between:
- **Growth** (substrate flux into biofilm).
- **Detachment** (shear loss, sloughing, endogenous decay).

### 2.2.2 Fickian Diffusion into Biofilm

Within a uniform, planar biofilm, the substrate flux is governed by **Fick's First Law**:

$$
J = -D_f \frac{dS}{dz}
$$

Where $D_f$ is the **effective diffusion coefficient in the biofilm** (cm²/s), typically 50–80% of the value in pure water due to the EPS matrix.

Solving the steady-state mass balance inside the biofilm gives the **flux into a deep biofilm**:

$$
J = \eta \cdot k \cdot S_s^{n}
$$

Where $\eta$ is the **effectiveness factor** (= actual uptake / uptake if entire biofilm at bulk concentration $S_s$).

### 2.2.3 Trickling Filter Design — NRC & Eckenfelder Equations

The **Eckenfelder equation** (most commonly tested at KTU):

$$
\frac{S_e}{S_0} = \exp\!\left[-K \cdot \frac{A^{0.5}}{Q^{n}} \cdot S_0^{m}\right]
$$

For a first-order approximation (rotating distributor, plastic media):

$$
\frac{S_e}{S_0} = e^{-k D / Q^{n}}
$$

Where:
- $S_0, S_e$ = influent and effluent BOD (mg/L)
- $D$ = depth of filter (m)
- $Q$ = hydraulic loading (m³/m²·d)
- $k, n$ = empirical constants (media and wastewater specific)

### 2.2.4 Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC)

The **hydraulic loading on an RBC** is:

$$
\Lambda = \frac{Q}{A_{\text{disk}}}
$$

And the **standard areal loading** for domestic wastewater secondary treatment: **5–15 g BOD/m²·d**.

---

## 2.3 Anaerobic Digestion — Stoichiometry & Kinetics

### 2.3.1 Theoretical Biogas Yield (Buswell's Equation)

For a generic organic compound $C_a H_b O_c N_d S_e$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_a H_b O_c N_d S_e &+ \left(a - \frac{c}{2} - \frac{3d}{4} - \frac{e}{2}\right)H_2O \\
&\longrightarrow \left(\frac{a}{2} - \frac{c}{8} + \frac{d}{4} + \frac{e}{4}\right)CH_4 \\
&\quad + \left(\frac{a}{2} + \frac{c}{8} - \frac{3d}{8} - \frac{e}{4}\right)CO_2 \\
&\quad + d\,NH_3 + e\,H_2S
\end{aligned}
$$

For **glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆)**, Buswell reduces to:

$$
C_6 H_{12} O_6 \longrightarrow 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2
$$

Thus, **theoretical methane yield = 0.37 m³ CH₄ per kg glucose** at STP.

### 2.3.2 Methane Yield from COD Removal

A more practical engineering equation:

$$
V_{CH_4} = 0.35 \, Q \, (S_0 - S_e) \;\; \text{(m}^3\text{ CH}_4\text{/d at STP)}
$$

Where $(S_0 - S_e)$ is COD removed (kg COD/d) and 0.35 m³ CH₄/kg COD is the standard theoretical yield (rises to ~0.40 for purely aceticlastic methanogenesis).

### 2.3.3 Process Kinetics (Chen-Hashimoto Model)

$$
\frac{1}{\theta_c} = \frac{\mu_m}{1 + K_S / S_e + \theta_c^{max} / \theta_c}
$$

Or the simpler **first-order kinetic model** for AD design:

$$
\theta_c^{min} = \theta_c^0 + \frac{K}{S_0 - S_e}
$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the canonical formula sheet you should be able to reproduce verbatim in the ESE.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F/M = Q S_0 / (V X)$ | Food/Microorganism ratio — operational classification |
| 2 | $\theta = V/Q$ | Hydraulic retention time |
| 3 | $\theta_c = V X / (Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e)$ | Mean cell residence time — sludge age |
| 4 | $SVI = SV_{30}/MLSS \cdot 1000$ | Sludge settleability index (mL/g) |
| 5 | $\theta_c = 1/(\mu - k_d)$ | Minimum SRT for substrate utilisation |
| 6 | $U = (S_0 - S_e)/(\theta X)$ | Specific substrate utilisation rate (d⁻¹) |
| 7 | $\mu = Y U - k_d$ | Specific growth rate (d⁻¹) |
| 8 | $1/S_e = K_S/(k \cdot X) + 1/(k X)$ | Linearised Monod (Lineweaver–Burk) |
| 9 | $Y_{obs} = Y/(1 + k_d \theta_c)$ | Observed yield (kg biomass/kg substrate) |
| 10 | $O_2 = 1.42\,Q\,S_0\,\eta\,Y_{obs} + 4.57\,Q\,(S_{0,N} - S_{e,N})$ | Oxygen demand for C + N removal (kg O₂/d) |
| 11 | $J = -D_f\, dS/dz$ | Fick's law in biofilm |
| 12 | $S_e/S_0 = e^{-kD/Q^n}$ | Eckenfelder trickling filter |
| 13 | $V_{CH_4} = 0.35\, Q\,(S_0 - S_e)$ | Methane production rate (m³/d at STP) |
| 14 | $\% CH_4 = 100 - (9\% H_2S + 2\% NH_3)$ | Biogas methane content estimate |
| 15 | $F_{VS\;destroyed} = F_{VS\;in} \cdot (\eta_{VS}/100)$ | Volatile solids reduction in digester |
| 16 | $\Lambda = Q/A_{disk}$ | RBC hydraulic / areal loading |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Municipal Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs)** in Kerala (e.g., Cochin, Thiruvananthapuram) predominantly use **conventional activated sludge with biological nutrient removal (BNR)** modifications.
- **Biofilm-based BAF/MBBR** units are widely adopted as compact upgrades for hotels, hospitals, and apartment complexes.
- **Anaerobic digesters** at sewage treatment plants recover **biogas for boiler/heater duty**, making STPs partially energy self-sufficient — a key driver under India's **SATAT (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation)** scheme.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of F/M Ratio and Its Kinematics

**Problem context:** An aeration tank receives $Q = 10{,}000$ m³/d of municipal wastewater with $S_0 = 250$ mg/L BOD. The tank volume is $V = 2{,}000$ m³ and MLVSS is $X = 2{,}500$ mg/L.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{2000}{10000} = 0.2 \text{ d} \\
\theta &= 0.2 \times 24 = 4.8 \text{ h} \quad \text{(hydraulic retention time)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
F/M = \frac{Q\,S_0}{V\,X} = \frac{10000 \times 250}{2000 \times 2500} = 1.0 \text{ d}^{-1}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** $F/M = 1.0$ d⁻¹ is *too high* for conventional activated sludge (acceptable range 0.2–0.5). It indicates **overloaded aeration tank** or **insufficient biomass**. Operational remedy: increase MLSS via higher RAS, or enlarge $V$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Mean Cell Residence Time Equation

Consider the **biomass mass balance** around the entire activated sludge system (reactor + clarifier):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Accumulation} &= \text{Inflow (from influent)} - \text{Outflow (WAS + effluent)} + \text{Net Growth} \\
\frac{dX}{dt}\,V &= Q\,X_0 - (Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e) + r_g V
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming $X_0 \approx 0$ (influent has no biomass of interest) and $r_g = (\mu - k_d) X$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= -(Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e) + (\mu - k_d) X V \quad \text{(steady state)} \\
(\mu - k_d) X V &= Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e
\end{aligned}
$$

By definition $\theta_c = V X / (Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e)$, hence:

$$
\frac{1}{\theta_c} = \mu - k_d \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_c = \frac{1}{\mu - k_d}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **fundamental minimum SRT equation** — a KTU board classic. Rearranged: $\mu = 1/\theta_c + k_d$. If the actual $\theta_c$ (operational) < $\theta_c^{min}$ = $1/(\mu_{max} - k_d)$, washout occurs.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Observed Yield Coefficient

The **true growth yield** $Y$ (kg biomass formed per kg substrate consumed) is reduced by **endogenous decay** $k_d$ (d⁻¹). Over a residence time $\theta_c$, the cumulative decay reduces observed yield:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{obs} &= Y - Y\,k_d\,\theta_c \quad \text{(simple decay model)} \\
Y_{obs} &= Y (1 - k_d \theta_c) \quad \text{(incorrect sign for long SRT)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — using the proper integrated model (decay follows first-order kinetics, so surviving biomass fraction = $e^{-k_d \theta_c}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{obs} &= \frac{Y}{1 + k_d \theta_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical Lawrence-McCarty form**. At $\theta_c \to 0$, $Y_{obs} \to Y$. At large $\theta_c$, $Y_{obs} \to 0$ (all biomass decays). Plug $Y = 0.6$, $k_d = 0.06$ d⁻¹, $\theta_c = 10$ d:
>
> $$
> Y_{obs} = \frac{0.6}{1 + 0.06 \times 10} = \frac{0.6}{1.6} = 0.375
> $$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Oxygen Demand for Carbonaceous + Nitrogenous BOD Removal

The oxygen consumed by heterotrophs on substrate + autotrophs on ammonia:

$$
\text{O}_2 = \underbrace{1.42 \cdot \Delta S_B \cdot \eta_B}_{\text{C-BOD}} + \underbrace{4.57 \cdot \Delta S_N \cdot \eta_N}_{\text{Nitrification}} \quad (\text{kg O}_2/\text{d})
$$

Where:
- $1.42$ = theoretical oxygen demand per kg of biomass oxidised (= 32/22.5, based on $C_5H_7NO_2$ stoichiometry).
- $4.57$ = oxygen demand per kg NH₄⁺-N oxidised to NO₃⁻.
- $\eta$ = fractional removal efficiency.

**Numerical example:**
$Q = 10{,}000$ m³/d, $S_{0,B} = 250$ mg/L, $S_{e,B} = 25$ mg/L, $S_{0,N} = 40$ mg/L, $S_{e,N} = 5$ mg/L:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta S_B &= (250 - 25)\times 10{,}000 \times 10^{-3} = 2250 \text{ kg BOD/d} \\
\Delta S_N &= (40 - 5)\times 10{,}000 \times 10^{-3} = 350 \text{ kg N/d} \\
\text{O}_2 &= 1.42 \times 2250 + 4.57 \times 350 \\
&= 3195 + 1599.5 = 4794.5 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{d}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Methane Yield from Anaerobic Digestion

Starting from Buswell's stoichiometry for glucose:

$$
C_6 H_{12} O_6 \longrightarrow 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2
$$

Molecular weight of glucose = 180 g/mol; each mole produces 3 mol CH₄ = 3 × 22.4 L at STP = 67.2 L.

$$
\frac{67.2 \text{ L}}{180 \text{ g}} = 0.373 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4 / \text{kg glucose}
$$

For arbitrary substrate measured as COD (1 kg COD ≈ 1.125 kg of $C_xH_yO_z$ oxygen equivalent), the universal AD yield is **0.35 m³ CH₄/kg COD removed**.

$$
V_{CH_4} = 0.35 \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S_e) \quad \text{(m}^3\text{ CH}_4\text{/d at STP)}
$$

**Worked example:** Digester treating $Q = 100$ m³/d of sludge with $S_0 = 30{,}000$ mg/L COD, $S_e = 6{,}000$ mg/L COD:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{CH_4} &= 0.35 \times 100 \times (30000 - 6000) \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 0.35 \times 100 \times 24 = 840 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4/\text{d}
\end{aligned}
$$

Energy content: $840 \times 35.8$ MJ/m³ ≈ **30,072 MJ/d ≈ 8356 kWh/d** — sufficient to power ~280 homes (assuming 30 kWh/household/d).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — How Each Process Handles a Disturbance

| Disturbance | Activated Sludge | Biofilm (TF/RBC) | Anaerobic Digestion |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hydraulic surge | Effluent turbidity spike, partial floc washout | Tolerated (HRT decoupled from SRT) | Risk of washout (long SRT required) |
| Toxic load (CN, phenol) | Inhibits heterotrophs, kills flocs | Robust due to diffusional gradient | Highly sensitive — kills methanogens |
| Temperature drop (< 15 °C) | Reduced nitrification | Moderate effect | Severe — methanogenesis halts |
| pH < 6.5 | Moderate tolerance | Moderate | Catastrophic — VFA accumulation |
| High influent BOD | Improves (more food) | Improved removal | Increased biogas yield |

## 4.1 Activated Sludge Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Influent Wastewater Q S0] --> B[Primary Clarifier]
    B --> C[Aeration Tank Reactor V]
    C --> D[Secondary Clarifier]

    D --> F[WAS Waste Activated Sludge]

    G --> H[Sludge Digestion or Disposal]

```

**Reading the flow:**
- Solid arrow = liquid stream.
- The recycle loop **D → I → C** is the **return sludge line** (typically 25–75% of Q).
- The waste line **D → F** controls SRT.

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the activated sludge process with a neat sketch. List the factors affecting the efficiency of the process.**

**Model Answer (structured for 3 marks):**

The activated sludge process is a **suspended-growth, aerobic biological wastewater treatment** process. Wastewater is mixed with a **biomass-rich mixed liquor** in an aeration tank, where microorganisms oxidise organic matter to CO₂ and H₂O. The mixed liquor flows to a **secondary clarifier**; settled sludge is partly recycled (RAS) to maintain MLSS, and partly wasted (WAS).

**Process flow:** Influent → Primary Clarifier → Aeration Tank (with aerators) → Secondary Clarifier → Disinfected Effluent; sludge → Thickening/Digestion.

**Factors affecting efficiency (at least three):**
1. **F/M ratio** — too high → poor flocculation; too low → endogenous respiration, pinpoint flocs.
2. **Dissolved Oxygen** — must be maintained at **1.5–2.0 mg/L** in aeration tank.
3. **Temperature** — affects reaction kinetics; mesophilic range 20–35 °C is optimal.
4. **pH** — should be **6.5–8.5**.
5. **MLSS concentration** — affects both substrate removal and settling.
6. **Sludge age (SRT)** — too short → washout; too long → old, dispersed sludge.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between aerobic and anaerobic biological treatment processes.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Aerobic | Anaerobic |
|---|---|---|
| Oxygen | **Required** | **Strictly absent** |
| End products | CO₂, H₂O, biomass | **CH₄ (60–70%) + CO₂ (30–40%) + biomass** |
| Energy yield | Low (some heat) | **High (biogas — combustible)** |
| Sludge production | High | **Low (1/5 to 1/10 of aerobic)** |
| Treatment efficiency | High BOD removal (>90%) | Moderate (60–80%); used for high-strength wastes |
| Energy input | High (aeration ≈ 1 kWh/kg BOD) | Low |
| Reaction rate | Fast | Slow (long SRT) |
| Typical reactors | Activated sludge, TF, RBC, MBBR | UASB, Anaerobic filter, CSTR digester |
| Sensitivity to toxins | Lower | **Higher** (especially to methanogens) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 1 Choice 1]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Explain the various stages of anaerobic digestion. With a neat sketch describe a typical continuous stirred tank anaerobic digester used for sludge digestion in municipal sewage treatment.**

**Model Solution:**

The anaerobic digestion of sludge proceeds through **four sequential biochemical stages** mediated by different microbial guilds:

1. **Hydrolysis** — Extracellular enzymes (proteases, amylases, lipases, cellulases) secreted by fermentative bacteria break down complex polymers (proteins, carbohydrates, lipids) into soluble monomers (amino acids, sugars, fatty acids). For municipal sludge this is the **rate-limiting step** because of the high particulate and cell-wall content.

2. **Acidogenesis (Fermentation)** — Acidogenic bacteria (e.g., *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*) convert monomers into **volatile fatty acids (VFAs)** — acetate, propionate, butyrate — plus alcohols, H₂, and CO₂. pH drops if unchecked.

3. **Acetogenesis** — Acetogenic bacteria (e.g., *Syntrophomonas*, *Syntrophobacter*) oxidise higher VFAs to **acetate, H₂, and CO₂**. These bacteria live in obligate syntrophy with methanogens because their reactions are thermodynamically unfavourable unless H₂ is kept very low.

4. **Methanogenesis** — Methanogenic archaea convert acetate, H₂, and CO₂ into **methane**. Two pathways:
   - **Acetoclastic** (~70%): $CH_3COOH \to CH_4 + CO_2$
   - **Hydrogenotrophic** (~30%): $4H_2 + CO_2 \to CH_4 + 2H_2O$

**CSTR Digester Sketch (description):**

A cylindrical steel tank (typically 10–30 m diameter, 10–15 m height), partially buried, fitted with:
- **Mechanical mixer / gas recirculation** for complete mixing.
- **Sludge feed inlet** at top.
- **Sludge draw-off** at bottom.
- **Gas collection dome** at top with pressure relief and gas piping.
- **Heating coils/jackets** to maintain mesophilic temperature (35 °C).
- **SRT = HRT** because the reactor is CSTR with no recycle; SRT = 15–40 days.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**A wastewater treatment plant treats 5 MLD of sewage with an influent BOD of 250 mg/L and effluent BOD of 30 mg/L. Calculate (i) the BOD removal efficiency, (ii) the quantity of methane produced per day assuming 90% of removed BOD is converted via anaerobic pathway. Use standard methane yield.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $Q = 5$ MLD = 5000 m³/d
- $S_0 = 250$ mg/L
- $S_e = 30$ mg/L
- Methane yield: $0.35$ m³ CH₄/kg COD removed (we approximate COD ≈ BOD for this problem).

**Step (i) — BOD Removal Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{250 - 30}{250} \times 100 = \frac{220}{250} \times 100 = 88\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii) — Methane Production:**

Mass of BOD removed per day:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta BOD &= Q \times (S_0 - S_e) \\
&= 5000 \text{ m}^3/\text{d} \times (250 - 30) \text{ g/m}^3 \\
&= 5000 \times 220 = 1{,}100{,}000 \text{ g/d} = 1100 \text{ kg/d}
\end{aligned}
$$

BOD actually routed to anaerobic methanogenesis (90%):

$$
\Delta BOD_{anaerobic} = 0.90 \times 1100 = 990 \text{ kg/d}
$$

Methane volume at STP:

$$
V_{CH_4} = 0.35 \times 990 = 346.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}
$$

**Energy recovered:** $346.5 \times 35.8$ MJ/m³ ≈ **12,405 MJ/d ≈ 3446 kWh/d.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 1 Choice 2]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**With a neat sketch, explain the working of a trickling filter. Derive the relationship between substrate removal efficiency and filter depth using the Eckenfelder equation.**

**Model Solution:**

A **trickling filter** is a packed-bed biofilm reactor. Wastewater is **distributed over the top** of a bed of inert media (rock, slag, or plastic) via a **rotary distributor**. As the wastewater trickles downward, a **biofilm** develops on the media surface; air flows naturally upward (or forced) providing oxygen. Treated effluent is collected at the underdrain and clarified. Excess sloughed biomass (humus) is removed in a secondary clarifier.

**Eckenfelder Equation — Derivation:**

For a small increment of filter depth $dD$, the fractional substrate removal is proportional to the substrate concentration remaining and the depth increment:

$$
-\frac{dS}{dD} = k \cdot S \cdot \frac{A^{0.5}}{Q^n}
$$

Where $A$ = specific surface area of media (m²/m³) and $Q$ = hydraulic loading (m³/m²·d).

Separating variables:

$$
-\int_{S_0}^{S_e} \frac{dS}{S} = k \frac{A^{0.5}}{Q^n} \int_{0}^{D} dD
$$

$$
\ln \frac{S_0}{S_e} = k \frac{A^{0.5} D}{Q^n}
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{S_e}{S_0} = \exp\!\left[- k \frac{A^{0.5} D}{Q^n}\right]}
$$

For first-order approximation with $A$ lumped into $k$:

$$
\frac{S_e}{S_0} = e^{-kD/Q^n}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- Increasing $D$ (depth) → greater removal (logarithmic).
- Increasing $Q$ (hydraulic loading) → poorer removal.
- $k$ embeds media properties (specific surface area, voidage, temperature).

**Numerical example:** For $k = 0.4$, $D = 2$ m, $Q = 10$ m³/m²·d, $n = 0.5$:

$$
\frac{S_e}{S_0} = e^{-0.4 \times 2 / 10^{0.5}} = e^{-0.4 \times 2 / 3.162} = e^{-0.253} = 0.776
$$

So removal efficiency ≈ 22.4% — typical of a single-stage roughing filter.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**A conventional activated sludge plant treats 10 MLD of wastewater. The influent BOD is 220 mg/L, MLSS is 3000 mg/L and aeration tank volume is 2500 m³. If the BOD removal efficiency is 92%, calculate (i) F/M ratio, (ii) HRT, (iii) aeration tank efficiency in terms of BOD loading rate (kg BOD/m³·d), and (iv) wasted sludge production per day assuming observed yield 0.4.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $Q = 10$ MLD = 10,000 m³/d
- $S_0 = 220$ mg/L
- $V = 2500$ m³
- $X \approx$ MLVSS ≈ 0.8 × MLSS = **2400 mg/L** (using 80% volatile fraction)
- $\eta = 92\%$
- $Y_{obs} = 0.4$ kg biomass/kg BOD removed

**Step (i) — F/M Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F/M &= \frac{Q S_0}{V X} = \frac{10{,}000 \times 220}{2500 \times 2400} \\
&= \frac{2{,}200{,}000}{6{,}000{,}000} = 0.367 \text{ d}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Falls in the conventional range 0.2–0.5 d⁻¹ — operation is well-tuned.)

**Step (ii) — HRT:**

$$
\theta = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{2500}{10000} = 0.25 \text{ d} = 6 \text{ h}
$$

**Step (iii) — BOD Volumetric Loading:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BOD Load} &= Q \cdot S_0 = 10000 \times 220 \times 10^{-3} = 2200 \text{ kg/d} \\
\text{BOD Volumetric Loading} &= \frac{2200}{2500} = 0.88 \text{ kg BOD/m}^3\cdot\text{d}
\end{aligned}
$$

BOD removed = $2200 \times 0.92 = 2024$ kg/d.

**Step (iv) — Wasted Sludge Production:**

$$
P_X = Y_{obs} \cdot \Delta BOD = 0.4 \times 2024 = 809.6 \text{ kg sludge/d}
$$

In volume, assuming 1% solids content (10,000 mg/L) for thickened WAS:

$$
V_{sludge} = \frac{809.6}{0.01} = 80{,}960 \text{ kg/d} \approx 80.96 \text{ m}^3/\text{d} \text{ (thickened)}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up MLSS and MLVSS** — Most design equations use MLVSS as $X$. If the problem gives MLSS, use $X = 0.7 \text{–} 0.8 \times MLSS$. Failure to do so inflates F/M ratio incorrectly.
> 2. **Units!** $Q$ in m³/d, $S_0$ in mg/L = g/m³, $V$ in m³ → $F/M$ in d⁻¹. Students frequently forget the $10^{-3}$ conversion or write $X$ in g/L without conversion.
> 3. **Sign convention in $S_0$ vs $S_e$** — Always $S_0 > S_e$. $\Delta S = S_0 - S_e > 0$.
> 4. **Methane yield** — Use **0.35 m³ CH₄/kg COD removed**, NOT per kg substrate loaded. Multiply by $\Delta S$, not $S_0$.
> 5. **Eckenfelder equation exponent** — Many students drop the $A^{0.5}$ term. In its simplified form, $k$ absorbs it; in its full form, $A$ is explicit.
> 6. **Stokes vs Newton for sludge settling** — KTU does not expect Stokes' law in Module 1; settleability is described by **SVI only**.
> 7. **Conflating SRT and HRT** — In a CSTR (e.g., anaerobic digester), they are equal. In an activated sludge system with recycle, **SRT ≫ HRT**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorise for KTU ESE**

- **Three pillars of biological treatment:** Activated sludge (suspended, aerobic), Biofilm (attached, mostly aerobic), Anaerobic digestion (suspended/granular, anaerobic).
- **MLSS vs MLVSS:** Use MLVSS in design equations; MLSS for settling.
- **F/M ratio rule of thumb:** Conventional = 0.2–0.5 d⁻¹; Extended aeration = 0.05–0.15 d⁻¹; High rate = 0.5–1.5 d⁻¹.
- **θc = V X / (Qw Xw + Qe Xe)** — fundamental sludge age formula.
- **Y_obs = Y / (1 + kd θc)** — accounts for endogenous decay.
- **SVI = (SV₃₀ / MLSS) × 1000** in mL/g; good sludge = 50–150 mL/g.
- **Oxygen demand coefficients:** 1.42 kg O₂/kg biomass; 4.57 kg O₂/kg NH₄⁺-N.
- **Trickling filter:** Eckenfelder $S_e/S_0 = \exp(-kA^{0.5}D/Q^n)$.
- **RBC standard loading:** 5–15 g BOD/m²·d for secondary treatment.
- **Anaerobic digestion four stages:** Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis.
- **Methane yield:** 0.35 m³ CH₄ per kg COD removed at STP; 0.37 m³ CH₄/kg glucose.
- **Methanogens:** Strict anaerobes, slow-growing (doubling 3–30 d), need **pH 6.8–7.4**, **T = 35 °C (mesophilic)** or 55 °C (thermophilic).
- **HRT for AD:** 15–40 days; SRT = HRT for CSTR.
- **Biogas composition:** ~60–70% CH₄, 30–40% CO₂, trace H₂S, NH₃.
- **Buswell's equation:** Generic formula for theoretical methane yield from any organic compound.
- **Process selection rule of thumb:** BOD > 1000 mg/L → anaerobic; tight footprint → biofilm; nitrification → extended aeration/BNR.
- **Diffusion in biofilm:** Fick's law; effectiveness factor η falls below 1 for thick biofilms.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sludge treatment and disposal: sludge thickening, sludge conditioning, sludge dewatering, sludge digestion and composting (brief overview)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT613 - PROCESS DESIGN FOR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sludge treatment and disposal: sludge thickening, sludge conditioning, sludge dewatering, sludge digestion and composting (brief overview)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sewage Sludge (Biosolids)** is the semi-liquid residual material produced as a by-product of municipal and industrial wastewater treatment processes. It consists primarily of water (**95% to 99%** by weight), with the remaining fraction containing organic and inorganic solids, pathogens, heavy metals, and trace contaminants.

**Sludge Treatment** refers to a sequence of unit operations and processes designed to:
1. **Reduce volume** (by removing water),
2. **Stabilize** organic matter (to prevent putrefaction and odor),
3. **Condition** the matrix (to improve dewaterability),
4. **Eliminate pathogens**, and
5. **Prepare the final residue** for safe disposal or beneficial reuse (e.g., land application).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module requires only a *brief overview* of the major unit operations. The focus is on understanding the **process objective, working principle, and application logic** of each stage, not on deep mathematical design of digesters.

### 1.2 The Five-Stage Treatment Train (Process Sequence)

The complete sludge treatment line consists of the following sequential stages:

| Stage | Unit Operation | Primary Objective | Typical Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Thickening** | Volume reduction by gravity/buoyancy | DS content: **0.5% → 4–6%** |
| 2 | **Conditioning** | Destabilize colloidal structure | Improved dewaterability |
| 3 | **Dewatering** | Mechanical water removal | Cake solids: **20–35%** |
| 4 | **Digestion** | Biological stabilization | VS reduction: **30–60%** |
| 5 | **Composting / Disposal** | Final stabilization / End-use | Pathogen-free humus |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wet Sponge" Model

Imagine a **kitchen sponge soaked in greasy, dirty water** — that is essentially raw sludge.

- **Thickening** = Letting the sponge sit on a plate so gravity pulls water out.
- **Conditioning** = Squeezing a little dish soap into the sponge to break the grease (loosens bound water).
- **Dewatering** = Physically wringing the sponge with a press or a vacuum.
- **Digestion** = Leaving the sponge in a warm, sealed container so bacteria "eat" the grease (stabilization).
- **Composting** = Mixing the sponge contents with dry leaves and letting it cure into rich, earthy soil.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Water exists in sludge in four forms — *free water, floc water, capillary water, and bound (intracellular) water*. Each stage targets a different water fraction. Thickening removes free water; conditioning/digestion attacks floc and capillary water; dewatering removes capillary water; only thermal drying can fully remove bound water.

### 1.4 Source and Characteristics of Sludge

Sewage sludge originates from three principal points in a wastewater treatment plant (WWTP):

1. **Primary Sludge** — Settled solids from primary clarifiers (high in organics, easy to thicken).
2. **Secondary (Biological) Sludge** — Excess activated sludge (WAS) or trickling filter humus (high in bound water, gelatinous).
3. **Tertiary/Chemical Sludge** — From advanced treatment (e.g., phosphate precipitation).

**Standard Design Parameters (bolded for KTU exam):**
- **Primary sludge solids content:** **2–6%** dry solids (DS)
- **Waste activated sludge (WAS) solids content:** **0.5–1.5%** DS
- **Combined (primary + secondary) sludge:** **3–6%** DS
- **Specific gravity of dry solids:** **~1.4 (organic)** to **~2.5 (inorganic)**
- **Sludge Volume Index (SVI) for WAS:** typically **80–150 mL/g**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stage 1 — Sludge Thickening

**Objective:** Increase the solids concentration by removing a portion of the liquid (free water) under gravity, flotation, or centrifugal force. This reduces the volume of sludge to be processed downstream, thereby **saving capital and operating costs**.

**Thickening Methods:**
- **Gravity Thickening** — Most common; sludge is held in a circular tank with slowly rotating pickets; based on Stoke's law settling.
- **Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF) Thickening** — Air bubbles attach to sludge particles, floating them upward; preferred for buoyant activated sludge.
- **Centrifugal Thickening** — High-speed bowl centrifuges; compact footprint but higher power consumption.
- **Gravity Belt Thickening (GBT)** — Sludge is drained over a porous moving belt under gravity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule of Thumb (KTU standard):** A well-designed gravity thickener can achieve a **solids loading of 30–50 kg/m²·day** and a **hydraulic loading of 15–30 m³/m²·day**.

### 2.2 Stage 2 — Sludge Conditioning

**Objective:** Destabilize the colloidal structure of sludge flocs (which hold large amounts of bound water) so that water can be released in the subsequent dewatering step.

**Methods of Conditioning:**
- **Chemical Conditioning:** Addition of inorganic coagulants (e.g., **ferric chloride FeCl₃, alum, lime Ca(OH)₂**) or synthetic organic polyelectrolytes (cationic polyacrylamides). Most widely used in municipal WWTPs.
- **Physical Conditioning:**
  - *Thermal conditioning* — Heating sludge to **150–200°C** under pressure; dissolves cell walls (used before dewatering in many plants).
  - *Freeze–thaw conditioning* — Natural or mechanical; effective but climate-dependent.
- **Biological (Enzymatic) Conditioning** — Addition of bio-surfactants or enzymes; an emerging sustainable option.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polymer Dose:** Typical cationic polymer dose for sludge conditioning = **2–10 kg polymer per tonne of dry solids (kg/TDS)**. KTU exam questions often quote this range.

### 2.3 Stage 3 — Sludge Dewatering

**Objective:** Mechanically remove as much water as possible to produce a "cake" suitable for transport, incineration, or landfilling.

**Dewatering Devices:**
- **Centrifuge (Solid-Bowl)** — High rotational speed (**2000–4000 rpm**); produces cake of **20–30% DS**.
- **Belt Filter Press** — Two porous belts squeeze sludge between rollers; cake of **18–28% DS**.
- **Vacuum Filter (Drum/Coil)** — Older technology; cake of **15–25% DS**.
- **Sludge Drying Beds (Sand Beds)** — Low-cost, weather-dependent; cake of **25–40% DS** (after weeks of drying).
- **Filter Press (Plate & Frame)** — Batch operation, high-pressure (**700–1500 kPa**); cake of **30–45% DS** — the highest mechanical dewatering.

### 2.4 Stage 4 — Sludge Digestion (Stabilization)

**Objective:** Biologically decompose biodegradable organic matter, reduce pathogens, and produce biogas (a renewable energy by-product).

**Two Principal Modes:**

**(a) Anaerobic Digestion (AD) — Most common for municipal sludge**
- Operates in the **absence of oxygen**.
- Occurs in sealed reactors called **digesters**.
- Temperature regimes:
  - **Mesophilic:** **30–38°C** (retention time ~20–30 days)
  - **Thermophilic:** **50–57°C** (faster, better pathogen kill, ~10–15 days)
- Biogas produced: **~60–70% methane (CH₄), 30–40% CO₂, trace H₂S**.
- **Gas yield:** approximately **0.7–1.1 m³ biogas per kg VS destroyed**.

**(b) Aerobic Digestion**
- Operates with continuous aeration; similar to activated sludge but with no food source.
- Simpler equipment; produces a more filterable, humus-like product.
- **No biogas recovery** (no energy benefit).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Comparison Point:** Anaerobic digestion is preferred when **energy recovery** is desired (biogas can be used for digester heating, electricity). Aerobic digestion is preferred for **simplicity and low-cost plants**.

### 2.5 Stage 5 — Sludge Composting

**Objective:** Aerobically stabilize dewatered sludge cake by mixing with a **bulking agent** (e.g., wood chips, sawdust, straw) to achieve a final product (humus) suitable for agricultural use.

**Process Parameters:**
- **C:N ratio of mix:** **25:1 to 35:1** (critical for microbial activity).
- **Moisture content:** maintained at **50–60%** for optimum decomposition.
- **Temperature:** thermophilic phase at **55–65°C** (pathogen destruction).
- **Duration:** **3–6 weeks active composting** + **4–12 weeks curing**.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Solids Concentration** | $C = \dfrac{M_s}{V_{sludge}} \times 100$ | % | Thickened sludge: **4–6%** |
| 2 | **Sludge Volume (Solids Basis)** | $V_2 = V_1 \times \dfrac{C_1}{C_2}$ | m³ | Volume inversely proportional to solids % |
| 3 | **Mass of Solids** | $M_s = Q \times C \times \rho_w$ | kg/d | — |
| 4 | **Specific Resistance to Filtration (SRF)** | $r = \dfrac{2 \cdot b \cdot P \cdot A^2}{\mu \cdot c}$ | m/kg | Dewaterability metric; **r < 10¹² m/kg** = good |
| 5 | **Capillary Suction Time (CST)** | measured via timer | seconds | **CST < 20 s** = readily dewaterable |
| 6 | **Digester Volume (CSTR)** | $V = Q \times \theta$ | m³ | Q = sludge flow; θ = HRT (days) |
| 7 | **Volatile Solids Reduction (VSR)** | $VSR = \dfrac{VS_{in} - VS_{out}}{VS_{in}} \times 100$ | % | **30–60%** in mesophilic AD |
| 8 | **Biogas Yield** | $G = 0.7 \text{ to } 1.1 \times VS_{destroyed}$ | m³/d | Methane content: **60–70%** |
| 9 | **Solids Loading Rate (Thickener)** | $SLR = \dfrac{M_s}{A}$ | kg/m²·d | Gravity: **30–50**; DAF: **100–250** |
| 10 | **Polymer Dose** | $D_p = \dfrac{m_{polymer}}{M_{DS}}$ | kg/TDS | **2–10** for cationic polyelectrolyte |
| 11 | **Composting C:N Ratio** | $C:N = \dfrac{M_C}{M_N}$ | dimensionless | **25–35 : 1** |
| 12 | **Sludge Specific Gravity (Mix)** | $\dfrac{1}{S_{sludge}} = \dfrac{W_s}{S_s} + \dfrac{W_w}{1.0}$ | — | $S_s \approx 1.4$ (organic), **2.5** (inorganic) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The solids-based volume equation ($V_2 = V_1 \cdot C_1/C_2$) is the single most-used relationship in sludge management. It instantly answers "what is the volume reduction?" — an essential cost and tank-sizing question for WWTPs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Solids-Based Volume Reduction (Thickening Mass Balance)

The fundamental relation used to evaluate volume reduction after thickening is derived from conservation of dry solids.

**Starting Principle:** Mass of dry solids entering = Mass of dry solids leaving (no solids destruction in thickening).

$$M_{s,\text{in}} = M_{s,\text{out}}$$

**Step 1 — Express dry solids in terms of volume and concentration:**

$$Q_1 \cdot C_1 \cdot \rho_w = Q_2 \cdot C_2 \cdot \rho_w$$

where $Q$ = sludge flow rate (m³/d), $C$ = solids concentration (fraction), $\rho_w$ = density of water (≈1000 kg/m³).

**Step 2 — Cancel the common terms $\rho_w$:**

$$Q_1 \cdot C_1 = Q_2 \cdot C_2$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to obtain the volume ratio:**

$$V_2 = V_1 \cdot \dfrac{C_1}{C_2}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the working numerical problem:**

> **Worked Example (KTU typical):** A WWTP produces **200 m³/d** of primary sludge at **3% DS**. After gravity thickening, solids are raised to **5% DS**. Compute the thickened sludge volume.

**Solution:**

$$V_2 = 200 \times \dfrac{0.03}{0.05}$$

$$V_2 = 200 \times 0.6$$

$$V_2 = \boxed{120 \ \text{m}^3/\text{d}}$$

Thus, thickening reduces sludge volume by **40%** (from 200 → 120 m³/d).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Specific Resistance to Filtration (SRF)

SRF is the most widely used analytical measure of sludge dewaterability. It is derived from Darcy's law applied to a filter medium.

**Darcy's Law for filter cake filtration:**

$$\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{P \cdot A^2}{\mu \cdot (r \cdot c \cdot V + R_m \cdot A)}$$

where:
- $V$ = filtrate volume (m³)
- $t$ = time (s)
- $P$ = applied pressure (N/m²)
- $A$ = filter area (m²)
- $\mu$ = filtrate viscosity (N·s/m²)
- $r$ = specific resistance (m/kg)
- $c$ = mass of solids per unit filtrate volume (kg/m³)
- $R_m$ = resistance of filter medium (1/m)

**Step 1 — For highly resistant cakes, $r \cdot c \cdot V \gg R_m \cdot A$ (medium resistance neglected):**

$$\dfrac{dV}{dt} \approx \dfrac{P \cdot A^2}{\mu \cdot r \cdot c \cdot V}$$

**Step 2 — Cross-multiply and integrate from 0 to $V$ and 0 to $t$:**

$$\dfrac{t}{V} = \dfrac{\mu \cdot r \cdot c \cdot V}{2 \cdot P \cdot A^2}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to solve for $r$:**

$$r = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot A^2 \cdot b}{\mu \cdot c}$$

where $b$ = slope of the straight line obtained when $t/V$ is plotted against $V$ (i.e., $b = \Delta(t/V)/\Delta V$).

**Step 4 — Interpretation of $r$:**
- $r < 4 \times 10^{11}$ m/kg → **readily dewaterable**
- $4 \times 10^{11} < r < 10^{13}$ m/kg → **moderately dewaterable**
- $r > 10^{13}$ m/kg → **difficult to dewater (needs chemical conditioning)**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** Although SRF is mentioned for context, the syllabus calls for only a *brief overview*. Be ready to write the *definition* and *interpretation bands*, not full laboratory derivations.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Sludge Treatment Technologies

| Criterion | Gravity Thickener | DAF Thickener | Anaerobic Digester | Aerobic Digester | Centrifuge (Dewatering) | Drying Bed |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Solids in feed** | 0.5–1.5% | 0.5–1.5% | 3–6% | 1–3% | 1–4% | 1–4% |
| **Solids in product** | 4–6% | 4–7% | 3–5% (digestate) | 1–2% | 20–30% | 25–40% |
| **Energy** | Low | Medium | Negative (produces biogas) | High (aeration) | High | Negligible |
| **Footprint** | Large | Small | Medium | Large | Very small | Very large |
| **Operator Skill** | Low | Medium | High | Medium | High | Low |
| **Capital Cost** | Low | Medium | High | Medium | High | Very low |
| **Pathogen Kill** | None | None | Good (esp. thermophilic) | Good | None | Excellent (solar) |
| **Energy Recovery** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (biogas) | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **Typical Use** | Primary sludge | WAS | Municipal mixed | Small plants | Medium-large plants | Small/tropical plants |

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Anaerobic Digester Sizing (CSTR)

A standard mesophilic anaerobic digester is sized using a continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR) approach.

**Step 1 — Definition of Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):**

$$\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q}$$

**Step 2 — Solved for volume:**

$$V = Q \times \theta$$

**Step 3 — Worked Example (KTU typical 14-mark pattern):**

> A WWTP produces **50 m³/d** of thickened mixed sludge. Design the volume of a mesophilic anaerobic digester for an HRT of **25 days**.

$$V = 50 \times 25 = \boxed{1250 \ \text{m}^3}$$

**Step 4 — Biogas yield estimation:**

Assuming VS concentration in feed = **30,000 mg/L = 30 kg/m³** and VS destruction = **50%**:

$$\text{VS destroyed} = 50 \times 30 \times 0.5 = 750 \ \text{kg/d}$$

$$\text{Biogas produced} = 0.9 \times 750 = \boxed{675 \ \text{m}^3/\text{d}}$$

(CH₄ content ≈ 65%, so usable methane ≈ **438 m³/d**, energy ≈ **~9.2 GJ/d** or ~2560 kWh/d)

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Overall Sludge Treatment Train — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[WWTP Liquid Stream] --> B[Primary Clarifier]
    A --> C[Secondary Clarifier / Aeration Tank]
    B --> P1[Primary Sludge 2-6 percent DS]
    C --> P2[Waste Activated Sludge 0.5-1.5 percent DS]
    P1 --> STAGE1
    P2 --> STAGE1
    STAGE1[Stage 1 Thickening] --> STAGE2[Stage 2 Conditioning Chemicals or Heat]
    STAGE2 --> STAGE3[Stage 3 Dewatering Centrifuge or Belt Press]
    STAGE3 --> STAGE4[Stage 4 Digestion Anaerobic or Aerobic]
    STAGE4 --> STAGE5[Stage 5 Composting or Disposal]
    STAGE5 --> OUT1[Land Application as Biosolids]
    STAGE5 --> OUT2[Sanitary Landfill]
    STAGE5 --> OUT3[Incineration]
    STAGE4 --> BIOGAS[Biogas CH4 plus CO2 captured for energy]
    BIOGAS --> ENERGY[Boiler CHP Engine Electricity]
    style STAGE1 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px
    style STAGE2 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72,stroke-width:2px
    style STAGE3 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:2px
    style STAGE4 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px
    style STAGE5 fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#512e5f,stroke-width:2px
    style BIOGAS fill:#fff3a0,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px
    style ENERGY fill:#abebc6,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The diagram shows the **five sequential stages** plus three alternative disposal pathways.
- Energy recovery is a side stream from the digestion stage — a key sustainability argument in modern plant design.
- The colour coding distinguishes each stage for quick reference in the KTU exam.

### 4.2 Anaerobic Digestion Process — Four-Stage Biochemistry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1 Hydrolysis] --> S2[Stage 2 Acidogenesis]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3 Acetogenesis]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4 Methanogenesis]
    S1 --> N1[Inputs Complex Polymers Proteins Lipids Carbohydrates]
    N1 --> S1
    S2 --> N2[Outputs Volatile Fatty Acids VFAs Alcohols CO2 H2]
    N2 --> S2
    S3 --> N3[Outputs Acetate H2 CO2]
    N3 --> S3
    S4 --> N4[Outputs Methane CH4 Carbon Dioxide CO2]
    N4 --> S4
    style S1 fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#7e5109
    style S2 fill:#f5cba7,stroke:#935116
    style S3 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1a5276
    style S4 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#145a32
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** "**Hydrolysis**" is the rate-limiting step in anaerobic digestion of complex sludges. The methanogens are the most sensitive to environmental shocks (pH drop, temperature fluctuation, toxic inputs). The four stages must be in balance; otherwise, VFA accumulation acidifies the digester and stalls gas production.

### 4.3 Composting Process — Mass & Energy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Mixed Sludge Cake plus Bulking Agent] --> P1[Phase 1 Mesophilic 25-40 C]
    P1 --> P2[Phase 2 Thermophilic 55-65 C]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3 Cooling and Curing]
    P3 --> P4[Stable Humus Product]
    P1 --> EM1[CO2 plus Heat and Microbial Growth]
    P2 --> EM2[Pathogen Destruction and Weed Seed Inactivation]
    P3 --> EM3[Maturation and Humification]
    style P1 fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d
    style P2 fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#9c640c
    style P3 fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d
    style P4 fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#512e5f
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
Define sewage sludge. List the major unit operations involved in sludge treatment and disposal, in correct sequence.

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

**Sewage sludge** is the semi-solid, organic-rich residue separated from municipal or industrial wastewater during primary, secondary, and tertiary treatment stages. It contains **95–99% water** by weight, with the remainder comprising organic and inorganic solids, pathogens, and trace contaminants.

**The major unit operations in sequence are:**

1. **Thickening** — to reduce water content and sludge volume,
2. **Conditioning** — to improve dewaterability (chemical / thermal),
3. **Dewatering** — mechanical removal of water (centrifuge, belt press),
4. **Digestion (stabilization)** — anaerobic or aerobic decomposition of organics,
5. **Composting / Final disposal** — land application, landfill, or incineration.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often omit **conditioning** as a separate stage, or write the sequence out of order. KTU specifically expects a *logical flow*. Marks are deducted for missing steps.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
Differentiate between **aerobic digestion** and **anaerobic digestion** of sludge in four points.

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Aerobic Digestion | Anaerobic Digestion |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Oxygen requirement** | Continuous aeration needed | No oxygen; sealed reactor |
| 2 | **End products** | CO₂, H₂O, stabilized biosolids | Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂), digestate |
| 3 | **Energy recovery** | Not possible (energy consumer) | Possible (biogas to boiler/CHP) |
| 4 | **Retention time** | 20–40 days (extended aeration) | 20–30 days (mesophilic) |
| 5 | **Operating temperature** | Ambient to mesophilic | Mesophilic (35°C) or thermophilic (55°C) |
| 6 | **Pathogen reduction** | Moderate | High (esp. thermophilic) |

---

### PART B — ESE Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Option 1]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **complete sludge treatment train** from sludge generation to final disposal. Mention typical solids concentrations at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The complete sludge treatment train consists of the following sequential stages:

**1. Sludge Generation:** Primary sludge from primary clarifiers (**2–6% DS**) and waste activated sludge (WAS) from secondary clarifiers (**0.5–1.5% DS**) are combined.

**2. Thickening:** Solids increased to **4–6% DS** (gravity thickener) or **4–7% DS** (DAF thickener). Volume reduction: **40–80%**.

**3. Conditioning:** Chemical (FeCl₃ + lime, or cationic polymer at **2–10 kg/TDS**) or thermal conditioning prepares sludge for dewatering.

**4. Dewatering:** Mechanical dewatering (centrifuge → **20–30% DS**; belt press → **18–28% DS**; filter press → **30–45% DS**).

**5. Digestion (Stabilization):** Anaerobic digester at **35°C (mesophilic)**, HRT **20–30 days**; biogas yield **0.7–1.1 m³/kg VS destroyed**; VS reduction **30–60%**.

**6. Final Disposal:** Land application as biosolids (if standards met), incineration, or sanitary landfill.

**Diagram:** Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1 above (KTU students should redraw the flowchart neatly during the exam).

| Stage | Typical DS% |
|---|---|
| Raw primary sludge | 2–6% |
| Raw WAS | 0.5–1.5% |
| Thickened sludge | 4–6% |
| Digested sludge | 3–5% |
| Dewatered cake | 20–35% |
| Dried sludge | 90–95% |

**(b)** A wastewater treatment plant produces **150 m³/d** of mixed sludge at **3.5% solids**. After gravity thickening, the solids content rises to **5.5%**. Calculate: **(i)** the volume of thickened sludge, **(ii)** the percentage volume reduction, and **(iii)** the mass of dry solids handled per day. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $V_1 = 150$ m³/d; $C_1 = 3.5\% = 0.035$; $C_2 = 5.5\% = 0.055$; $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³.

**(i) Volume of thickened sludge:**

$$V_2 = V_1 \times \dfrac{C_1}{C_2} = 150 \times \dfrac{0.035}{0.055}$$

$$V_2 = 150 \times 0.6364 = \boxed{95.45 \ \text{m}^3/\text{d}}$$

**(ii) Percentage volume reduction:**

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \dfrac{V_1 - V_2}{V_1} \times 100 = \dfrac{150 - 95.45}{150} \times 100$$

$$= \dfrac{54.55}{150} \times 100 = \boxed{36.37\%}$$

**(iii) Mass of dry solids per day:**

$$M_s = Q_1 \times C_1 \times \rho_w = 150 \times 0.035 \times 1000$$

$$M_s = \boxed{5250 \ \text{kg/d}}$$

**Verification (mass balance check):**

$$M_{s,\text{out}} = 95.45 \times 0.055 \times 1000 = 5250 \ \text{kg/d} \quad \checkmark$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Option 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle, working, and design considerations of an anaerobic sludge digester**. Mention the biochemical stages and key design parameters. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** Anaerobic digestion is the biochemical decomposition of organic matter by microorganisms in the **absence of oxygen**, producing biogas (CH₄ + CO₂) and stabilized biosolids.

**Working:** Thickened sludge is fed into a sealed, heated, and mixed reactor. Microorganisms break down organics through four sequential biochemical stages:

1. **Hydrolysis** — Complex polymers (proteins, lipids, carbohydrates) are broken into monomers. *(Rate-limiting step.)*
2. **Acidogenesis** — Monomers are converted into volatile fatty acids (VFAs), alcohols, CO₂, and H₂.
3. **Acetogenesis** — VFAs and alcohols are converted into acetate, H₂, and CO₂.
4. **Methanogenesis** — Acetate and H₂/CO₂ are converted into **CH₄** and **CO₂** (the biogas).

**Design Considerations:**

| Parameter | Typical Value (Mesophilic) |
|---|---|
| Temperature | **30–38°C** (mesophilic) / **50–57°C** (thermophilic) |
| Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) | **20–30 days** |
| Solids Retention Time (SRT) | **20–40 days** |
| Volatile Solids (VS) loading | **1.6–3.2 kg VS/m³·day** |
| VS reduction | **30–60%** |
| Biogas yield | **0.7–1.1 m³/kg VS destroyed** |
| Methane content | **60–70%** |
| pH | **6.8–7.4** (strict control) |
| Alkalinity | **>2000 mg/L as CaCO₃** (buffering) |
| C:N ratio of feed | **20:1 to 30:1** |
| Mixing | Continuous or intermittent; mechanical / gas recirculation |

**Reactor types:** Standard-rate (no mixing/heating), high-rate (mixed, heated), and two-stage (separate acid-phase and methane-phase reactors).

**(b)** Design an **anaerobic digester** for a WWTP producing **80 m³/d** of thickened sludge. The design HRT is **25 days**, and the digester is a CSTR. Calculate (i) the **digester volume**, (ii) the **methane gas production rate** if VS concentration is **25,000 mg/L** and VS destruction efficiency is **50%**, assuming methane yield = **0.5 m³ CH₄ per kg VS destroyed**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $Q = 80$ m³/d; $\theta = 25$ days; $VS = 25{,}000$ mg/L = **25 kg/m³**; VS destruction = **50%**; methane yield = **0.5 m³ CH₄/kg VS destroyed**.

**(i) Digester Volume:**

$$V = Q \times \theta = 80 \times 25 = \boxed{2000 \ \text{m}^3}$$

For a cylindrical digester with a height-to-diameter ratio of 1:1, the diameter and height are:
- Diameter $D \approx \left(\dfrac{4V}{\pi}\right)^{1/3} \approx \left(\dfrac{4 \times 2000}{3.1416}\right)^{1/3} \approx (2546.5)^{1/3} \approx 13.66$ m
- Height $H \approx 13.66$ m (rounded to **~14 m**)

**(ii) Methane Production Rate:**

**Step 1 — Mass of VS fed per day:**

$$M_{VS} = Q \times VS = 80 \times 25 = 2000 \ \text{kg VS/d}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of VS destroyed per day:**

$$M_{VS,destroyed} = M_{VS} \times \text{efficiency} = 2000 \times 0.5 = 1000 \ \text{kg/d}$$

**Step 3 — Methane gas production rate:**

$$G_{CH_4} = 1000 \times 0.5 = \boxed{500 \ \text{m}^3 \ \text{CH}_4/\text{d}}$$

**Step 4 — Total biogas production (if CH₄ ≈ 65% of biogas):**

$$G_{biogas} = \dfrac{500}{0.65} = \boxed{769.2 \ \text{m}^3/\text{d}}$$

**Step 5 — Energy content of methane:**

$$E = 500 \times 35.8 \ \text{MJ/m}^3 = 17{,}900 \ \text{MJ/d} \approx \boxed{4970 \ \text{kWh/d}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Three common mistakes that cost KTU students 2–3 marks each:
> 1. **Unit confusion** between mg/L and kg/m³ (numerically equal, but must be stated correctly).
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply yield by VS destroyed**, not VS fed. Students who write 0.5 × 2000 directly get full credit; those who mistakenly use 0.5 × 25 × 80 without the destruction factor lose marks.
> 3. **Not showing the energy conversion** in the final step — for a 7-mark sub-question, examiners expect at least 4–5 well-separated calculation lines.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Notes for KTU Exam Day**

- ✅ Sludge is **~95–99% water** — sludge treatment is essentially **water management**.
- ✅ **Thickening** is volume reduction (no solids destruction); use $V_2 = V_1 \cdot C_1/C_2$.
- ✅ **Conditioning** is a *preparatory* step — it does not remove water itself but enables the *next* step to work.
- ✅ **Dewatering** produces a "cake" (**20–35% DS**); the higher the cake solids, the lower the disposal cost.
- ✅ **Anaerobic digestion** = **biogas** (renewable energy) + stabilized biosolids. The four biochemical stages end with **methanogenesis**.
- ✅ **Hydrolysis** is the rate-limiting step of anaerobic digestion — remember this for theory questions.
- ✅ **Aerobic digestion** is simpler, but no energy recovery.
- ✅ **Composting C:N ratio = 25:1 to 35:1** is the magic number for the exam.
- ✅ Typical yields: **Biogas = 0.7–1.1 m³/kg VS destroyed**; CH₄ content **60–70%**; energy value **~35.8 MJ/m³** of CH₄.
- ✅ Digester volume = $Q \times \theta$ (simple CSTR design).
- ✅ VS reduction efficiency: **30–60%** for mesophilic AD.
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark questions, always (a) draw a labelled diagram first, (b) list the design parameters in a table, then (c) do the calculation with clear units.
- ✅ Common formula trap: SRF interpretation — **lower SRF = better dewaterability**.
- ✅ Final disposal routes: **land application, sanitary landfill, incineration, or reuse as construction fill**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Topic:** Solid waste treatment: sources and classification, disposal methods - open dumping, sanitary landfill, incineration, composting (brief overview)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Solid Waste — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solid Waste (as per Municipal Solid Waste Management Rules, 2016 — MoEFCC, India):**
> *"Solid waste" means and includes solid or semi-solid domestic waste, sanitary waste, commercial waste, institutional waste, bio-medical waste, industrial waste, construction and demolition waste, e-waste, hazardous waste, and any other waste which is not a gas or liquid in nature.*

In KTU environmental engineering parlance, **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** is the heterogeneous mixture of predominantly **household refuse**, **commercial waste**, **street sweepings**, **construction debris**, and **institutional waste** generated within a municipality or notified area.

**Key Standard Metrics (commonly used in KTU numericals):**

- **Per capita waste generation (India, urban):** 0.2 – 0.6 kg/capita/day
- **In-situ density of MSW in a landfill:** **500 – 900 kg/m³**
- **Density (loose):** 100 – 200 kg/m³
- **Moisture content:** 20 – 60 % (Indian MSW)
- **Calorific value:** 1000 – 1500 kcal/kg (Indian MSW — low)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Sink of a City"

Imagine a city is a giant household. Every day, citizens produce **trash from the kitchen** (food scraps), **the study** (paper, e-waste), **the garage** (broken parts), and **the bathroom** (sanitary waste). **Solid waste management (SWM)** is essentially the city's housekeeping system — collecting, segregating, treating, and finally *disposing of* this mixed "city garbage" in a way that does not pollute the groundwater, the air, or make people sick.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Solid Waste Hierarchy (KTU favourite diagram)** — ranked from MOST preferred to LEAST preferred:
> **Prevent → Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover (energy) → Treat → Dispose**
> Landfilling and open dumping are the **LAST resort** in the hierarchy.

---

## 1.3 Sources of Solid Waste

Solid waste originates from **seven major generator categories** (CPHEEO Manual on MSW, India):

| Source Category | Typical Examples | % of MSW (India) |
|---|---|---|
| **Residential** | Food, paper, plastic, yard waste | 40 – 60 % |
| **Commercial** | Office paper, packaging, restaurant waste | 15 – 25 % |
| **Institutional** | School, hospital, government waste | 5 – 10 % |
| **Construction & Demolition (C&D)** | Concrete, steel, wood, bricks | 10 – 30 % |
| **Street Sweeping** | Leaves, dust, silt | 5 – 10 % |
| **Industrial (non-hazardous)** | Process scraps, slag | Variable |
| **Biomedical & Hazardous** | Needles, chemicals, e-waste | 1 – 5 % |

---

## 1.4 Classification of Solid Waste

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification by KTU Examination Standard — 4 different ways:**

**(a) By Physical State**
- Solid (paper, plastic, glass)
- Semi-solid (sludge, kitchen waste)

**(b) By Origin**
- **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)**
- **Industrial Solid Waste**
- **Biomedical Waste**
- **Hazardous Waste**
- **E-Waste**
- **Construction & Demolition Waste**

**(c) By Biodegradability** (most exam-relevant)
- **Biodegradable / Organic** — food, yard waste, paper
- **Non-biodegradable / Inorganic** — plastic, glass, metal, rubber

**(d) By Hazard Potential**
- **General / Non-hazardous waste**
- **Hazardous waste** (flammable, toxic, reactive, corrosive)

---

## 1.5 The 4 Disposal / Treatment Methods (Module Scope)

This module covers **brief overview** of:

1. **Open Dumping** — primitive, uncontrolled
2. **Sanitary Landfill** — engineered disposal on land
3. **Incineration** — thermal treatment / combustion
4. **Composting** — biological treatment of organic fraction

> [!NOTE]
> **Mental Map:** Open dumping = *no engineering*; Sanitary landfill = *engineered*; Incineration = *burns it*; Composting = *lets nature eat it*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Disposal Method 1 — Open Dumping

**Definition:** The unscientific, uncontrolled disposal of collected solid waste on **low-lying land**, abandoned quarries, or marshes **without any engineered liner, daily cover, leachate collection, or gas venting**.

**Characteristics (Why it is the worst):**
- No leachate control → **groundwater contamination**
- No gas venting → **methane buildup → fires, explosions**
- No compaction → easy access for **vectors (rats, flies, mosquitoes)**
- No daily cover → **odour, litter, wind-blown plastic**
- **Methane** (CH₄) is a greenhouse gas ~ **25 times more potent than CO₂**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"Why is open dumping not permitted under SWM Rules 2016?"*
> Answer: It causes uncontrolled leachate, GHG emissions, vector breeding, and is unscientific — the Rules mandate engineered disposal only.

**Indian Examples (commonly asked):**
- Ghazipur (Delhi), Deonar (Mumbai), Kodungaiyur (Chennai)

---

## 2.2 Disposal Method 2 — Sanitary Landfill

**Definition:** An **engineered facility** for the disposal of solid waste on land in a manner that protects **public health and the environment** (Tchobanoglous, 1993).

**The 5 Essential Components of a Modern Sanitary Landfill:**

| # | Component | Engineering Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Liner System** (clay / HDPE / GCL) | Prevents leachate → groundwater migration |
| 2 | **Leachate Collection & Removal System (LCRS)** | Drains leachate to treatment plant |
| 3 | **Gas Collection System** | Extracts landfill gas (CH₄ + CO₂) for flaring or energy use |
| 4 | **Daily Cover (150–300 mm soil)** | Controls vectors, odour, litter, fire |
| 5 | **Final Cover (Multi-layer)** | After closure — minimises infiltration |

**Three Construction Methods of a Landfill:**

**(a) Trench Method** — for *flat terrain*, dig a trench, fill waste, cover with excavated soil.
**(b) Area Method** — for *hilly/undulating* terrain, build above ground in layers.
**(c) Depression / Ramp Method** — in a natural valley or abandoned quarry, fill from the bottom upward.

**Landfill Site Selection Criteria (KTU high-weight topic):**
- Distance from airport: ≥ **10 km** (bird-strike)
- Distance from habitation: ≥ **500 m**
- Distance from water body: ≥ **100 m** (periphery), **200 m** (high flood line)
- Distance from highway / river: ≥ **200 m**
- Depth to water table: ≥ **3 m** (or > 1.5 m from bedrock)
- Avoid **flood plains**, **wetlands**, **fault zones**

---

## 2.3 Disposal Method 3 — Incineration

**Definition:** **Thermal treatment** of solid waste at high temperatures in the presence of excess air (oxygen) to convert it into **ash, flue gas, and heat**.

**The Three Ts of Combustion (Burn it Right):**

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| **Temperature** | 850 – 1100 °C (afterburner ≥ 1000 °C) |
| **Turbulence** | Mixing of waste and air — *good mixing = complete combustion* |
| **Time (Residence)** | ≥ 2 seconds for gases (to destroy dioxins) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Volume Reduction by Incineration:** ~ **90 %**
> **Mass Reduction:** ~ **70 %**
> **Calorific Value of MSW** (needed for self-sustaining combustion): ≥ **1200 kcal/kg**

**Types of Incinerators (KTU classification):**

- **Mass-burn incinerators** (large-scale, no pre-processing) — e.g., Timarpur, Delhi
- **Modular incinerators** (small community / hospital waste)
- **Refuse-Derived Fuel (RDF) incinerators** (after shredding & drying)

**Key Pollutants (must be controlled):**
- **Dioxins & Furans** (PCDD/F)
- **Heavy metals** (Hg, Pb, Cd)
- **HCl, SO₂, NOx**
- **Particulate matter (PM)**
- **Air Pollution Control Devices (APCDs):** ESP, baghouse, wet scrubber, dry scrubber

---

## 2.4 Disposal Method 4 — Composting

**Definition:** The **biological decomposition** of the **organic (biodegradable) fraction** of solid waste by **micro-organisms** under **controlled aerobic** (oxygen-rich) conditions to produce a humus-like, nutrient-rich product called **compost**.

**Aerobic Composting Process:**
$$\text{Organic Matter} + O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{microbes}} \text{Compost} + CO_2 + H_2O + \text{Heat}$$

**Anaerobic Composting / Digestion:**
$$\text{Organic Matter} \xrightarrow{\text{absence of } O_2} \text{Bio-gas (CH}_4 \text{ + CO}_2\text{)} + \text{Digestate}$$

**The Ideal Carbon-to-Nitrogen Ratio (C/N):**

| C/N Ratio | Effect |
|---|---|
| **< 20 : 1** | Excess N → ammonia loss → odour |
| **25 – 30 : 1** | **OPTIMAL** — microbes are most efficient |
| **> 35 : 1** | Excess C → slow decomposition |

**Composting Methods (KTU table):**

| Method | Scale | Aeration | Time | India Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Windrow** (open heaps) | Large | Manual turning | 6 – 12 weeks | Most Indian municipalities |
| **In-Vessel / Bin** | Medium | Forced aeration | 2 – 4 weeks | Solid waste plants |
| **Vermicomposting** | Small | Earthworms (Eisenia foetida) | 8 – 12 weeks | Schools, decentralized |
| **Anaerobic Digestion** | Medium-Large | Sealed tank | 20 – 30 days | Nisargruna Biogas plants |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these 7 equations — they appear almost every KTU examination cycle.**

**(1) Mass-Volume Relationship for Landfill:**
$$V_{landfill} = \frac{M_{waste}}{\rho_{in-situ}}$$

Where $V_{landfill}$ = volume of landfill cell (m³), $M_{waste}$ = mass of waste (kg), $\rho_{in-situ}$ = compacted density in landfill ($\approx$ **600 – 900 kg/m³**)

**(2) Required Air Space (Tchobanoglous equation):**
$$V_{air} = V_{waste} + V_{daily\,cover} + V_{leachate\,storage} + V_{final\,cover}$$

**(3) Landfill Water Balance / Leachate Estimation:**
$$L = P - R - ET - \Delta S - CR$$

Where $L$ = leachate generated, $P$ = precipitation, $R$ = runoff, $ET$ = evapotranspiration, $\Delta S$ = change in soil moisture storage, $CR$ = capillary release (all in mm/yr)

**(4) IPCC First-Order Methane Generation Model:**
$$Q_{CH_4} = L_0 \cdot M \cdot \left(1 - e^{-k t}\right)$$

Where $Q_{CH_4}$ = methane generation rate (m³/yr), $L_0$ = methane generation potential (m³ CH₄/kg waste), $M$ = mass of waste in place (kg), $k$ = decay constant (yr⁻¹), $t$ = time (yr)

For MSW, typical **$L_0$ ≈ 0.4 – 0.5 m³/kg** of waste (dry basis) and **$k$ ≈ 0.05 – 0.1 yr⁻¹**

**(5) Volume Reduction by Incineration (%):**
$$\% \text{Reduction} = \frac{V_{in} - V_{out}}{V_{in}} \times 100$$

**(6) Mass Reduction by Incineration (%):**
$$\% \text{Mass Reduction} = \frac{M_{in} - M_{out}}{M_{in}} \times 100$$

**(7) C/N Ratio of Compost Mix:**
$$C/N_{mix} = \frac{\sum(C_i \times m_i)}{\sum(N_i \times m_i)}$$

Where $C_i$, $N_i$ = carbon % and nitrogen % of each component, $m_i$ = mass of component

**Real-world engineering utility:**
- Equation (1) is used by **KSPCB / CPCB** to size landfill cells.
- Equation (4) is used to design **landfill gas (LFG) recovery systems** and earn **carbon credits** under CDM/Verra.
- Equation (7) is used in **mechanical biological treatment (MBT)** plants to balance wet kitchen waste with dry leaves.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Landfill Volume Calculation

**Problem:** A city generates **400 tonnes/day** of MSW with a compacted in-situ density of **750 kg/m³**. Daily cover adds **15 % extra volume**. Calculate the **annual landfill volume** required. Density of cover soil = **1500 kg/m³**, cover thickness = **0.2 m**.

**Step 1 — Convert daily mass to kg:**
$$M_{daily} = 400 \text{ tonnes/day} \times 1000 \text{ kg/tonne} = 4 \times 10^5 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2 — Volume of waste (compacted) per day:**
$$V_{waste} = \frac{M_{daily}}{\rho_{in-situ}} = \frac{4 \times 10^5 \text{ kg/day}}{750 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

$$V_{waste} = 533.33 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 3 — Add 15 % volume for daily cover:**
$$V_{cell} = V_{waste} \times 1.15 = 533.33 \times 1.15 = 613.33 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 4 — Annual volume:**
$$V_{annual} = 613.33 \times 365 = 2.239 \times 10^5 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to more intuitive units:**
$$V_{annual} \approx 2.24 \times 10^5 \text{ m}^3/\text{yr} \approx 0.224 \text{ million m}^3/\text{yr}$$

> This is the **minimum engineered landfill cell volume** needed per year.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — IPCC Methane Generation

**Problem:** A landfill has accumulated **$M$ = 5 × 10⁶ kg** of waste at closure (assumed deposited at one time, $t = 0$). Compute methane generation rate at $t$ = **5 years**, $t$ = **10 years**, and $t$ = **20 years**.
Given: $L_0$ = 0.4 m³ CH₄/kg waste, $k$ = 0.05 yr⁻¹.

**Step 1 — Recall the model:**
$$Q_{CH_4}(t) = L_0 \cdot M \cdot \left(1 - e^{-k t}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the coefficient $L_0 \cdot M$:**
$$L_0 \cdot M = 0.4 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg} \times 5 \times 10^6 \text{ kg} = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate at $t = 5$ years:**
$$e^{-0.05 \times 5} = e^{-0.25} = 0.7788$$

$$Q_{CH_4}(5) = 2 \times 10^6 \times (1 - 0.7788) = 2 \times 10^6 \times 0.2212 = 4.424 \times 10^5 \text{ m}^3/\text{yr}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate at $t = 10$ years:**
$$e^{-0.05 \times 10} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$

$$Q_{CH_4}(10) = 2 \times 10^6 \times (1 - 0.6065) = 2 \times 10^6 \times 0.3935 = 7.870 \times 10^5 \text{ m}^3/\text{yr}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate at $t = 20$ years:**
$$e^{-0.05 \times 20} = e^{-1.0} = 0.3679$$

$$Q_{CH_4}(20) = 2 \times 10^6 \times (1 - 0.3679) = 2 \times 10^6 \times 0.6321 = 1.264 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3/\text{yr}$$

**Interpretation:** Methane generation **rises with time** and **asymptotes** at $L_0 \cdot M = 2 \times 10^6$ m³/yr. Gas extraction systems must be sized for this long-term profile.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Compost C/N Ratio Blending

**Problem:** A composting plant mixes **1000 kg of kitchen waste** (C = 45 %, N = 2.5 %) with **500 kg of dry leaves** (C = 50 %, N = 0.8 %). Compute the **C/N ratio of the mix**.

**Step 1 — Carbon in kitchen waste:**
$$C_1 = 1000 \times 0.45 = 450 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Nitrogen in kitchen waste:**
$$N_1 = 1000 \times 0.025 = 25 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Carbon in dry leaves:**
$$C_2 = 500 \times 0.50 = 250 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Nitrogen in dry leaves:**
$$N_2 = 500 \times 0.008 = 4 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Total carbon and nitrogen:**
$$C_{total} = 450 + 250 = 700 \text{ kg}$$

$$N_{total} = 25 + 4 = 29 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6 — C/N ratio of mix:**
$$C/N_{mix} = \frac{C_{total}}{N_{total}} = \frac{700}{29} \approx 24.1$$

**Interpretation:** 24.1 is **within the optimal range of 25 – 30 : 1**, indicating good microbial activity. (To improve further, mix in more dry carbonaceous material.)

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All 4 Disposal Methods

> [!NOTE]
> This **comparison table** is THE most asked KTU table in MSW units — *memorize it line by line.*

| Parameter | Open Dumping | Sanitary Landfill | Incineration | Composting |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Capital Cost** | Very Low | High | Very High | Medium |
| **Operating Cost** | Low | Medium | High | Medium |
| **Volume Reduction** | 0 % | −30 % (compaction) | ~ 90 % | ~ 50 % |
| **Environmental Risk** | Very High | Low | Medium (air) | Low |
| **Leachate Risk** | High | Controlled | Low | Low |
| **GHG Emissions** | Very High (CH₄) | Moderate (captured) | Moderate (CO₂) | Low |
| **Energy Recovery** | None | Possible (LFG) | Yes (heat/power) | None / slight |
| **Final Product** | None | Landfilled mass | Ash + flue gas | Compost |
| **End-Use of Output** | None | Land (long term) | Electricity / heat | Soil fertilizer |
| **SWM 2016 Status** | **Prohibited** | Permitted (only) | Permitted | Permitted |
| **Best Suited For** | Nowhere (legacy) | Mixed, residual MSW | Low-moisture, high-CV waste | Organic / biodegradable fraction |

---

## 3.5 Logical Decision Flow — Which Disposal Method to Pick?

**Step 1 — Segregate the waste into Organic vs Inorganic.**

**Step 2 — If Organic fraction > 50 % and moisture > 50 %** → **Composting** (or anaerobic digestion).

**Step 3 — If Inorganic fraction is dominant, calorific value > 1500 kcal/kg, and land is scarce** → **Incineration with energy recovery.**

**Step 4 — If mixed residual waste, land is available, and CV < 1000 kcal/kg** → **Sanitary Landfill** (the default disposal for non-recoverable residual).

**Step 5 — Never** → **Open Dumping** (banned under SWM Rules 2016).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1 — Solid Waste Hierarchy & Disposal Methods (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Prevent Reduce Reuse] --> B[Recycle]
    B --> C[Recovery Energy]
    C --> D[Treat]
    D --> E{Choose Disposal Method}

    E -->|High calorific value low moisture| G[Incineration]
    E -->|Mixed residual inert| H[Sanitary Landfill]
    E -->|Illegal not permitted| I[Open Dumping BANNED]
    F --> J[Compost Soil amendment]
    G --> K[Ash Energy flue gas treatment]
    H --> L[Landfill cell liner leachate gas control]
    I --> M[Groundwater contamination methane fire]
```

---

## 4.2 Figure 2 — Modern Sanitary Landfill Cross Section (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        A1[Final Multi Layer Cover Clay Veg Soil]
    end

    end

    TOP --> GAS --> WASTE --> LCRS --> LINER
```

---

    B --> C[Combustion Chamber 850 to 1100 deg C]

    E --> F[Ash Collection Bottom and Fly Ash]

```

    D -->|Anaerobic Digester| F[Bio gas CH4 plus CO2]
    E --> G[Heat 55 to 65 deg C pathogen kill]
