<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List and briefly explain any two engineered components of a sanitary landfill.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Liner System (1.5 marks):** A composite barrier made of compacted clay (≥ 0.6 m) and HDPE geomembrane (1.5–2.5 mm) installed at the base of the landfill to prevent leachate migration into groundwater.

2. **Leachate Collection and Removal System — LCRS (1.5 marks):** A network of perforated HDPE pipes embedded in a gravel blanket above the liner, which drains the percolating contaminated liquid (leachate) to a sump for treatment.

*(Alternate valid components: Gas collection wells, daily cover, final cover, monitoring wells.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is open dumping of municipal solid waste not permitted under SWM Rules 2016? Give any two reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Groundwater contamination (1.5 marks):** Open dumps have no liner or leachate collection; rainwater percolating through waste carries toxic leachate directly into the soil and aquifer.

2. **Greenhouse gas emissions and fire hazard (1.5 marks):** Anaerobic decomposition releases methane (a GHG 25× more potent than CO₂), and uncontrolled buildup of CH₄ leads to spontaneous fires and explosions (e.g., Deonar Mumbai 2016, Ghazipur Delhi 2022).

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[INTERNAL CHOICE — Answer ANY ONE of the following]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Sources, Classification & Sanitary Landfill**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Classify municipal solid waste in detail based on (i) origin, (ii) biodegradability, and (iii) hazard potential. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Classification by Origin (2 marks):**

- **Residential / Domestic waste** — food scraps, paper, packaging
- **Commercial waste** — offices, restaurants, hotels
- **Institutional waste** — schools, hospitals, government buildings
- **Construction & Demolition (C&D) waste** — concrete, bricks, steel
- **Street sweeping & silt** — leaves, road grit
- **Industrial (non-hazardous) waste** — process scraps, fly ash
- **Biomedical / Hazardous / E-waste** — needles, chemicals, electronics

**(ii) Classification by Biodegradability (2.5 marks):**

- **Biodegradable / Organic fraction** (40–60 % of Indian MSW): food waste, yard trimmings, paper, cardboard. Decomposes quickly under microbial action.
- **Non-biodegradable / Inorganic fraction**: plastics, glass, metals, rubber, leather. Remains in environment for decades to centuries.

**(iii) Classification by Hazard Potential (2.5 marks):**

- **General / Non-hazardous waste**: domestic, commercial, institutional — handled by municipality.
- **Hazardous waste**: flammable, toxic, reactive, corrosive — regulated under Hazardous Waste Management Rules 2016, handled by specialized agencies with manifest system.
- **Biomedical waste**: regulated under BMW Management Rules 2016 (separate colour-coded bins: yellow, red, blue, white).
- **E-waste**: regulated under E-Waste Management Rules 2016.

---

**(b) With the help of a neat sketch, explain the essential components of a sanitary landfill. List the criteria for site selection. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Essential Components (3 marks)** — Refer to Figure 2 in Section 4 for the schematic.

1. **Bottom Liner System** (compacted clay + HDPE geomembrane) — prevents leachate entering groundwater
2. **Leachate Collection and Removal System (LCRS)** — perforated pipes in gravel blanket to drain leachate
3. **Gas Collection System** — vertical/horizontal wells to extract methane for flaring or power generation
4. **Daily Cover (150–300 mm)** — placed at end of each day to control vectors, odour, litter, fire
5. **Final Cover (multi-layer)** — applied at closure to minimise infiltration and erosion
6. **Surface Water Drainage** — peripheral storm water drains
7. **Monitoring Wells** — upstream and downstream groundwater sampling

**Site Selection Criteria (4 marks):**

- Distance from airport: ≥ 10 km
- Distance from habitation / water supply: ≥ 500 m
- Distance from river / water body: ≥ 100 m (periphery), 200 m (high flood line)
- Depth to water table: ≥ 3 m (or 1.5 m above bedrock)
- Soil should be low-permeability clay
- Avoid flood plains, wetlands, fault zones, environmentally sensitive areas
- Adequate haulage distance (≤ 25 km typically)
- Land area sufficient for 20–25 years of operation
- Approach road accessibility throughout the year

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Incineration & Composting**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) What is incineration? List the "3 Ts" of combustion. Explain the air pollution control devices (APCDs) used in an incineration plant. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Incineration is the **controlled thermal treatment** of solid waste at high temperatures (850–1100 °C) in the presence of excess air, converting it into **ash, flue gas, and heat**, with potential recovery of energy.

**The 3 Ts of Combustion (2 marks):**

| T | Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | Combustion chamber | ≥ 850 °C (afterburner ≥ 1000 °C) |
| **Time** | Gas residence in secondary chamber | ≥ 2 seconds |
| **Turbulence** | Mixing of waste + air | Good mixing = complete burn |

If any of the 3 Ts is insufficient → **incomplete combustion → dioxins / CO / soot**.

**Air Pollution Control Devices (APCDs) (4 marks):**

1. **Particulate Control:**
   - **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)** — removes fly ash by charging particles; efficiency > 99 %
   - **Baghouse (Fabric Filter)** — fabric bags capture PM; efficiency > 99.9 %

2. **Acid Gas Control (HCl, SO₂):**
   - **Wet Scrubber** — lime/NaOH slurry spray
   - **Dry Scrubber** — lime injection; produces dry residue

3. **NOx Control:**
   - **Selective Non-Catalytic Reduction (SNCR)** — urea/ammonia injection
   - **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** — for stricter limits

4. **Dioxin/Furan Control:**
   - **Activated Carbon Injection (ACI)** upstream of baghouse
   - Rapid quenching from 300 °C → 100 °C prevents re-synthesis

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often **skip the 3 Ts** and directly write about APCDs — losing 2 marks.
> 2. Many write *only* ESP and forget the **dioxin/NOx control** — losing 2 more.
> 3. Always mention that **gas residence time ≥ 2 s at ≥ 850 °C** is the *legal minimum* under Indian CPCB norms.
> 4. Naming an incinerator type (mass-burn / modular / RDF) carries an additional 1 mark.

---

**(b) Define composting. Differentiate between aerobic and anaerobic composting. List the factors affecting the composting process. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Composting is the **biological decomposition** of the **organic fraction** of solid waste by micro-organisms under **controlled conditions** to produce a humus-like, nutrient-rich product called compost, which is used as a soil conditioner.

**Aerobic vs Anaerobic Composting (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Aerobic Composting | Anaerobic Composting / Digestion |
|---|---|---|
| Oxygen | Required (free O₂) | Absent (no O₂) |
| End Products | Compost + CO₂ + H₂O + Heat | Bio-gas (CH₄ + CO₂) + Digestate |
| Decomposition time | 6 – 12 weeks (windrow) | 20 – 30 days (digester) |
| Temperature | 55 – 65 °C (thermophilic) | 35 – 55 °C (mesophilic) |
| Odour | Low | Can be high (H₂S) |
| Energy recovery | None directly | Yes — bio-gas for cooking/electricity |
| C/N requirement | 25 – 30 : 1 (ideal) | 20 – 30 : 1 |
| Example method | Windrow, in-vessel | Nisargruna, biogas plant |

**Factors Affecting Composting (3 marks):**

1. **C/N Ratio** — optimal 25–30 : 1
2. **Moisture Content** — optimal 50–60 %
3. **Aeration / Oxygen supply** — ≥ 5 % O₂ in pile
4. **Temperature** — thermophilic range 55–65 °C for pathogen kill
5. **pH** — 6.5 – 8.0
6. **Particle size** — 25–75 mm (smaller = faster, but needs aeration)
7. **Turning frequency** — every 3–7 days for windrow

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students confuse **composting** with **landfilling** — composting is a *biological*, *aerobic* process; landfill is *physical*, *anaerobic*.
> 2. **Vermicomposting** is a *type of aerobic composting* — please don't list it as a separate method unless the question asks for a list of all methods.
> 3. The **C/N ratio of 25–30:1 is the single most expected numerical fact** in any composting question.
> 4. Always mention the **end-use of compost** (soil conditioner / fertilizer) — losing this is a 0.5–1 mark penalty.
> 5. Don't write "composting = decomposition of waste" — the **controlled, engineered** aspect must be highlighted.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Solid Waste =** anything solid/semi-solid discarded (SWM Rules 2016 definition).
- **Sources:** Residential (40–60%) > Commercial > C&D > Institutional > Industrial > Street Sweeping > Biomedical/E-waste.
- **Classification:** by origin, biodegradability, hazard potential, physical state.
- **Solid Waste Hierarchy:** Prevent → Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover → Treat → Dispose.
- **Open Dumping:** Primitive, **BANNED** under SWM 2016; causes leachate + CH₄ + fires.
- **Sanitary Landfill:** Engineered facility with **liner + LCRS + gas collection + daily cover + final cover**.
- **Landfill Site Criteria:** ≥ 10 km from airport, ≥ 500 m from habitation, ≥ 100 m from water body, water table depth ≥ 3 m.
- **Landfill Methods:** Trench (flat land), Area (hilly), Depression (valley / quarry).
- **In-situ Density:** 600 – 900 kg/m³ (compacted).
- **Methane Generation:** $Q_{CH_4} = L_0 \cdot M \cdot (1 - e^{-kt})$; $L_0 \approx 0.4$ m³/kg; $k \approx 0.05$ yr⁻¹.
- **Leachate Water Balance:** $L = P - R - ET - \Delta S - CR$.
- **Incineration 3 Ts:** **Temperature** (≥ 850 °C), **Time** (≥ 2 s), **Turbulence**.
- **Incineration Reduction:** Volume ~ 90 %, Mass ~ 70 %.
- **Calorific Value needed for self-sustaining burn:** ≥ 1200 kcal/kg.
- **APCDs in Incinerator:** ESP / Baghouse (PM) + Wet/Dry Scrubber (acid gas) + ACI (dioxin) + SNCR/SCR (NOx).
- **Composting:** Aerobic biological decomposition of **organic** fraction.
- **Optimal C/N Ratio:** **25 – 30 : 1**.
- **Aerobic end-products:** Compost + CO₂ + H₂O + Heat.
- **Anaerobic end-products:** Bio-gas (CH₄ + CO₂) + Digestate.
- **Composting Methods:** Windrow (large), In-Vessel (medium), Vermicomposting (small), Anaerobic Digestion (large).
- **Indian Landfill Examples to Avoid:** Ghazipur, Deonar, Kodungaiyur (open dumps).
- **Indian Incinerator Examples:** Timarpur (Delhi), Okhla (Delhi) — mass-burn plants.
- **Indian Composting Examples:** Indore (windrow — famous model city), Nisargruna (anaerobic).
- **Indian Regulations:** SWM Rules 2016, Hazardous Waste Rules 2016, BMW Rules 2016, E-Waste Rules 2016.
- **Methane Global Warming Potential:** ~ **25 × CO₂** over 100 years.
- **The single most asked KTU formula:** $V_{landfill} = M_{waste} / \rho_{in-situ}$.

> ✅ **End of Module 2 Notes — Solid Waste Treatment**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Noise pollution: sources, effects on people, control methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT613 - PROCESS DESIGN FOR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Noise pollution: sources, effects on people, control methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Noise Pollution

**Noise pollution** is defined as the presence of unwanted, excessive, or harmful sound in the environment that disrupts normal human activities, harms physiological or psychological health, damages property, or degrades the ecological balance. From an engineering perspective, the **World Health Organization (WHO)** and the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB, India)** define noise pollution as any sound that has the potential to cause adverse health effects on humans and other living organisms when exposed for a significant duration.

In KTU terminology aligned with the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** and the **Noise Pollution (Regulation and Control) Rules, 2000** (amended 2010, 2011), noise pollution is characterized by three measurable parameters:
- **Frequency (Hz)** — the pitch of sound
- **Sound Pressure Level (dB)** — the loudness
- **Duration of Exposure (hours)** — the time of impact

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 2, you must be able to (i) identify the principal sources of noise in industrial and urban environments, (ii) describe the physiological, psychological, and behavioral effects on humans, and (iii) explain the engineering control methods (source, path, and receiver control).

### 1.2 The Audible Spectrum and Reference Standards

The human ear responds to sound pressure in the range of approximately **20 Hz to 20,000 Hz**. Because the range of audible sound pressure spans over six orders of magnitude, a **logarithmic decibel (dB) scale** is used rather than a linear one.

| Parameter | Reference Value | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Reference sound pressure | **20 µPa (0.00002 Pa)** | $p_0$ |
| Reference sound intensity | **10⁻¹² W/m²** | $I_0$ |
| Reference sound power | **10⁻¹² W (1 pW)** | $W_0$ |
| Threshold of hearing | 0 dB SPL | — |
| Threshold of pain | 120–130 dB SPL | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the decibel scale?** Human hearing responds logarithmically to stimulus intensity (Weber–Fechner Law). The decibel compresses the enormous linear range of pressures into a manageable 0–140 dB scale, where every 10 dB increase represents a **10-fold increase in sound intensity** and a **2-fold increase in perceived loudness**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Banana-Bunch Principle"

Imagine you are in a quiet library reading a book (≈ 30 dB). Suddenly, one person starts talking (≈ 60 dB). The conversation is now 30 dB louder than the background — but does it sound 1,000 times louder? No — it sounds roughly **8 times louder** (because every 10 dB doubles the perceived loudness). Now add a second person talking at the same volume. The level rises only by **3 dB**, not by another 30 dB! This is the *non-linear addition of sound* — the single most tested concept in KTU noise problems. The "banana bunch" rule: **adding two identical sound sources does not double the loudness; it adds just 3 dB**.

### 1.4 Categories of Noise

- **Continuous / Steady-State Noise** — produced by machinery operating at constant output (transformers, compressors, HVAC systems).
- **Intermittent Noise** — occurs in bursts with intervals of silence (aircraft flyovers, rail crossings).
- **Impulsive / Impact Noise** — short-duration, high-intensity bursts (hammering, explosions, pile driving).
- **Infrasound (<20 Hz)** and **Ultrasound (>20 kHz)** — both can cause physiological harm even if inaudible.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Sound Quantities

Sound is a mechanical, longitudinal pressure wave traveling through an elastic medium. Three fundamental quantities describe any sound field:

**1. Sound Pressure (p)** — the local pressure deviation from atmospheric, measured in **Pascals (Pa)**.

**2. Sound Intensity (I)** — the acoustic power transmitted per unit area in the direction of wave propagation, measured in **W/m²**. For a plane/ spherical wave:
$$I = \frac{p^2}{\rho \, c}$$
where $\rho$ is the medium density and $c$ is the speed of sound (≈ 343 m/s in air at 20 °C).

**3. Sound Power (W)** — the total acoustic energy radiated per unit time by a source, measured in **Watts (W)**. This is an intrinsic property of the source, independent of distance.

### 2.2 The Decibel Equations (KTU Most-Tested)

Because the linear values span 12+ orders of magnitude, all three quantities are expressed in decibels:

$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right) \text{ dB} \quad \text{(Sound Pressure Level)}$$

$$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) \text{ dB} \quad \text{(Sound Intensity Level)}$$

$$L_W = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{W_0}\right) \text{ dB} \quad \text{(Sound Power Level)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor of **20** in the SPL equation comes from squaring the pressure ratio (since intensity is proportional to pressure²). The factor of **10** in SIL/SWL is because intensity and power are first-order quantities.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units | Key Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$, $p_0 = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$ | dB | Field measurements |
| 2 | Sound Intensity Level | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$, $I_0 = 10^{-12}\,\text{W/m}^2$ | dB | Free-field calculations |
| 3 | Sound Power Level | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}(W/W_0)$, $W_0 = 10^{-12}\,\text{W}$ | dB | Source rating |
| 4 | Total Noise (n sources) | $L_{tot} = 10 \log_{10}\!\big[\sum 10^{L_i/10}\big]$ | dB | Multi-source addition |
| 5 | Two-source difference rule | $\Delta L > 10 \Rightarrow L_{tot} \approx L_{max}$ | dB | Quick estimates |
| 6 | Inverse-Square Law (free field) | $L_{p_2} = L_{p_1} - 20\log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$ | dB | Distance attenuation |
| 7 | Hemispherical spreading (outdoor) | $L_p = L_W - 20\log_{10}(r) - 8$ | dB | Ground-level source |
| 8 | Equivalent Continuous Level | $L_{eq} = 10\log_{10}\!\Big[\tfrac{1}{T}\!\int_0^T 10^{L(t)/10}\,dt\Big]$ | dBA | Variable noise rating |
| 9 | Noise Pollution Level (LNP) | $L_{NP} = L_{eq} + K_n \sigma$ | dBA | Community annoyance |
| 10 | Day–Night Sound Level | $L_{dn} = 10\log_{10}\!\Big[\tfrac{1}{24}\big(15\!\cdot\!10^{L_d/10} + 9\!\cdot\!10^{(L_n+10)/10}\big)\Big]$ | dBA | 24-hr combined metric |
| 11 | Transmission Loss (wall) | $TL = 10\log_{10}(1/\tau)$ | dB | Partition acoustics |
| 12 | Sabine Reverberation Time | $T_{60} = 0.161 \, V / A$, $A = \sum S_i \alpha_i$ | s | Room acoustics |
| 13 | Mass Law (barrier) | $TL \approx 20 \log_{10}(m f) - 47$ | dB | Single-wall attenuation |
| 14 | OSHA Dose formula | $D = 100 \sum \tfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | % | Hearing damage risk |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the table above, the **vertical bar** $\vert$ is written as $\vert$ to avoid breaking markdown. In LaTeX it is always rendered as $\vert$.

### 2.4 The Two-Source Addition Rule (Procedure for KTU Problems)

When two or more uncorrelated noise sources operate simultaneously, their **sound pressures (not decibels) add in quadrature**. The decibel rule of thumb used by KTU examiners is:

| Difference $\Delta L = L_1 - L_2$ (dB) | Add to higher level |
|---|---|
| 0 or 1 | +3 dB |
| 2 or 3 | +2 dB |
| 4 to 9 | +1 dB |
| ≥ 10 | 0 dB (ignore weaker) |

### 2.5 Octave Bands and Frequency Analysis

Human hearing is not equally sensitive to all frequencies. The **A-weighting network** approximates the 40-phon equal-loudness contour and is the standard for community noise regulations. Sound levels measured with A-weighting are denoted **dBA**. A standard octave-band analysis uses centre frequencies:
**31.5, 63, 125, 250, 500, 1000, 2000, 4000, 8000, 16000 Hz**.

### 2.6 Engineering Relevance

These formulas are the backbone of:
- **Industrial hygiene assessments** (OSHA / ISO 9612 compliance)
- **Environmental Impact Assessments (EIA)** under the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC), India
- **Architectural acoustics** for auditoria and hospital design
- **Design of mufflers, barriers, and silencers** for IC engines and DG sets
- **Urban traffic noise mapping** using SoundPLAN / CadnaA software

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Addition of Two Sound Pressure Levels

**Statement:** Two uncorrelated noise sources produce sound pressures $p_1$ and $p_2$. Derive the combined Sound Pressure Level $L_{p,\,tot}$.

**Step 1 — Total RMS Pressure.** For two statistically independent (uncorrelated) sources, total RMS pressure adds in quadrature:

$$p_{tot}^2 = p_1^2 + p_2^2$$

**Step 2 — Divide both sides by $p_0^2$** so that the RHS is dimensionless:

$$\frac{p_{tot}^2}{p_0^2} = \frac{p_1^2}{p_0^2} + \frac{p_2^2}{p_0^2}$$

**Step 3 — Take common logarithm and multiply by 10:**

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{tot}^2}{p_0^2}\right) = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_1^2}{p_0^2}\right) + \log_{10}\!\left[1 + \frac{p_2^2}{p_1^2}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Apply the definition $L_p = 20\log_{10}(p/p_0)$:**

$$L_{p,\,tot} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_1^2}{p_0^2} + \frac{p_2^2}{p_0^2}\right)$$

$$L_{p,\,tot} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{L_1/10} + 10^{L_2/10}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Factor the larger term** $L_1 \geq L_2$:

$$L_{p,\,tot} = L_1 + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(1 + 10^{(L_2 - L_1)/10}\right)$$

This is the **master equation** for two-source noise addition. The second term is always non-negative and is tabulated in Section 2.4.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Equivalent Continuous Sound Level ($L_{eq}$)

**Statement:** For a noise signal $L(t)$ that varies with time, derive the single-value equivalent level $L_{eq}$ that delivers the same A-weighted sound energy over measurement time $T$.

**Step 1 — Start with instantaneous A-weighted intensity proportional to squared pressure:**

$$I(t) = \frac{p_A^2(t)}{\rho c}$$

**Step 2 — Total energy over duration T** is:

$$E_{tot} = \int_0^T I(t)\, dt = \frac{1}{\rho c}\int_0^T p_A^2(t)\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Define the constant equivalent intensity** $\bar{I}$ delivering the same total energy:

$$\bar{I} \cdot T = E_{tot} \;\Rightarrow\; \bar{I} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T I(t)\, dt$$

**Step 4 — Convert to decibels using $L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}(\bar{I}/I_0)$:**

$$L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T \frac{p_A^2(t)}{p_0^2}\, dt\right] = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T 10^{L(t)/10}\, dt\right]$$

This is the **integral definition** of $L_{eq}$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Two machines operate simultaneously in a workshop. Machine A produces 84 dBA at the operator's station; Machine B produces 81 dBA. Determine the total sound pressure level at the operator's position.

**Solution:**

$$\Delta L = 84 - 81 = 3 \text{ dB}$$

From the table in §2.4, $\Delta L = 3 \Rightarrow$ **add 2 dB** to the higher level.

$$L_{tot} = 84 + 2 = 86 \text{ dBA}$$

**Verification using the master equation:**

$$L_{tot} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{8.4} + 10^{8.1}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(2.512 \times 10^8 + 1.259 \times 10^8\right)$$

$$L_{tot} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(3.771 \times 10^8\right) = 10 \times 8.576 = 85.76 \text{ dBA} \approx 86 \text{ dBA} \;\checkmark$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Inverse-Square Law

**Problem:** A compressor emits 90 dBA at a distance of 5 m. Assuming free-field (spherical) propagation, find the SPL at 50 m.

**Solution:**

$$L_{p_2} = L_{p_1} - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = 90 - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{50}{5}\right) = 90 - 20 \log_{10}(10)$$

$$L_{p_2} = 90 - 20 \times 1 = 70 \text{ dBA}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Every 10-fold increase in distance reduces SPL by 20 dB in free field.** The same level reduction requires the weaker source to be turned off completely, illustrating the power of the *path-control* approach (see §4).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Health Effects vs. Exposure Level

| Continuous $L_{eq}$ (dBA) | Physiological Effect | Psychological Effect | Communication Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| 50–55 | None significant | Mild annoyance possible | Normal conversation |
| 60–65 | Slight hearing shift (temporary) | Annoyance increases | Telephone use marginal |
| 70–75 | Vasoconstriction, sleep disturbance | Reduced work efficiency | Shouting at 1 m |
| 80–85 | Tinnitus risk after years | Irritability, fatigue | Shouting at 2 m |
| 90–95 | Permanent threshold shift begins | Anxiety, aggression | Very difficult |
| 100–110 | Hearing loss after months | Severe stress, hypertension | Impossible without effort |
| 120+ | Immediate pain, ear damage | Startle reflex, panic | None |
| 140+ | Physical eardrum rupture possible | Disorientation, nausea | None |

### 3.6 Source → Pathway → Receiver: An Engineering Triad

Effective noise control always operates on **at least one** of three elements:

1. **At the Source** — reduce generation (e.g., vibration damping, replacement with quieter equipment, lubrication).
2. **Along the Path** — reduce transmission (e.g., barriers, enclosures, silencers, distance).
3. **At the Receiver** — protect the listener (e.g., ear plugs, ear muffs, administrative rotation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Cost-Effectiveness:** Source control is the most economical; path control is intermediate; receiver control is the most expensive over the project life-cycle. The "hierarchy of controls" — Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Control → Administrative → PPE — is a favourite KTU 2-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Source–Path–Receiver Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[NOISE SOURCES]:::src --> B[TRANSMISSION PATH]:::path
    B --> C[RECEIVER HUMANS ECOSYSTEM]:::rcv
    A -. Control 1 .-> A1[Source Control: dampers mufflers isolation]:::ctrl
    B -. Control 2 .-> B1[Path Control: barriers enclosures absorption]:::ctrl
    C -. Control 3 .-> C1[Receiver Control: PPE ear muffs rotation]:::ctrl

    classDef src fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#b30000,color:#000
    classDef path fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b38b00,color:#000
    classDef rcv fill:#d6e6ff,stroke:#003380,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#1a661a,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Hierarchy of Noise Control Measures

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Hierarchy of Noise Control]:::top --> L1[1 ELIMINATION]:::lvl1
    L0 --> L2[2 SUBSTITUTION]:::lvl2
    L0 --> L3[3 ENGINEERING CONTROLS]:::lvl3
    L0 --> L4[4 ADMINISTRATIVE CONTROLS]:::lvl4
    L0 --> L5[5 PPE LAST RESORT]:::lvl5

    L1 --> L1a[Remove process entirely]
    L2 --> L2a[Replace with quieter equipment]
    L3 --> L3a[Source modification, vibration isolation, mufflers]
    L3 --> L3b[Barriers, enclosures, absorptive lining]
    L4 --> L4a[Shift rosters, limit exposure duration]
    L5 --> L5a[Ear plugs, ear muffs, helmets]

    classDef top fill:#222,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef lvl1 fill:#ff8080,stroke:#800,color:#000
    classDef lvl2 fill:#ffb380,stroke:#a40,color:#000
    classDef lvl3 fill:#ffe680,stroke:#a80,color:#000
    classDef lvl4 fill:#80d6ff,stroke:#048,color:#000
    classDef lvl5 fill:#b380ff,stroke:#404,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Sources, Effects and Controls — Cross-Walked Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC[NOISE SOURCES]
        S1[Industrial Machines]
        S2[Vehicular Traffic]
        S3[Aircraft Overflights]
        S4[Construction Activities]
        S5[Domestic Appliances]
        S6[Social and Religious Events]
    end

    subgraph EFF[EFFECTS ON PEOPLE]
        E1[Hearing Loss Temporary Permanent]
        E2[Cardiovascular Hypertension]
        E3[Sleep Disturbance]
        E4[Cognitive Impairment in Children]
        E5[Reduced Productivity]
        E6[Mental Stress Anxiety]
    end

    subgraph CTR[CONTROL METHODS]
        C1[Source: Quiet Equipment Vibration Damping]
        C2[Path: Acoustic Barriers Enclosures Silencers]
        C3[Receiver: Ear Protection Administrative Limits]
        C4[Zoning Land Use Planning]
        C5[Building Insulation Double Glazing]
    end

    S1 --> E1
    S1 --> E2
    S2 --> E3
    S3 --> E1
    S3 --> E4
    S4 --> E5
    S5 --> E6
    S6 --> E3

    E1 --> C1
    E2 --> C1
    E3 --> C2
    E4 --> C4
    E5 --> C3
    E6 --> C3
    E1 --> C5
```

### 4.4 Sound-Level Decision Matrix (Quick Reference)

| $L_{eq}$ (dBA) | Permissible Exposure Time (OSHA) | CPCB Ambient Standard (India) |
|---|---|---|
| 75 | Industrial – full shift | Industrial zone |
| 55 | — | Residential daytime |
| 40 | — | Silence zone (hospitals) |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define noise pollution. List any four major sources of community noise.**
**Model Answer:**
Noise pollution is the presence of unwanted sound in the environment at levels and durations that cause adverse health effects or discomfort to humans and other organisms. It is measured on a logarithmic decibel scale referenced to 20 µPa.
Four major sources are:
1. **Transportation noise** — road, rail, and air traffic.
2. **Industrial noise** — machinery, compressors, DG sets, boilers.
3. **Construction noise** — pile driving, concrete mixing, demolition.
4. **Neighbourhood/domestic noise** — loudspeakers, household appliances, social events.

**Q2. State the OSHA permissible exposure limit for occupational noise and the rule-of-thumb exchange rate.**
**Model Answer:**
OSHA's permissible exposure limit (PEL) is **90 dBA for 8 hours per day**. The exchange (or doubling) rate is **5 dB**, meaning every 5 dB increase halves the permissible exposure time (e.g., 95 dBA ⇒ 4 h, 100 dBA ⇒ 2 h). The action level, at which hearing protection is mandatory, is **85 dBA (8-hr TWA)**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain the physiological and psychological effects of noise pollution on human beings. (7 marks)**
**(b) Two machines produce sound pressure levels of 88 dBA and 86 dBA respectively at a point. If a third machine producing 83 dBA is started, determine the new total sound pressure level. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Physiological and Psychological Effects (7 marks):**
- **Auditory effects:** Temporary Threshold Shift (TTS) after brief exposure; Permanent Threshold Shift (PTS) and sensorineural hearing loss after chronic exposure above 85 dBA; tinnitus (ringing in the ears).
- **Cardiovascular effects:** Noise triggers release of stress hormones (cortisol, adrenaline) leading to vasoconstriction, elevated blood pressure, increased heart rate, and a higher long-term risk of hypertension and ischemic heart disease.
- **Sleep and rest:** Disruption of sleep architecture reduces REM and deep-sleep stages, leading to fatigue, irritability, and reduced immunity.
- **Endocrine and metabolic:** Chronic noise stress elevates blood glucose and cholesterol.
- **Pregnancy and children:** High exposure linked to low birth weight and impaired cognitive development in children.
- **Psychological effects:** Annoyance, anxiety, aggression, reduced concentration, and decreased work productivity.
- **Severe exposure (>120 dB):** Immediate pain, acoustic trauma, and possible tympanic-membrane rupture.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**
- Step 1: Combine 88 dBA and 86 dBA first. $\Delta L = 2$ dB $\Rightarrow$ add 2 dB to the higher.
$$L_{12} = 88 + 2 = 90 \text{ dBA}$$
- Step 2: Now combine 90 dBA (combined) with 83 dBA. $\Delta L = 90 - 83 = 7$ dB $\Rightarrow$ add 1 dB to the higher.
$$L_{tot} = 90 + 1 = 91 \text{ dBA}$$
- **Verification using master equation:**
$$L_{tot} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{8.8} + 10^{8.6} + 10^{8.3}\right)$$
$$= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(6.31 \times 10^8 + 3.98 \times 10^8 + 2.00 \times 10^8\right)$$
$$= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(1.229 \times 10^9\right) = 90.9 \approx 91 \text{ dBA} \;\checkmark$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, describe the three principal methods of engineering control of noise pollution. (7 marks)**
**(b) A construction site has an equivalent noise level of 78 dBA at 50 m. Assuming hemispherical spreading, estimate the SPL at 200 m and at 1 km. Comment on whether residential housing is permissible at 200 m. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Methods of Engineering Control (7 marks):**

1. **Source Control** — Quieter equipment selection, vibration isolation mounts, dynamic balancing, lubrication, replacement of impact processes with rotary ones, use of resilient mountings for motors.
2. **Path Control** — Acoustic enclosures around machinery, noise barriers between source and receiver, absorption lining inside enclosures, mufflers/silencers on exhausts, increased distance (inverse-square law), and green belts (trees give ~3–5 dB attenuation per 30 m dense foliage).
3. **Receiver Control** — Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) such as ear plugs (NRR 25–30 dB) and ear muffs (NRR 20–30 dB), administrative controls limiting exposure duration, and hearing-conservation programmes.

**Block diagram** (text-rendered, refer to §4.1):
`[SOURCE] → [Vibration Isolator / Silencer] → [Barrier / Enclosure / Green Belt] → [Receiver with PPE]`

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**
- Step 1 — Using the hemispherical spreading equation for outdoor ground-level source:
$$L_{p}(r) = L_{p}(r_1) - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r}{r_1}\right)$$
- Step 2 — At $r = 200$ m:
$$L_{p}(200) = 78 - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{200}{50}\right) = 78 - 20 \log_{10}(4) = 78 - 20 \times 0.602 = 78 - 12.04$$
$$L_{p}(200) \approx 65.96 \approx 66 \text{ dBA}$$
- Step 3 — At $r = 1$ km = 1000 m:
$$L_{p}(1000) = 78 - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1000}{50}\right) = 78 - 20 \log_{10}(20) = 78 - 20 \times 1.301 = 78 - 26.02$$
$$L_{p}(1000) \approx 51.98 \approx 52 \text{ dBA}$$
- Step 4 — **Comment:** CPCB ambient noise standard for **residential area during daytime is 55 dBA** and during night is 45 dBA. At 200 m the predicted level is ≈ 66 dBA, which **exceeds** the residential standard. Hence **residential housing is not permissible at 200 m** without additional noise-control measures (barriers, green belt, building insulation).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. Forgetting to use **20 log** (not 10 log) for SPL and distance calculations.
> 2. Adding decibels arithmetically (e.g., 80 + 85 = 165) — dB values **never** add directly.
> 3. Using **10 log** for a pressure ratio (you must square first or use 20 log).
> 4. Confusing the **A-weighted** dBA (community) with the unweighted dB (machine test) standards.
> 5. Mixing up **free-field (–6 dB doubling)** and **hemispherical (no +3 dB correction; direct 20 log)** formulas.

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Definition:** Noise pollution = unwanted sound; measured in **dB (SPL)** or **dBA (A-weighted)**.
- **Reference pressure:** $p_0 = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$.
- **Key formulas:** $L_p = 20\log(p/p_0)$, $L_W = 10\log(W/W_0)$, $L_{tot} = 10\log\!\sum 10^{L_i/10}$.
- **Two-source rule of thumb:** 0/1→+3, 2/3→+2, 4–9→+1, ≥10→+0 dB.
- **Inverse-square law:** $-6$ dB per doubling of distance in free field; **-20 log** for arbitrary ratio.
- **Equivalent level $L_{eq}$:** single value representing variable noise over a period.
- **LNP (Noise Pollution Level):** $L_{eq} + K_n \sigma$ — adds annoyance from variability.
- **$L_{dn}$ (Day-Night Level):** night-time (22:00–07:00) gets a **+10 dB** penalty.
- **OSHA limits:** 90 dBA (8 h) with **5 dB** exchange rate; action level **85 dBA**.
- **CPCB limits (India):** Industrial 75/70, Commercial 65/55, Residential 55/45, Silence 50/40 dBA (day/night).
- **Hierarchy of Controls:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- **Control triad:** Source (damping, isolation) | Path (barrier, enclosure, muffler, green belt) | Receiver (PPE, rotation).
- **Health effects:** hearing loss (TTS/PTS), hypertension, sleep loss, cognitive impairment, anxiety, reduced productivity.
- **High-frequency hearing loss begins around 4 kHz**; A-weighting de-emphasizes low frequencies to match human perception.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU viva:** "**D-D-F-A-H**" — **D**ecibel, **D**istance (inverse-square), **F**requency (A-weighting), **A**ddition rule, **H**ierarchy of controls. Recite this in 30 seconds and you will cover 80% of the viva panel's questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air pollution: units of measurement, sources, classification, sampling and analysis of air pollutants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT613 - PROCESS DESIGN FOR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Air pollution: units of measurement, sources, classification, sampling and analysis of air pollutants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Air Pollution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air Pollution (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> *Air pollution is defined as the presence of one or more contaminants in the atmosphere in quantities, characteristics, and duration such as to be injurious to human, plant, or animal life, or to property, or which unreasonably interferes with the comfortable enjoyment of life or property or with the conduct of business.* — Adapted from the **U.S. Clean Air Act, 1970** and adopted under the **Indian Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981**.

In engineering terms, air pollution is the **contamination of the atmospheric air** by physical, chemical, or biological agents that alter its natural composition and cause adverse effects on living systems and the built environment.

## 1.2 The Concept of "Contaminant" vs "Pollutant"

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Contaminant** | Any substance present in air that was not originally part of its natural composition |
| **Pollutant** | A contaminant that causes measurable harmful effect (toxic, irritating, or damaging) |
| **Precursor** | A substance (e.g., NOₓ, VOCs) that reacts in the atmosphere to form a secondary pollutant (e.g., O₃) |

> [!NOTE]
> The atmosphere is **never pure** — even the cleanest marine air contains trace CO₂ (~415 ppm), argon, neon, dust, and sea-salt aerosols. Pollution is therefore a *threshold-based* concept.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Cafeteria Soup Bowl"

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — Imagine the atmosphere as a giant cafeteria soup bowl 🍲**
> The "soup base" is clean dry air (78% N₂ + 21% O₂ + 1% Ar + traces). When the chef (industry, vehicles, volcanoes, fires) accidentally drops in unwanted ingredients — *salt* (SO₂), *chili* (NO₂), *smoke particles* (PM), and *stale fish smell* (VOCs) — even a *pinch* changes the flavor dramatically. A pinch of chili in 1000 litres of soup is exactly what **ppm** measures! Too much chili and the soup becomes inedible (= respiratory illness). The chef's duty is to keep the added ingredients below the *taste threshold* — that threshold is the **air quality standard**.

## 1.4 Units of Measurement — The Language of Air Pollution

Air pollutants exist in **tiny quantities**, so we cannot use % or mg/L directly. Engineers use three families of units:

### (a) Volume Mixing Ratio Units — *Ratio, dimensionless*

$$\text{ppm} = \frac{\text{Volume of pollutant}}{\text{Total volume of air sample}} \times 10^{6}$$

$$\text{ppb} = \frac{\text{Volume of pollutant}}{\text{Total volume of air sample}} \times 10^{9}$$

$$\% = \frac{\text{Volume of pollutant}}{\text{Total volume of air sample}} \times 100$$

> **Conversion**: $1\% = 10{,}000 \text{ ppm} = 10^7 \text{ ppb}$

### (b) Mass Concentration Units — *µg/m³, mg/m³, ng/m³*

These measure the actual *mass* of pollutant per cubic metre of air and are the units used by **CPCB / WHO / US-EPA** for ambient air quality reporting.

### (c) Emission Rate Units — *Used for stacks/industrial sources*

$$\text{kg/hr}, \quad \text{g/s}, \quad \text{t/day}, \quad \text{Nm}^3/\text{hr}$$

where **Nm³** = cubic metre at **Normal conditions (0 °C, 1 atm)**.

## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in Pollution Control Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Module 3 Learning Outcome:**
> *After this module, the student should be able to identify, quantify, and characterize air pollutants using standardized units, classify them based on origin and chemistry, and design/select appropriate sampling and analytical protocols consistent with CPCB and USEPA methods.*

Every pollution-control design — whether a cyclone, scrubber, ESP, or catalytic converter — begins with a **quantitative knowledge** of *what* is in the air, *how much*, and *where*. Without the units and sampling principles taught here, no abatement device can be sized correctly.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Air Pollution

Air pollution sources are classified into **two super-groups**:

### 2.1.1 Natural Sources

| Source | Major Pollutants Released |
|---|---|
| Volcanic eruptions | SO₂, H₂S, CO₂, dust (PM₁₀) |
| Forest / bush fires | CO, CO₂, NOₓ, PM, VOCs |
| Dust storms / sandstorms | Suspended particulate matter (PM₁₀, PM₂.₅) |
| Sea-spray aerosols | NaCl, MgCl₂ (marine particulates) |
| Biogenic (plants, soil) | Terpenes, isoprene, pollen, methane (CH₄) |
| Lightning | NOₓ (high-temperature fixation of N₂) |

### 2.1.2 Anthropogenic (Man-Made) Sources — *Engineering-Relevant*

> [!NOTE]
> For pollution-control design, **anthropogenic sources** are further classified as:
>
> 1. **Stationary (Point) Sources** — single identifiable outlet (e.g., power plant stack, refinery vent)
> 2. **Mobile Sources** — moving emitters (cars, aircraft, ships)
> 3. **Area Sources** — diffuse, collective emissions (city roads, agricultural fields, domestic cooking)
> 4. **Line Sources** — long narrow emitters (highways, runways)

**Typical industrial source inventory:**

| Industry | Dominant Pollutants |
|---|---|
| Thermal power plants | SO₂, NOₓ, fly ash, Hg, CO₂ |
| Cement plants | Particulate matter, CO₂ |
| Iron & steel | PM, CO, SO₂, fluorides, VOCs |
| Petrochemical refineries | VOCs, H₂S, SO₂, hydrocarbons |
| Pulp & paper | H₂S, mercaptans, VOCs |
| Fertilizer | NH₃, NOₓ, fluorides |

## 2.2 Classification of Air Pollutants

### 2.2.1 Classification by Formation Mechanism

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Pollutants** — emitted *directly* from a source in a harmful form (CO, SO₂, NO, PM, VOCs).
> **Secondary Pollutants** — formed in the atmosphere by *chemical reactions* between primary pollutants and normal atmospheric constituents (O₃, PAN, H₂SO₄, HNO₃, smog).

**Key secondary-pollutant formation reactions:**

$$2\,\text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{NO}_2 \quad \text{(oxidation of NO)}$$

$$\text{NO}_2 + h\nu (\lambda < 400 \text{ nm}) \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}\cdot$$

$$\text{O}\cdot + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{O}_3 + \text{M}$$

$$2\,\text{SO}_2 + \text{O}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\,\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \quad \text{(acid rain)}$$

### 2.2.2 Classification by Physical State

> **Gaseous Pollutants** — SO₂, NOₓ, CO, CO₂, O₃, VOCs, H₂S, NH₃, HCl, HF, Cl₂
>
> **Particulate Pollutants (PM)** — dust, smoke, fumes, mist, spray, fog, fly ash
>
> > - **PM₁₀** — particles ≤ 10 µm aerodynamic diameter (thoracic)
> > - **PM₂.₅** — particles ≤ 2.5 µm (respirable; alveoli-penetrating)
> > - **Ultrafine (PM₀.₁)** — ≤ 100 nm

### 2.2.3 Classification by Chemical Nature

> **Organic** — hydrocarbons, aldehydes, ketones, PAHs, dioxins
>
> **Inorganic** — SO₂, NOₓ, CO, CO₂, NH₃, H₂S, acids
>
> **Biological** — pollen, spores, bacteria, viruses

### 2.2.4 The "Criteria Pollutants" (Regulatory Classification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Under the Indian National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS, 2009)** and **USEPA**, six **criteria pollutants** are regulated by mass/limit value:
> 1. Particulate Matter (PM₂.₅ and PM₁₀)
> 2. Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂)
> 3. Nitrogen Dioxide (NO₂)
> 4. Carbon Monoxide (CO)
> 5. Ozone (O₃)
> 6. Lead (Pb)
>
> Plus **additional parameters** monitored in India: NH₃, CO, O₃, Benzene, BaP, As, Ni.

### 2.2.5 Hazardous Air Pollutants (HAPs) — *Air Toxics*

**189 listed compounds** under USEPA, including benzene, formaldehyde, vinyl chloride, mercury, asbestos, dioxins, PAHs.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **most-tested formulas** in KTU Process Design for Pollution Control exams. Memorize the units carefully.

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| **F1** | $C_{\mu g/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{M_w}{24.45} \times \dfrac{298}{T(K)} \times \dfrac{P}{1\text{ atm}}$ | **Conversion ppm → µg/m³** at any T,P |
| **F2** | $C_{ppm} = C_{\mu g/m^3} \times \dfrac{24.45}{M_w}$ (at 25 °C, 1 atm) | **Conversion µg/m³ → ppm** (standard) |
| **F3** | $\dfrac{C_1}{C_2} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}$ (at constant P) | **Charles's Law** (volume-mixing ratios depend only on mole fraction) |
| **F4** | $\rho_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{P \cdot M_w}{R \cdot T}$ | Air density (kg/m³) at given T,P |
| **F5** | $V_{\text{std}} = V_{\text{meas}} \times \dfrac{P_{\text{meas}}}{P_{\text{std}}} \times \dfrac{T_{\text{std}}}{T_{\text{meas}}}$ | Stack-gas volume correction to STP |
| **F6** | $V_s = K_p \cdot C_p \cdot \sqrt{\Delta H}$ | **Pitot-tube velocity** in stack (m/s) |
| **F7** | $V_{\text{stack}} = A_{\text{stack}} \cdot v_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot v_s$ | Stack gas volumetric flow (m³/s) |
| **F8** | $I = I_p \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{T_s \, P_m}{T_m \, P_s}}$ | **Isokinetic sampling** correction factor |
| **F9** | $\text{AQI} = \dfrac{I_{\text{hi}} - I_{\text{lo}}}{\text{BP}_{\text{hi}} - \text{BP}_{\text{lo}}} (C - \text{BP}_{\text{lo}}) + I_{\text{lo}}$ | **Indian National AQI** (linear interpolation) |
| **F10** | $\text{PM}_{10}\ \text{in}\ \mu g/m^3 = \dfrac{W_f - W_i}{V_{\text{air}}} \times 10^6$ | Gravimetric PM concentration (µg/m³) |
| **F11** | $\eta_{\text{emis}} = \dfrac{\text{mass emitted}}{\text{mass of fuel burned}} \times 100\%$ | **Emission factor / efficiency** (%) |
| **F12** | $\bar{C} = \dfrac{\sum Q_i \, C_i}{\sum Q_i}$ | **Stack-gas mixing** weighted average |

where
$M_w$ = molecular weight of pollutant (g/mol), $T(K)$ = absolute temperature, $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**, $K_p$ = Pitot-tube constant (typically 0.84), $C_p$ = pressure coefficient, $\Delta H$ = velocity head, $A_{\text{stack}}$ = stack cross-sectional area, $D$ = stack diameter, BP = break-point concentration, $I$ = AQI sub-index.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These units/formulas underpin **every regulatory compliance test**:
- **Stack monitoring reports** to CPCB/SPCB use F5–F8.
- **Ambient air quality reports** (CAAQMS stations) use F1–F2 and F9.
- **Emission inventory** for an EIA report uses F11–F12.
- **Design of control equipment** (cyclone, ESP, bag filter, scrubber) requires the **inlet mass loading** in g/m³ — obtained from F10.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivations below are **board-favourite questions** for KTU examinations. Practice each step until you can write it on paper without referring to notes.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Conversion between ppm (by volume) and µg/m³

**Starting Definition (volumetric ppm):**

$$\text{ppm}_v = \frac{V_{\text{pollutant}}}{V_{\text{total}}} \times 10^6$$

**Step 1 — Apply the Ideal Gas Law** to convert volumes to moles.

At the same T and P, the volume ratio = mole ratio:

$$\text{ppm}_v = \frac{n_{\text{poll}}}{n_{\text{total}}} \times 10^6$$

**Step 2 — Express mass of pollutant** using $m = n \cdot M_w$:

$$n_{\text{poll}} = \frac{m_{\text{poll}}}{M_w}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute moles per cubic metre** in terms of concentration in µg/m³. Take $V_{\text{total}} = 1 \text{ m}^3$ and $m_{\text{poll}}$ in micrograms:

$$C_{\mu g/m^3} = \frac{m_{\text{poll}}}{V_{\text{total}}}$$

$$\therefore \text{ppm}_v = \frac{m_{\text{poll}} / M_w}{n_{\text{total}}} \times 10^6$$

**Step 4 — Compute $n_{\text{total}}$ per m³ of air at STP (25 °C, 1 atm)**:

$$n_{\text{total}} = \frac{P \cdot V}{R \cdot T} = \frac{101325 \times 1}{8.314 \times 298.15} = 40.87 \text{ mol/m}^3$$

> **The molar volume of air at 25 °C, 1 atm is 24.47 L/mol = 0.02447 m³/mol**, so 1 m³ of air contains $1 / 0.02447 = 40.87$ moles.

**Step 5 — Combine:**

$$C_{\mu g/m^3} = \text{ppm}_v \times M_w \times n_{\text{total}}$$

$$\boxed{C_{\mu g/m^3} = \text{ppm}_v \times M_w \times 40.87}$$

For temperature/pressure corrections:

$$C_{\mu g/m^3} = \text{ppm}_v \times \frac{M_w}{24.45} \times \frac{298}{T(K)} \times \frac{P}{1\text{ atm}}$$

**Worked Numerical Example 1:**

> *The 8-hour average CO concentration in a city is 9 ppm. Express it in mg/m³ at 30 °C and 1 atm. (M_w of CO = 28 g/mol)*

**Step 1:** Insert into formula F1.

$$C_{\mu g/m^3} = 9 \times \frac{28}{24.45} \times \frac{298}{303} \times 1$$

$$= 9 \times 1.1453 \times 0.9835 = 10.14\ \mu g/m^3$$

Hmm — this is too low for CO. The correct reading is that ambient CO standards are in **mg/m³**. Let me redo with correct scale.

**Correction:** 9 ppm is too high for ambient CO (standard is ~4 ppm for 8-hr). Re-do example with realistic value:

> *Ambient SO₂ reading = 80 µg/m³. Convert to ppb. (M_w SO₂ = 64 g/mol)*

**Step 1:** Apply F2 at 25 °C, 1 atm:

$$C_{ppm} = \frac{80}{1} \times \frac{24.45}{64} = 80 \times 0.3820 = 30.56\ \text{ppm}$$

This is too high — the correct ambient SO₂ is around 30 ppb, not 30 ppm. **Scale error identified** — µg/m³ of SO₂ should map to ppb, not ppm:

$$\text{ppb} = \frac{C_{\mu g/m^3} \times 24.45}{M_w} = \frac{80 \times 24.45}{64} = 30.56\ \text{ppb} \;\;\checkmark$$

> **Correct final boxed result:**
> $$C_{\text{ppb}} = \frac{C_{\mu g/m^3} \times 24.45}{M_w}$$
> $$\boxed{C_{\text{ppb}} = \frac{80 \times 24.45}{64} = 30.56\ \text{ppb}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Indian National AQI Calculation (Step-by-Step)

The **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** AQI follows the US-EPA linear-interpolation method. Each pollutant $p$ has a sub-index $I_p$ computed from its measured concentration $C_p$.

**Step 1 — Identify the pollutant concentration** $C_p$ (e.g., PM₂.₅ = 78 µg/m³).

**Step 2 — Look up the break-point table** (CPCB). For PM₂.₅:

| $I_{\text{lo}}$ | $I_{\text{hi}}$ | $\text{BP}_{\text{lo}}$ | $\text{BP}_{\text{hi}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 50 | 0 | 30 |
| 51 | 100 | 31 | 60 |
| 101 | 200 | 61 | 90 |
| 201 | 300 | 91 | 120 |

For $C_p = 78 \Rightarrow$ it falls in 101–200 band: $I_{\text{lo}}=101$, $I_{\text{hi}}=200$, $\text{BP}_{\text{lo}}=61$, $\text{BP}_{\text{hi}}=90$.

**Step 3 — Apply interpolation formula F9:**

$$I_p = \frac{I_{\text{hi}} - I_{\text{lo}}}{\text{BP}_{\text{hi}} - \text{BP}_{\text{lo}}} \times (C_p - \text{BP}_{\text{lo}}) + I_{\text{lo}}$$

$$I_{PM_{2.5}} = \frac{200 - 101}{90 - 61} \times (78 - 61) + 101$$

$$= \frac{99}{29} \times 17 + 101 = 3.4138 \times 17 + 101$$

$$= 58.03 + 101 = 159.03$$

**Step 4 — Compute sub-indices for ALL criteria pollutants** present in monitoring data (PM₂.₅, PM₁₀, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, NH₃, Pb).

**Step 5 — The overall AQI = MAX of all sub-indices** (the dominant pollutant governs).

$$\boxed{\text{AQI} = \max\left(I_{PM_{2.5}},\ I_{PM_{10}},\ I_{SO_2},\ I_{NO_2},\ I_{CO},\ I_{O_3},\ I_{NH_3},\ I_{Pb}\right)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Isokinetic Sampling Velocity Correction

**Physical Principle:**
When sampling particulates from a stack, the **probe inlet velocity must exactly equal the local gas stream-line velocity**; otherwise, particulates (due to inertia) deviate from the gas path and the sample becomes non-representative.

> *Isokinetic condition* → $v_{\text{nozzle}} = v_{\text{stack, local}}$

**Step 1 — Probe nozzle velocity:**

$$v_n = \frac{Q_n}{A_n} = \frac{4 Q_n}{\pi d_n^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Local stack velocity** at sampling point (using Pitot tube F6):

$$v_s = K_p C_p \sqrt{2 g \, \Delta H \, \frac{T_s \, \rho_{\text{air},m}}{T_m \, \rho_{\text{air},s}}}$$

**Step 3 — Correction factor for non-standard T,P:**

$$I = I_p \sqrt{\frac{T_s P_m}{T_m P_s}}$$

For *iso*-kinetic condition, $I = 1$.

**Step 4 — Final relationship for sampling flow rate:**

$$Q_n = A_n \cdot v_s \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{(T_s P_m)/(T_m P_s)}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Stack Gas Velocity Using a Pitot Tube

The **velocity head** $\Delta H$ is measured by a Type-S Pitot tube:

$$\Delta H = H_{\text{dynamic}} = \frac{v_s^{2}}{2 g} \cdot \frac{\rho_{\text{gas}}}{\rho_{\text{air}}}$$

Solving for $v_s$:

$$\boxed{v_s = K_p \, C_p \, \sqrt{2 g \, \Delta H_{\text{water}} \, \frac{\rho_{\text{water}}}{\rho_{\text{gas}}}}}$$

**Worked Numerical Example 2:**

> *A Type-S Pitot tube gives $\Delta H = 12.7$ mm of water at a stack. Stack gas temp = 180 °C, gas molecular weight = 30 g/mol. Calculate stack gas velocity at the point.*

**Step 1 — Air density at stack conditions:**

$$\rho_{\text{gas}} = \frac{P M_w}{R T} = \frac{101325 \times 0.030}{8.314 \times 453} = 0.806\ kg/m^3$$

**Step 2 — Water density ≈ 1000 kg/m³, $K_p C_p = 0.84$ for Type-S:**

$$v_s = 0.84 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.0127 \times \frac{1000}{0.806}}$$

$$= 0.84 \times \sqrt{0.2492 \times 1240.7} = 0.84 \times \sqrt{309.2}$$

$$= 0.84 \times 17.58 = 14.77\ m/s$$

$$\boxed{v_s \approx 14.77 \text{ m/s}}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing Sampling & Analytical Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a **ready-reckoner** for selecting the right instrument per pollutant.

| Pollutant | Sampling Method | Typical Analyser / Method | Detection Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| SO₂ | Impinger with **Sodium tetrachloromercurate (TCM)** | West-Gaeke **spectrophotometry (p-Rosaniline)** | 5 µg/m³ |
| NOₓ | Impinger with **Sodium hydroxide + Sodium arsenite** | **Modified Griess–Saltzman** spectrophotometry | 5 µg/m³ |
| CO | Direct-reading **NDIR** analyser | Non-dispersive infrared | 0.5 ppm |
| O₃ | Direct-reading **UV photometric** analyser | UV absorption at 254 nm | 5 ppb |
| PM₁₀ | **High-volume air sampler** (1–1.5 m³/min) | Gravimetric (filter weighing) | 1 µg/m³ |
| PM₂.₅ | **EPA Federal Reference Method** impactor | Gravimetric | 1 µg/m³ |
| Pb | Low-volume sampler on **Teflon filter** | ICP-MS or AAS | 0.05 µg/m³ |
| VOCs | **Summa canister / Tedlar bag** | GC-MS / GC-FID | 1 ppb |
| NH₃ | Impinger with **dilute H₂SO₄** | Indophenol blue spectrophotometry | 2 µg/m³ |

## 3.6 Sampling Train — Component-by-Component Flow

The **US-EPA Method 5 / Method 17 stack-sampling train** consists of:

$$\text{Nozzle} \rightarrow \text{Pitot tube} \rightarrow \text{Heated probe} \rightarrow \text{Filter holder (PM capture)} \rightarrow \text{Condenser} \rightarrow \text{Impingers (gas absorption)} \rightarrow \text{Drying tube} \rightarrow \text{Meter box} \rightarrow \text{Pump}$$

Each component has a **specific function** to capture particulates and gases separately for accurate mass-balance reporting.
