## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks each)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper]* ⭐

**(a)** Describe the **classification of air pollutants** based on (i) formation mechanism, (ii) physical state, and (iii) chemical nature. Give at least two examples under each category. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A CPCB monitoring station reports the following 24-hr average concentrations:
- PM₂.₅ = 65 µg/m³
- PM₁₀ = 110 µg/m³
- SO₂ = 35 µg/m³
- NO₂ = 50 µg/m³
- CO = 2.5 mg/m³
- O₃ = 80 µg/m³

**Calculate the National AQI** and identify the dominant pollutant. **Use standard CPCB break-points.** **[7 marks]**

#### **Model Solution to A(a) — 7 marks**

> **Formation-based classification (2 marks)**
> - Primary: emitted directly (CO, SO₂, NO, PM, VOCs)
> - Secondary: formed in atmosphere (O₃, PAN, H₂SO₄, NO₂)
>
> **Physical state classification (2 marks)**
> - Gaseous: SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, NH₃
> - Particulate (PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, ultrafine), aerosols, fumes, mist
>
> **Chemical classification (2 marks)**
> - Organic: VOCs, PAHs, aldehydes
> - Inorganic: SO₂, NOₓ, CO, H₂S
>
> **Conclusion with significance (1 mark)**
> Knowledge of classification guides control strategy and standard selection.

#### **Model Solution to A(b) — 7 marks**

> **Step 1 — Use CPCB break-point table for each pollutant and compute sub-index using F9:**
>
> | Pollutant | Conc. | BP range | $I_{\text{lo}}$ | $I_{\text{hi}}$ | BP_lo | BP_hi | Sub-index $I_p$ |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | PM₂.₅ | 65 | 61–90 | 101 | 200 | 61 | 90 | $\frac{99}{29}\times4+101 = 114.66$ |
> | PM₁₀ | 110 | 101–250 | 101 | 200 | 101 | 250 | $\frac{99}{149}\times9+101 = 106.98$ |
> | SO₂ | 35 | 31–60 | 51 | 100 | 31 | 60 | $\frac{49}{29}\times4+51 = 57.76$ |
> | NO₂ | 50 | 41–80 | 51 | 100 | 41 | 80 | $\frac{49}{39}\times9+51 = 62.31$ |
> | CO | 2.5 | 2.1–10 mg/m³ | 51 | 100 | 2.1 | 10 | $\frac{49}{7.9}\times0.4+51 = 53.48$ |
> | O₃ | 80 | 58–108 | 51 | 100 | 58 | 108 | $\frac{49}{50}\times22+51 = 72.56$ |
>
> **Step 2 — Overall AQI = Maximum sub-index** = **114.66 (PM₂.₅)**.
>
> **Step 3 — AQI Category:** Since 101–200 = **"Poor"** (CPCB category), the dominant pollutant is **PM₂.₅**.
>
> **Conclusion:** The city falls in the "Poor" AQI band; immediate PM₂.₅ mitigation measures are required.

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]* ⭐

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, describe the **EPA Method 5 stack-sampling train** for the determination of particulate matter emissions. List the function of each component. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 1.5 m diameter stack at a cement plant has a flue gas temperature of 180 °C, pressure 1 atm, and the gas composition (by mole) is 12% CO₂, 6% H₂O, 4% O₂, and 78% N₂. The average velocity measured by a Type-S Pitot tube is 14 m/s. Calculate the **actual volumetric flow rate (m³/s)**, the **density of flue gas**, and the **mass flow rate (kg/s)**, assuming ideal-gas behaviour. **[7 marks]**

#### **Model Solution to B(a) — 7 marks**

> **Diagram (4 marks)** — draw labelled schematic of the EPA Method 5 train as in Section 4.2.
>
> **Component functions (3 marks — ½ mark each):**
> 1. **Nozzle** — provides isokinetic sampling entry.
> 2. **Type-S Pitot tube** — measures velocity pressure (ΔH).
> 3. **Heated probe** (~120 °C) — prevents condensation of moisture/sulphuric acid mist.
> 4. **Filter holder + glass-fibre filter** — captures particulate matter for gravimetric analysis.
> 5. **Condenser / impinger train** — absorbs gaseous pollutants (e.g., SO₂ in H₂O₂, HCl in water).
> 6. **Drying tube + silica gel** — removes residual moisture before metering.
> 7. **Meter box** — measures total gas volume sampled.
> 8. **Vacuum pump** — draws sample through the train.

#### **Model Solution to B(b) — 7 marks**

> **Step 1 — Average molecular weight of flue gas (1.5 marks):**
>
> $$M_w = 0.12(44) + 0.06(18) + 0.04(32) + 0.78(28)$$
> $$= 5.28 + 1.08 + 1.28 + 21.84 = 29.48\ g/mol = 0.02948\ kg/mol$$
>
> **Step 2 — Density of flue gas (1.5 marks):**
>
> $$T = 180 + 273 = 453\ K,\quad P = 101325\ Pa$$
> $$\rho_g = \frac{P M_w}{R T} = \frac{101325 \times 0.02948}{8.314 \times 453} = 0.793\ kg/m^3$$
>
> **Step 3 — Cross-sectional area of stack (1 mark):**
>
> $$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(1.5)^2 = 1.767\ m^2$$
>
> **Step 4 — Volumetric flow rate at stack conditions (1.5 marks):**
>
> $$Q_{\text{actual}} = A \cdot v_s = 1.767 \times 14 = 24.74\ m^3/s$$
>
> **Step 5 — Mass flow rate (1.5 marks):**
>
> $$\dot{m} = \rho_g \cdot Q = 0.793 \times 24.74 = 19.62\ kg/s$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — DO NOT LOSE THESE MARKS:**
>
> 1. **Unit errors** in ppm ↔ µg/m³ conversion: most students forget the factor **24.45** — write it on the formula sheet and use it.
> 2. **Temperature must be in Kelvin** (°C + 273). Converting incorrectly gives wrong density and wrong AQI.
> 3. **AQI = MAX, not average** — examiners explicitly deduct if a student writes the average.
> 4. **CPCB break-points are different for CO (mg/m³) vs SO₂ (µg/m³)** — re-check units before interpolation.
> 5. **Pitot tube constant** is **0.84 for Type-S**; using 1.0 loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Isokinetic sampling** is a *must-mention* concept when describing stack sampling.
> 7. **EPA Method 5** requires the filter to be dried & weighed pre/post to ±0.1 mg — state the gravimetric principle.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> ✅ **Air Pollution Basics**
> - Definition: presence of contaminants causing measurable harm (Indian Air Act, 1981).
> - Analogy: atmosphere = cafeteria soup bowl; ppm = "pinch of spice in 1000 L".

> ✅ **Units of Measurement**
> - Volume-ratio: **ppm = 10⁻⁶, ppb = 10⁻⁹, % = 10⁻²**
> - Mass: **µg/m³, mg/m³, ng/m³**
> - Emission: **kg/hr, t/day, Nm³/hr**
> - **Master formula**: $C_{\mu g/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times M_w \times 40.87$ (at 25 °C, 1 atm).

> ✅ **Sources**
> - **Natural**: volcanic, forest fires, dust, biogenic, lightning.
> - **Anthropogenic**: stationary, mobile, area, line.

> ✅ **Classification**
> - Primary vs Secondary
> - Gaseous vs Particulate
> - Organic vs Inorganic
> - Criteria pollutants (PM, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, Pb)
> - Hazardous Air Pollutants (189 listed by USEPA)

> ✅ **Sampling**
> - **Ambient**: High-Vol Sampler (PM₁₀), EPA FRM impactor (PM₂.₅), impinger trains (SO₂, NO₂).
> - **Stack**: EPA Method 5 train (Nozzle → Pitot → Heated Probe → Filter → Condenser → Impingers → Dryer → Meter → Pump).
> - **Isokinetic** sampling mandatory for accurate PM measurement.

> ✅ **Analysis**
> - **Gravimetric** for PM
> - **West-Gaeke** (p-Rosaniline) for SO₂
> - **Griess-Saltzman** for NOₓ
> - **NDIR** for CO
> - **UV Photometric** for O₃
> - **AAS/ICP-MS** for Pb
> - **GC-MS / GC-FID** for VOCs

> ✅ **AQI — Must Memorise**
> - Formula: linear interpolation F9.
> - **AQI = MAX of all sub-indices.**
> - Bands: Good (0–50), Satisfactory (51–100), Moderate (101–200), Poor (201–300), Very Poor (301–400), Severe (401–500).

> ✅ **Key Pitfalls**
> - Always Kelvin for temperature in gas equations.
> - M_w must be in **g/mol** for ppm conversion.
> - Stack gas velocity via Pitot tube: $v_s = 0.84\sqrt{2 g \Delta H \cdot \rho_w / \rho_g}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Health effects of air pollution, global warming, and ozone depletion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT613 - PROCESS DESIGN FOR POLLUTION CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Health effects of air pollution, global warming, and ozone depletion ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Air Pollution — Formal KTU Definition > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** *Air pollution* refers to the presence of one or more contaminants in the atmosphere in quantities and duration that are injurious to human health, animal life, vegetation, or property, or which unreasonably interfere with the comfortable enjoyment of life or the conduct of business. Air pollution is quantified by **concentration (µg/m³ or ppm)**, **duration of exposure**, and the **toxicity index** of each pollutant. The **WHO Global Air Quality Guidelines (2021)** specify that **PM₂.₅ annual mean should not exceed 5 µg/m³** and **PM₁₀ should not exceed 15 µg/m³**. ### 1.2 The Six Classical "Criteria Pollutants" | # | Pollutant | Primary Sources | Key Health Endpoint | |---|-----------|-----------------|---------------------| | 1 | **Particulate Matter (PM₁₀ / PM₂.₅)** | Combustion, dust, vehicular | Deep lung penetration, alveolar damage | | 2 | **Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂)** | Coal/oil combustion, smelters | Bronchoconstriction, asthma | | 3 | **Nitrogen Dioxide (NO₂)** | Vehicular exhaust, power plants | Pulmonary edema, smog formation | | 4 | **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** | Incomplete combustion | Hemoglobin binding (carboxyhemoglobin) | | 5 | **Ground-Level Ozone (O₃)** | Photochemical smog | Lung inflammation, crop damage | | 6 | **Lead (Pb)** | Leaded fuel, smelters | Neurotoxicity in children | ### 1.3 Global Warming — The Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Greenhouse Effect Analogy:** Imagine the Earth wrapped in a *transparent thermal blanket*. Short-wave solar radiation passes through easily, but the *long-wave infrared radiation* emitted by the warm Earth's surface gets *absorbed and re-radiated* by trace gases — analogous to a one-way mirror. The more blanket (GHG concentration), the warmer the interior (troposphere). ### 1.4 Ozone Depletion — The Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stratospheric Ozone — The UV Shield Analogy:** Stratospheric ozone (O₃) at 15–35 km altitude acts as Earth's *sunscreen*, absorbing harmful Ultraviolet-B (UV-B, 280–315 nm) radiation. Chlorine radicals (Cl•) released from CFCs act like *molecular scissors* — one Cl atom can destroy **~100,000 ozone molecules** in a catalytic chain (the *Chapman–Molina cycle*). ### 1.5 Real-World Damage Metrics - **DALY (Disability-Adjusted Life Year):** One lost year of "healthy" life. Used by WHO to quantify disease burden. - **Mortality Rate per 100,000:** Standard epidemiological metric. - **Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP):** Relative destructive capacity vs. CFC-11 (= 1.0). - **Global Warming Potential (GWP):** Relative heat-trapping capacity vs. CO₂ (= 1.0) over a time horizon (typically 100 years). ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Health Effects Pathway Human health impact follows this sequential causal chain: 1. **Emission Source** → releases contaminant 2. **Atmospheric Transport & Transformation** → secondary pollutant formation (e.g., NO₂ + VOC + sunlight → O₃) 3. **Inhalation / Dermal Contact / Ingestion** → dose enters body 4. **Biological Dose at Target Organ** → deposition in alveoli, blood–gas barrier 5. **Biochemical Response** → inflammation, oxidative stress, enzyme inhibition 6. **Clinical Outcome** → asthma, COPD, cancer, death ### 2.2 Dose–Response for Carbon Monoxide The **Coburn–Forster–Kane (CFK) equation** predicts the rate of carboxyhemoglobin (COHb%) formation in blood: $$ \frac{d[COHb]}{dt} = \frac{V_{CO} - \frac{[COHb]\cdot V_{CO}\cdot P_{CO}}{P_{CO} \cdot M}}{V_{b}\cdot Hb} $$ where: - $V_{CO}$ = CO generation rate (mL/min) - $V_b$ = blood volume (mL) - $Hb$ = hemoglobin concentration - $P_{CO}$ = partial pressure of CO - $M$ = Haldane constant ≈ **218** ### 2.3 Global Warming — Radiative Forcing The change in outgoing long-wave radiation per unit change in CO₂ concentration (Myhre et al., 1998): $$ \Delta F = 5.35 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{C}{C_{0}}\right) \quad [\text{W/m}^2] $$ where: - $C$ = current CO₂ concentration (ppm) - $C_0$ = reference concentration (pre-industrial ≈ 280 ppm) - $\Delta F$ = radiative forcing change > [!NOTE] > **Why the logarithm?** CO₂ absorption bands saturate. Doubling the gas does *not* double the warming — only adds another fixed increment. ### 2.4 Stefan–Boltzmann Equilibrium Temperature $$ E = \sigma T^{4} $$ with **σ = 5.670 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴** (Stefan–Boltzmann constant). Equilibrium Earth surface temperature accounting for greenhouse effect: $$ T_{s} = \left(\frac{S(1-\alpha)}{4\sigma(1-\frac{\epsilon}{2})}\right)^{1/4} $$ where: - $S$ = solar constant ≈ **1361 W/m²** - $\alpha$ = albedo ≈ **0.30** - $\epsilon$ = effective emissivity of atmosphere ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Table) | Formula | Expression | Variables & Units | |---------|-----------|-------------------| | Stefan–Boltzmann | $E = \sigma T^{4}$ | E = W/m², σ = 5.67×10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴, T = K | | Radiative Forcing (CO₂) | $\Delta F = 5.35 \cdot \ln(C/C_{0})$ | F = W/m², C in ppm | | GWP | $GWP_{x} = \frac{\int_{0}^{TH} a_{x}\cdot c_{x}(t)\, dt}{\int_{0}^{TH} a_{CO_{2}}\cdot c_{CO_{2}}(t)\, dt}$ | TH = time horizon (yrs), a = radiative efficiency | | ODP | $ODP_{x} = \frac{\text{Cl atoms released by } x}{\text{Cl atoms released by CFC-11}}$ | dimensionless | | CFK equation | $\frac{d[COHb]}{dt} = \frac{V_{CO}}{V_b\cdot Hb}\left[1 - \frac{[COHb]}{100\cdot M}\right]$ | COHb in % | | DALY | $DALY = YLL + YLD$ | Years of life lost + years lived with disability | | Clausius–Clapeyron (water-holding capacity) | $e_{s} = 6.112 \cdot e^{\frac{17.67\,T}{T+243.5}}$ | T in °C, eₛ in hPa | | Climate Sensitivity | $\Delta T_{2\times} = \lambda \cdot \Delta F_{2\times}$ | λ = 0.8 K/(W/m²), ΔF₂ₓ ≈ 3.7 W/m² | ### 2.6 Engineering Utility - **Process Designers** use CFK equation to design ventilation systems in tunnels, parking garages, and steel plants. - **Emission Inventory Engineers** compute CO₂-equivalent emissions as: $E_{CO_{2}-eq} = \sum_i (m_i \cdot GWP_i)$. - **Refrigeration Engineers** select refrigerants based on ODP and GWP — the KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes this in *Green Refrigeration*. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Doubling CO₂ Forcing from Pre-Industrial **Problem:** Calculate the radiative forcing if atmospheric CO₂ rises from **280 ppm (1750)** to **560 ppm (doubled)**. **Step 1 — Identify input variables** $$ C_0 = 280 \text{ ppm}, \quad C = 560 \text{ ppm} $$ **Step 2 — Form ratio** $$ \frac{C}{C_0} = \frac{560}{280} = 2.0 $$ **Step 3 — Apply Myhre logarithmic relationship** $$ \Delta F = 5.35 \cdot \ln(2.0) $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate natural log** $$ \ln(2.0) = 0.6931 $$ **Step 5 — Multiply by 5.35** $$ \Delta F = 5.35 \times 0.6931 = 3.708 \text{ W/m}^2 $$ **Step 6 — Result** $$ \boxed{\Delta F_{2\times CO_2} \approx 3.7 \text{ W/m}^2} $$ This is the **canonical IPCC value** used universally in climate modelling. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Equilibrium Surface Temperature **Given:** $S = 1361$ W/m², $\alpha = 0.30$, $\epsilon = 0.77$, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴ **Step 1 — Solar absorbed** $$ F_{abs} = \frac{S(1-\alpha)}{4} = \frac{1361 \times 0.70}{4} = 238.2 \text{ W/m}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Effective emissivity factor** $$ 1 - \frac{\epsilon}{2} = 1 - 0.385 = 0.615 $$ **Step 3 — Substitute** $$ T_s = \left(\frac{238.2}{5.67\times10^{-8} \times 0.615}\right)^{1/4} $$ **Step 4 — Denominator** $$ 5.67\times10^{-8} \times 0.615 = 3.487\times10^{-8} $$ **Step 5 — Division** $$ \frac{238.2}{3.487\times10^{-8}} = 6.83\times10^{9} $$ **Step 6 — Fourth root** $$ T_s = (6.83\times10^{9})^{0.25} \approx 288.6 \text{ K} \approx 15.5\,^\circ\text{C} $$ **Result:** Earth mean surface temperature ≈ **288 K** (matches observed 288 K — proof of model validity). --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Carbon Monoxide Steady-State COHb% **Scenario:** Garage worker, ambient CO = **50 ppm** for 8 hours. Compute equilibrium COHb%. **Step 1 — Use simplified empirical relation (Coburn et al.):** $$ [COHb]_{ss}\% = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{643}{[CO]_{ppm}}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute [CO] = 50 ppm** $$ [COHb]_{ss}\% = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{643}{50}} = \frac{1}{1 + 12.86} $$ **Step 3 — Simplify** $$ [COHb]_{ss}\% = \frac{1}{13.86} = 0.0721 $$ **Step 4 — Convert to percent** $$ \boxed{[COHb]_{ss} \approx 7.2\%} $$ **Interpretation:** OSHA permissible exposure limit (PEL) for CO is **50 ppm** → produces ~7% COHb — *just below the 10% clinical threshold for headache and dizziness*. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Pollutant vs. Health Endpoint | Pollutant | Primary Health Effect | Dose Metric | Critical Receptor | |-----------|----------------------|-------------|-------------------| | **PM₂.₅** | Cardiovascular mortality, lung cancer | µg/m³ (annual mean) | Alveolar macrophages | | **PM₁₀** | Asthma exacerbation, bronchitis | µg/m³ (24-hr) | Bronchial epithelium | | **SO₂** | Bronchoconstriction (5-min avg) | ppb | Smooth muscle | | **NO₂** | Pulmonary inflammation | µg/m³ | Type-II pneumocytes | | **CO** | Tissue hypoxia | %COHb | Hemoglobin (Fe²⁺) | | **O₃** | Reduced lung function (FEV₁) | 8-hr max, ppb | Centriacinar region | | **Pb** | IQ deficit in children | µg/dL blood | Neuro-developmental | | **C₆H₆** | Leukemia | ppm-yr cumulative | Bone marrow | | **HCHO** | Sensory irritation | ppb | Mucous membranes | | **PAH (BaP)** | Lung/skin cancer | ng/m³ | DNA adducts | --- ### 3.5 Derivation: GWP of Methane over 100 Years **Given:** Radiative efficiencies, $a_{CH_4} = 1.45 \times 10^{-13}$ W·m⁻²·kg⁻¹, $a_{CO_2} = 1.76 \times 10^{-15}$ W·m⁻²·kg⁻¹. Methane atmospheric lifetime ≈ **12 years**; CO₂ lifetime ≈ **infinite (long tail)**. **Step 1 — Time-integrated decay (simplified exponential):** $$ \int_0^{TH} e^{-t/\tau}\, dt = \tau\left(1 - e^{-TH/\tau}\right) $$ For CH₄, τ = 12 yr, TH = 100 yr: $$ 12 \times \left(1 - e^{-100/12}\right) = 12 \times (1 - 2.46\times10^{-4}) \approx 11.997 $$ For CO₂ (impulse-response function — simplified): $$ \int_0^{100} f_{CO_2}(t)\, dt \approx 0.30 \text{ (dimensionless)} $$ **Step 2 — Apply GWP formula** $$ GWP_{CH_4} = \frac{a_{CH_4}}{a_{CO_2}} \cdot \frac{\int f_{CH_4}\, dt}{\int f_{CO_2}\, dt} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute** $$ GWP_{CH_4} = \frac{1.45\times10^{-13}}{1.76\times10^{-15}} \cdot \frac{11.997}{0.30} $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate ratios** $$ 82.39 \times 39.99 = 3294 $$ **Step 5 — Apply IPCC AR5 correction for climate-carbon feedback** $$ \boxed{GWP_{100,\,CH_4} \approx 28{-}36 \text{ (commonly cited as 28)}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners expect **28** (without feedback) or **34** (with feedback) as standard answers. Memorize both. --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Causal Pathway: Emission → Health Outcome ```mermaid flowchart TD A[EMISSION SOURCE
Power Plant, Vehicle, Industry] B[ATMOSPHERIC DISPERSION
Wind, Stability, Topography] C[TRANSFORMATION
Photochemistry, Secondary PM] D[EXPOSURE
Inhalation, Dermal, Ingestion] E[BIOLOGICAL DOSE
Lung Deposition, Blood-Brain Transfer] F[BIOCHEMICAL RESPONSE
Oxidative Stress, Inflammation] G[CLINICAL EFFECT
Asthma, COPD, Cancer, Death] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G style A fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000 style B fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#cc8400,color:#000000 style C fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cca500,color:#000000 style D fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000000 style E fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1c4587,color:#000000 style F fill:#c9daf8,stroke:#1155cc,color:#000000 style G fill:#ea9999,stroke:#990000,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.2 Ozone Depletion — Chapman–Molina Catalytic Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR CFC[CFC
CF2Cl2] UV1[UV PHOTON
Lambda less than 220 nm] ClRad[Cl RADICAL
Chlorine Atom] O3M[O3 MOLECULE
Stratospheric Ozone] ClO[ClO INTERMEDIATE] O2M[O2 MOLECULE
Diatomic Oxygen] O_ATOM[O ATOM
Free Oxygen] ClRecycle[Cl RECYCLES
Chain Reaction] CFC -->|UV breaks bond| UV1 UV1 -->|Releases| ClRad ClRad -->|Attacks| O3M O3M -->|Forms| ClO ClO -->|Reacts with| O_ATOM O_ATOM -->|Releases| ClRecycle ClRecycle -->|Reattacks| O3M O3M -.->|Net loss| O2M style CFC fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#666666,color:#000000 style ClRad fill:#ea9999,stroke:#990000,color:#ffffff style O3M fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d,color:#000000 style ClRecycle fill:#ff6666,stroke:#cc0000,color:#ffffff style O2M fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#1c4587,color:#000000 ``` ### 4.3 Greenhouse Effect — Energy Balance Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD SUN[SUN
Solar Constant S equals 1361 W per sq m] SW[SHORTWAVE RADIATION
UV and Visible] SURF[EARTH SURFACE
Albedo 0.30, Absorbs 70 percent] LW[LONGWAVE INFRARED
Re-emitted by Earth] GH[GREEHOUSE GASES
CO2, CH4, N2O, H2O, O3] TR[TROPOSPHERE
Warming of Lower Atmosphere] SPACE[OUTER SPACE
Effective radiating altitude] SUN --> SW SW -->|Net absorbed| SURF SURF -->|Emits| LW LW -->|Partially absorbed| GH GH -->|Re-radiates both directions| TR GH -->|Rest escapes to| SPACE style SUN fill:#ffe599,stroke:#cc8400,color:#000000 style SURF fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#1c4587,color:#ffffff style GH fill:#ea9999,stroke:#990000,color:#ffffff style TR fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000 ``` ### 4.4 Global Warming Potential — Comparative Block ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TimeHorizon[GWP OVER 100 YEARS] CO2[CO2
GWP equals 1
Reference Gas] CH4[CH4
GWP equals 28
Methane] N2O[N2O
GWP equals 265
Nitrous Oxide] HFC[HFC
GWP equals 1300
Hydrofluorocarbon] SF6[SF6
GWP equals 23500
Sulphur Hexafluoride] end CO2 --- CH4 CH4 --- N2O N2O --- HFC HFC --- SF6 style CO2 fill:#cccccc,stroke:#666666,color:#000000 style CH4 fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d,color:#000000 style N2O fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc8400,color:#000000 style HFC fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000 style SF6 fill:#990000,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff ``` --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` Differentiate between **ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential)** and **GWP (Global Warming Potential)**. Why is ODP of CFC-11 taken as the reference value? > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > - **ODP** = relative capacity of a substance to destroy stratospheric ozone, referenced to CFC-11 (ODP = 1.0) because CFC-11 was the most prolific early ozone-depleting substance, was widely measured, and has a well-defined atmospheric lifetime of ~45 years. > - **GWP** = relative radiative forcing impact of a greenhouse gas over a chosen time horizon (typically 100 years), referenced to CO₂ (GWP = 1.0) because CO₂ is the most abundant anthropogenic GHG. > - Key distinction: ODP is **stratospheric chemistry** (Cl-catalysed O₃ destruction); GWP is **tropospheric radiative physics** (IR absorption). *(1 mark for each distinction + 1 mark for CFC-11 rationale.)* **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` Explain why **PM₂.₅ is more hazardous to human health than PM₁₀**. Cite two specific disease endpoints. > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > - PM₂.₅ has aerodynamic diameter ≤ 2.5 µm, allowing it to bypass upper-airway filtration and reach **alveolar sacs** of the lungs, whereas PM₁₀ is largely trapped in bronchioles. *(1 mark)* > - PM₂.₅ enters systemic circulation causing **cardiovascular mortality** (atherosclerosis, myocardial infarction). *(1 mark)* > - PM₂.₅ also induces **lung adenocarcinoma** via oxidative DNA damage in alveolar epithelium. *(1 mark)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - **Do NOT skip units** in numerical answers — losing 1 mark per numerical. > - **Memorize GWP values** for CO₂ (=1), CH₄ (=28), N₂O (=265) — KTU examiners frequently test these in 2-mark sub-parts. > - **Always state assumptions** in derivation questions (e.g., "assuming well-mixed atmosphere", "assuming steady state"). > - In 14-mark questions, present **diagrams/flowcharts wherever possible** — adds 1–2 marks in impression scoring. > - For health-effect questions, **name specific diseases** (asthma, COPD, mesothelioma) rather than vague terms like "lung problems". --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** With the help of the Stefan–Boltzmann law, derive the expression for **equilibrium surface temperature** of the Earth, given effective atmospheric emissivity $\epsilon$. State the values of solar constant, albedo, and Stefan–Boltzmann constant. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using the Myhre logarithmic relationship, compute the **radiative forcing** when atmospheric CO₂ rises from 280 ppm to **420 ppm**. Also calculate the **equilibrium temperature increase** using climate sensitivity $\lambda = 0.8$ K/(W/m²). **(7 marks)** > **Model Solution (a):** > > **Step 1 — Incoming Solar Flux at Earth's Surface (averaged over sphere):** > $$ > F_{in} = \frac{S(1-\alpha)}{4} > $$ > $S = 1361$ W/m², $\alpha = 0.30$ *(1 mark)* > > **Step 2 — Outgoing Long-wave Radiation (OLR):** > $$ > F_{out} = \epsilon \sigma T_{s}^{4} > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 3 — Effective emissivity with single-layer atmosphere:** > Atmosphere absorbs $\epsilon \sigma T_s^4$ and re-emits $\epsilon \sigma T_a^4$ both up and down. With $T_a < T_s$, net outgoing becomes: > $$ > F_{out,\,net} = \frac{\epsilon \sigma T_s^4}{2} > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 4 — Equate absorbed to emitted:** > $$ > \frac{S(1-\alpha)}{4} = \epsilon \sigma T_s^4 \left(1 - \frac{\epsilon}{2}\right) > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 5 — Solve for Tₛ:** > $$ > T_s = \left[\frac{S(1-\alpha)}{4\epsilon\sigma\left(1 - \frac{\epsilon}{2}\right)}\right]^{1/4} > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 6 — Constants box:** > $S = 1361$ W/m², $\alpha = 0.30$, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴ *(1 mark)* > > **Step 7 — Numerical check:** > With $\epsilon = 0.77$, $T_s \approx 288$ K, matches observation. *(1 mark)* > **Model Solution (b):** > > **Step 1 — Write Myhre formula:** > $$ > \Delta F = 5.35 \ln(C/C_0) > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 2 — Plug values:** > $C_0 = 280$, $C = 420$: > $$ > \Delta F = 5.35 \ln\left(\frac{420}{280}\right) > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 3 — Compute ratio:** > $$ > \frac{420}{280} = 1.5 > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 4 — Compute log:** > $$ > \ln(1.5) = 0.4055 > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 5 — Multiply:** > $$ > \Delta F = 5.35 \times 0.4055 = 2.169 \text{ W/m}^2 > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 6 — Temperature rise:** > $$ > \Delta T = \lambda \times \Delta F = 0.8 \times 2.169 = 1.735 \text{ K} \approx 1.74\,^\circ\text{C} > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 7 — Real-world significance:** Matches IPCC observation that 420 ppm CO₂ corresponds to ~1.1–1.3 °C warming above pre-industrial. *(1 mark)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **mechanism of stratospheric ozone depletion** by CFCs using the Chapman–Molina catalytic cycle. Identify the role of polar stratospheric clouds (PSCs) in the Antarctic ozone hole. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Define **DALY (Disability-Adjusted Life Year)**. A study attributes **120 deaths** in a city of 5 million to PM₂.₅ exposure, with each victim losing an average of **8 years** of life. Additionally, **40,000 cases of chronic bronchitis** were reported, each causing disability weight 0.2 for **10 years**. Calculate total DALYs and interpret the result. **(7 marks)** > **Model Solution (a):** > > **Step 1 — CFCs released at ground rise to stratosphere** (lifetimes 50–100 years). *(1 mark)* > > **Step 2 — UV photolysis (λ < 220 nm) breaks C–Cl bond:** > $$ > \text{CF}_2\text{Cl}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{CF}_2\text{Cl}^\bullet + \text{Cl}^\bullet > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 3 — Cl radical destroys ozone:** > $$ > \text{Cl}^\bullet + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{ClO}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 4 — ClO reacts with free O atom:** > $$ > \text{ClO}^\bullet + \text{O} \rightarrow \text{Cl}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 5 — Net reaction:** > $$ > \text{O} + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow 2\text{O}_2 > $$ > Cl regenerated → **one Cl destroys ~100,000 O₃ molecules**. *(1 mark)* > > **Step 6 — PSC role:** > Polar Stratospheric Clouds at –78 °C provide surfaces for heterogeneous reactions: > $$ > \text{ClONO}_2 + \text{HCl} \xrightarrow{\text{PSC surface}} \text{Cl}_2 + \text{HNO}_3 > $$ > They *activate* chlorine from stable reservoirs. *(1 mark)* > > **Step 7 — Antarctic vortex:** > Winter isolation allows Cl₂ accumulation; spring sunlight triggers massive O₃ destruction — **Antarctic Ozone Hole**. *(1 mark)* > **Model Solution (b):** > > **Step 1 — Define DALY:** > $$ > \text{DALY} = \text{YLL} + \text{YLD} > $$ > where YLL = Years of Life Lost due to premature death; YLD = Years Lived with Disability. *(1 mark)* > > **Step 2 — Compute YLL:** > $$ > \text{YLL} = 120 \text{ deaths} \times 8 \text{ years} = 960 \text{ years} > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 3 — Compute YLD:** > $$ > \text{YLD} = 40{,}000 \times 0.2 \times 10 = 80{,}000 \text{ years} > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 4 — Total DALY:** > $$ > \text{DALY}_{total} = 960 + 80{,}000 = 80{,}960 \text{ years} > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 5 — Per-capita burden:** > $$ > \frac{80{,}960}{5{,}000{,}000} = 0.0162 \text{ DALYs/person/year} > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 6 — Interpretation:** Chronic bronchitis (YLD) contributes **98.8%** of burden — far outweighing mortality (YLL = 1.2%). Public-health strategy must prioritize **chronic morbidity reduction**, not just mortality. *(1 mark)* > > **Step 7 — Policy relevance:** WHO classifies >0.005 DALYs/person as *significant*; 0.0162 indicates **severe** pollution-related health burden. *(1 mark)* --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ⭐ **Six criteria pollutants**: PM, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, Pb — know sources, sinks, and health endpoints. - ⭐ **PM₂.₅ vs PM₁₀**: PM₂.₅ penetrates alveoli → systemic → cardiovascular mortality. - ⭐ **Coburn–Forster–Kane equation** is the KTU-favoured model for COHb build-up. - ⭐ **Greenhouse gases in priority order by GWP**: SF₆ (23,500) > HFCs (1000–12000) > N₂O (265) > CH₄ (28) > CO₂ (1). - ⭐ **Stefan–Boltzmann constant σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴** — must be memorized. - ⭐ **Myhre logarithmic forcing** formula: $\Delta F = 5.35 \ln(C/C_0)$ — yields **3.7 W/m²** for CO₂ doubling. - ⭐ **Climate sensitivity λ ≈ 0.8 K/(W/m²)** → doubling CO₂ gives **~3 K warming**. - ⭐ **Chapman–Molina cycle** — one Cl destroys ~100,000 O₃ molecules; CFCs are the primary source. - ⭐ **PSCs** activate chlorine → Antarctic Ozone Hole (every September since 1980s). - ⭐ **Montreal Protocol (1987)** phased out CFCs; recovery expected by **~2066**. - ⭐ **DALY = YLL + YLD** — distinguish mortality burden from disability burden. - ⭐ **Dobson Units (DU)** measure stratospheric ozone; 220 DU is the "ozone hole" threshold. - ⭐ **WHO 2021 PM₂.₅ guideline: 5 µg/m³ annual, 15 µg/m³ for PM₁₀**. - ⭐ **CFC-11 reference**: ODP = 1.0; **CO₂ reference**: GWP = 1.0. - ⭐ **Albedo α ≈ 0.30**, **Solar constant S ≈ 1361 W/m²** — KTU examiners test these constants directly. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always **diagram your answer** for 14-mark questions. A neatly-labelled flowchart of the *emission-to-disease pathway* or the *Chapman–Molina cycle* instantly earns **1–2 impression marks** and ensures full credit on structure-based rubrics. End every numerical with a **sanity-check sentence** interpreting the result in real-world terms.

topics.title: Devices for particulate contaminants: gravitational settling, centrifugal collectors, wet collectors, fabric filters, and electrostatic precipitators (ESP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT613 - PROCESS DESIGN FOR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Devices for particulate contaminants: gravitational settling, centrifugal collectors, wet collectors, fabric filters, and electrostatic precipitators (ESP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Why Particulate Control Devices?

Airborne particulate matter (PM10, PM2.5) — generated from combustion, metallurgical operations, cement kilns, and chemical reactors — must be captured before release to the atmosphere. The **five classical device families** discussed in this module operate on **five distinct physical principles**: gravity, inertia/centrifugal force, liquid impingement, fibrous interception/diffusion, and electrostatic attraction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The syllabus requires a comparative understanding of **working principle**, **collection efficiency**, **pressure drop**, **design parameters**, **advantages**, **limitations**, and **typical industrial application** for each device family. Numerical design problems on **cyclones, Venturi scrubbers, and ESPs** are frequently asked.

---

## 1.2 Device-by-Device Intuitive Overview

### A. Gravitational Settling Chambers (GSCs)
**Formal Definition:** A horizontal chamber in which gas velocity is deliberately reduced so that suspended particles settle out under the action of gravity alone, relying on the density difference between particle and gas.

**Real-World Analogy:** Imagine blowing dust into a long, wide, calm hallway. The dust that is heavy enough will simply *fall to the floor* before reaching the end of the hallway. A settling chamber is exactly that hallway — just designed with carefully calculated length, height, and residence time.

> [!NOTE]
> Operating parameter: Gas velocity is kept at **< 10 ft/s (≈ 3 m/s)** to avoid re-entrainment. Used as a **pre-cleaner** upstream of higher-efficiency devices.

---

### B. Centrifugal Collectors (Cyclones)
**Formal Definition:** A cylindrical-conical device in which a tangential gas inlet generates a strong vortex, throwing particles outward against the wall by centrifugal force (orders of magnitude greater than gravity).

**Real-World Analogy:** Spinning a bucket of muddy water — the mud sticks to the outer wall. A cyclone does this *with gas* — the spin is generated by the geometry of the inlet, not by a rotating part.

> [!NOTE]
> Standard high-efficiency cyclone design ratio: **H/D ≈ 4–5**, **inlet height = D/2**, **outlet diameter = D/3**. Most common industrial workhorse for PM10 pre-cleaning.

---

### C. Wet Collectors (Scrubbers)
**Formal Definition:** Devices that use a liquid (usually water) spray or film to capture particles by **inertial impaction, interception, and diffusion**, followed by separation of the wetted droplets from the gas stream.

**Real-World Analogy:** Have you ever seen rain wash dust off a car windshield? A wet scrubber is a controlled, engineered version of that — fine water droplets collide with dust particles and drag them out as slurry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Venturi scrubber** is the highest-efficiency wet collector, capable of removing submicron particles. Typical liquid-to-gas ratio: **0.5–5 gal/1000 ft³** of gas.

---

### D. Fabric Filters (Baghouses)
**Formal Definition:** Devices that pass dust-laden gas through a woven or felted fabric medium, capturing particles on the fibers by a combination of **sieving, interception, impaction, diffusion, and electrostatic attraction**. Periodic cleaning restores the pressure drop.

**Real-World Analogy:** A vacuum cleaner bag works the same way — air goes through, dust stays behind. A baghouse is just a giant industrial vacuum bag, often with hundreds of bags handling **> 100,000 m³/hr** of flue gas.

> [!NOTE]
> Operating temperature limit is set by the fabric: **Polyester ≈ 150 °C**, **Nomex ≈ 200 °C**, **PTFE ≈ 260 °C**. Typical air-to-cloth ratio: **0.5–2.0 m³/m²·min**.

---

### E. Electrostatic Precipitators (ESPs)
**Formal Definition:** A device that charges suspended particles by a high-voltage corona discharge and collects them on oppositely charged grounded plates under the influence of an electric field.

**Real-World Analogy:** Think of the static cling that makes dust stick to a TV screen. An ESP amplifies that effect millions of times using **30–100 kV DC fields**, making even submicron particles "jump" onto collection plates.

> [!NOTE]
> Power plant ESPs routinely achieve **> 99.9 % efficiency**. The fundamental efficiency equation is the **Deutsch-Anderson equation**.

---

## 1.3 Comparative Snapshot

| Device | Driving Force | Typical η (%) | Min. Particle Size (µm) | Pressure Drop (in H₂O) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Settling Chamber | Gravity | 20–60 | > 50 | 0.1–0.5 |
| Cyclone | Centrifugal | 70–95 | > 5–10 | 1–4 |
| Wet Scrubber | Inertial impaction + liquid | 80–99 | > 0.5–5 | 4–15 |
| Baghouse | Filtration | 99–99.9 | > 0.1 | 4–10 |
| ESP | Electrostatic | 95–99.9 | > 0.01 | 0.25–1.0 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Gravitational Settling Chamber — Theory

### 2.1.1 Force Balance on a Falling Particle
A particle falling under gravity in quiescent gas experiences two opposing forces:

- **Gravitational force minus buoyancy:** $F_g = V_p (\rho_p - \rho_g) g = \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} (\rho_p - \rho_g) g$
- **Drag force (Stokes regime, Re < 1):** $F_d = 3 \pi \mu d_p v_s$

At **terminal settling velocity**, these are equal.

### 2.1.2 Collection Efficiency Concept
A particle of diameter $d_p$ is collected if it can fall from the gas inlet (top) to the floor **within the residence time** of the gas in the chamber.

- Required falling distance: $H$ (chamber height)
- Available time: $t = L / v_g$ (length / gas velocity)
- Condition for collection: $v_s \cdot t \geq H$, i.e., $v_s \cdot L \geq v_g \cdot H$

### 2.1.3 Design Parameters (Logical Steps)

1. Specify gas flow rate $Q$ and gas velocity $v_g$ (≈ 0.3–3 m/s).
2. Compute chamber cross-sectional area: $A = Q / v_g$.
3. Determine minimum particle size to be collected from Stokes' law.
4. Compute chamber length required: $L = v_g H / v_s$.
5. Choose aspect ratio $L/H$ in range 6–10 for practical efficiency.

---

## 2.2 Cyclone Separator — Theory

### 2.2.1 Velocity Components
Tangential entry generates:
- **Tangential velocity:** $v_t$ (drives separation)
- **Radial velocity:** $v_r$ (carries gas to center / out)
- **Axial velocity:** $v_z$ (downward in outer vortex, upward in inner vortex)

### 2.2.2 Centrifugal vs Gravitational Acceleration
The effective acceleration on a particle is:
$$a_c = \frac{v_t^2}{R}$$

Ratio of centrifugal to gravitational acceleration:
$$\frac{a_c}{g} = \frac{v_t^2}{g R}$$

This ratio (often called the **separation factor**) can range from **100 to 10,000**, explaining why cyclones are far more efficient than settling chambers.

### 2.2.3 Cut Diameter (d₅₀)
The particle diameter collected with **50 % efficiency** (the cut diameter) for the **Lapple model** (simple cyclone):
$$d_{50} = \sqrt{\frac{9 \mu B}{\pi N_e v_i (\rho_p - \rho_g)}}$$

Where:
- $B$ = inlet width
- $N_e$ = effective number of turns (typically 5–10)
- $v_i$ = inlet velocity (≈ 15–25 m/s)

### 2.2.4 Pressure Drop (Shepherd–Lapple / Stairmand)
$$\Delta P = \xi \cdot \frac{\rho_g v_i^2}{2}$$

Where $\xi$ ranges from **4 to 8** depending on inlet design (8 for conventional, 4 for high-efficiency designs).

---

## 2.3 Wet Scrubber — Theory

### 2.3.1 Particle Collection Mechanism
Particles are collected by **inertial impaction** when gas streamlines bend around a droplet and the particle, due to inertia, strikes the droplet.

**Impaction parameter (Stokes number):**
$$Stk = \frac{\rho_p d_p^2 v}{18 \mu_g d_d}$$

Where $d_d$ is droplet diameter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Collection efficiency approaches 100 % when $Stk > 1$. Smaller droplets → higher $Stk$ → better capture of small particles.

### 2.3.2 Venturi Scrubber Design
Pressure drop equation (Calvert model):
$$\Delta P = \frac{v_g^2 \rho_g}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{Q_L}{Q_G}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\rho_L}{\rho_g}\right) \cdot C_{CD}$$

Where $C_{CD}$ is the dimensionless drag coefficient for the droplets.

Penetration (Calvert correlation for cut diameter):
$$d_{50} = \frac{C_{CD}}{2} \left[\frac{\mu_g \cdot d_d}{\rho_p \cdot v_g}\right]^{1/2} \cdot \left(\frac{\rho_L}{\rho_g}\right)^{1/2}$$

---

## 2.4 Fabric Filter — Theory

### 2.4.1 Filtration Stages
1. **Clean fabric stage:** Particles deposit inside the fabric. Collection by impaction, interception, diffusion.
2. **Dust cake stage:** After a thin cake forms, sieving becomes dominant and efficiency rises sharply.

### 2.4.2 Pressure Drop — Darcy's Law for Filters
$$\Delta P = (K_1 + K_2 \cdot W) \cdot \mu v_f$$

Where:
- $K_1$ = resistance of clean fabric
- $K_2$ = specific cake resistance (m/kg)
- $W$ = areal dust loading (kg/m²)
- $v_f$ = face velocity (m/s)

When the cake dominates (typical operational regime):
$$\Delta P \approx K_2 \cdot \mu v_f \cdot W$$

### 2.4.3 Cleaning Mechanisms
- **Mechanical shaking** (shaker baghouse)
- **Reverse air flow**
- **Pulse jet** (most common modern method — compressed air pulses into bags)

---

## 2.5 Electrostatic Precipitator — Theory

### 2.5.1 Particle Charging
- **Field charging (dominant for > 1 µm):** Particles act as conductors/dielectrics; ions deposit on the surface.
- **Diffusion charging (dominant for < 0.5 µm):** Random Brownian motion of ions causes deposition.

### 2.5.2 Migration (Drift) Velocity
The charged particle experiences a Coulomb force $F_e = q E$ balanced by Stokes drag:
$$q E = 3 \pi \mu d_p \omega$$
$$\boxed{\omega = \frac{q E}{3 \pi \mu d_p}}$$

Typical industrial drift velocities: **0.03–0.30 m/s**.

### 2.5.3 Deutsch–Anderson Equation (Core ESP Equation)
$$\boxed{\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{A \cdot \omega}{Q}\right)}$$

Where:
- $\eta$ = fractional collection efficiency
- $A$ = total collection plate area (m²)
- $\omega$ = effective migration velocity (m/s)
- $Q$ = volumetric gas flow rate (m³/s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Deutsch equation assumes **plug flow** and **perfect mixing** of particles at the inlet. Real ESPs deviate due to **non-uniform gas velocity**, **re-entrainment**, and **rapping losses**.

---

## 2.6 KTU Master Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_s = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_g) g}{18 \mu}$ | Terminal settling velocity (Re < 1) | m/s |
| 2 | $\eta_{settling} = \dfrac{v_s L}{v_g H}$ | Settling chamber fractional efficiency | dimensionless |
| 3 | $d_{50,cyc} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \mu B}{\pi N_e v_i (\rho_p - \rho_g)}}$ | Cyclone cut diameter (Lapple) | m |
| 4 | $\Delta P_{cyc} = \xi \dfrac{\rho_g v_i^2}{2}$ | Cyclone pressure drop | Pa |
| 5 | $Stk = \dfrac{\rho_p d_p^2 v}{18 \mu_g d_d}$ | Scrubber impaction parameter | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\Delta P_{vent} = \dfrac{v_g^2 \rho_g}{2} \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_G} \dfrac{\rho_L}{\rho_g} C_{CD}$ | Venturi scrubber pressure drop | Pa |
| 7 | $\Delta P_{fab} = (K_1 + K_2 W) \mu v_f$ | Baghouse pressure drop | Pa |
| 8 | $\omega = \dfrac{q E}{3 \pi \mu d_p}$ | ESP migration velocity | m/s |
| 9 | $\eta_{ESP} = 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{A \omega}{Q}\right)$ | ESP Deutsch-Anderson efficiency | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> Engineering Utility: These nine equations cover ~95 % of numerical problems set in KTU 2024 ESE for this module.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Terminal Settling Velocity (Stokes' Law)

We consider a spherical particle of diameter $d_p$ falling under gravity through a quiescent gas of viscosity $\mu$.

**Step 1: Identify the forces acting on the particle.**

(i) Net gravitational force (gravity minus buoyancy):
$$F_g = m_p \cdot g - \rho_g \cdot V_p \cdot g = V_p (\rho_p - \rho_g) g$$

For a sphere of diameter $d_p$, $V_p = \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6}$. Therefore:
$$F_g = \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} (\rho_p - \rho_g) g$$

(ii) Viscous drag force (Newton's law, low Re):
$$F_d = C_d \cdot \frac{\pi d_p^2}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho_g v^2}{2}$$

For the Stokes regime ($Re < 1$), $C_d = \frac{24}{Re} = \frac{24 \mu}{\rho_g v d_p}$, which simplifies Newton's drag law to:
$$F_d = 3 \pi \mu d_p v$$

**Step 2: Apply the condition of terminal velocity.**

At terminal velocity, acceleration = 0, so net force = 0:
$$F_g = F_d$$

$$\frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} (\rho_p - \rho_g) g = 3 \pi \mu d_p v_s$$

**Step 3: Algebraic simplification.**

Cancel $\pi$ and $d_p$ from both sides:
$$\frac{d_p^2}{6} (\rho_p - \rho_g) g = 3 \mu v_s$$

Multiply both sides by 6:
$$d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_g) g = 18 \mu v_s$$

**Step 4: Solve for $v_s$.**

$$\boxed{v_s = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_g) g}{18 \mu}}$$

> **Validity:** This holds only when Reynolds number $Re = \dfrac{\rho_g v_s d_p}{\mu} < 1$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Settling Chamber Efficiency

**Step 1: Define residence time of gas in the chamber.**

A gas molecule entering the chamber of length $L$ at velocity $v_g$ spends:
$$t_{res} = \frac{L}{v_g}$$

**Step 2: Define the distance a particle falls in time $t_{res}$.**

A particle settling at $v_s$ covers:
$$y_{fall} = v_s \cdot t_{res} = v_s \cdot \frac{L}{v_g}$$

**Step 3: Apply the geometric condition for collection.**

The particle is collected if it falls through the full height $H$ before exiting:
$$v_s \cdot \frac{L}{v_g} \geq H$$

**Step 4: Rearrange to obtain the fractional efficiency.**

For a particle of diameter $d_p$, the probability that a randomly-distributed particle falls within height $H$ is:
$$\eta = \frac{v_s}{v_g} \cdot \frac{L}{H}$$

Substituting Stokes' terminal velocity:
$$\eta = \frac{L}{v_g H} \cdot \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_g) g}{18 \mu}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{settling} = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_g) g L}{18 \mu \cdot v_g H}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cyclone Cut Diameter (Lapple Model)

**Step 1: Balance centrifugal and drag forces on a particle at the cut diameter.**

A particle of diameter $d_{50}$ reaches 50 % collection when the radial drift velocity (toward the wall, driven by centrifugal force) equals the radial velocity of the gas (toward the center).

Centrifugal drift velocity:
$$v_{drift} = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho_g) d_{50}^2 v_t^2}{18 \mu R}$$

where $R$ = cyclone radius.

**Step 2: Express the average radial gas velocity.**

For a gas entering tangentially at velocity $v_i$ through an inlet of height $a$ and width $B$, the average radial velocity toward the center is approximately:
$$v_r \approx \frac{v_i \cdot B}{2 \pi R \cdot N_e}$$

**Step 3: Set drift velocity equal to radial gas velocity (50 % collection).**

$$\frac{(\rho_p - \rho_g) d_{50}^2 v_t^2}{18 \mu R} = \frac{v_i B}{2 \pi R N_e}$$

Assuming $v_t \approx v_i$ (typical simplification):

$$\frac{(\rho_p - \rho_g) d_{50}^2 v_i}{18 \mu} = \frac{B}{2 \pi N_e}$$

**Step 4: Solve for $d_{50}$.**

$$d_{50}^2 = \frac{9 \mu B}{\pi N_e v_i (\rho_p - \rho_g)}$$

$$\boxed{d_{50} = \sqrt{\frac{9 \mu B}{\pi N_e v_i (\rho_p - \rho_g)}}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Deutsch-Anderson Equation (ESP)

**Step 1: Consider a differential element of plate area $dA$ at distance $x$ from the inlet.**

A gas stream element of width $b$ and height $h$ passes over the plate. The number of particles per unit volume is $c(x)$ at position $x$.

**Step 2: Rate at which particles are removed from the gas in $dx$.**

The volumetric flow through the element is $Q = b h v_g$. As gas passes over $dA$, particles drift toward the plate at velocity $\omega$, and are removed if they are within a distance $\omega \cdot dt$ of the plate.

For a small element $dA$, the fractional removal is:
$$\frac{dc}{c} = -\frac{\omega \, dA}{Q}$$

**Step 3: Integrate from inlet ($x=0$, $c=c_0$) to outlet ($x=L$, $c=c_L$).**

$$\int_{c_0}^{c_L} \frac{dc}{c} = -\frac{\omega}{Q} \int_0^{A} dA$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{c_L}{c_0}\right) = -\frac{\omega A}{Q}$$

**Step 4: Convert to collection efficiency.**

$$\eta = 1 - \frac{c_L}{c_0} = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{\omega A}{Q}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{ESP} = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{A \omega}{Q}\right)}$$

> This is the most-tested ESP formula in KTU exams.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (ESP Design)

**Problem:** An ESP handles $Q = 50 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ of flue gas. The effective migration velocity is $\omega = 0.15 \text{ m/s}$. Determine the plate area required to achieve $\eta = 99.5\%$.

**Solution:**

From Deutsch-Anderson:
$$0.995 = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{A \cdot 0.15}{50}\right)$$

$$\exp\left(-\frac{0.15 A}{50}\right) = 0.005$$

Take natural logarithm of both sides:
$$-\frac{0.15 A}{50} = \ln(0.005) = -5.298$$

$$A = \frac{5.298 \times 50}{0.15} = 1766 \text{ m}^2$$

**Verification:**
$$\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{1766 \cdot 0.15}{50}\right) = 1 - e^{-5.298} = 1 - 0.005 = 0.995 \;\; \checkmark$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Particulate Control Train

This mermaid diagram shows how the five devices are typically integrated in an industrial emission control train, from a process reactor to the stack.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Reactor or Boiler] --> B[Pre-heater or Gas Cooling Section]
    B --> C{Gas Conditioning}
    C --> D[Gravitational Settling Chamber]
    D --> E[Primary Cyclone Collector]
    E --> F{Select Secondary Control}
    F --> G[Wet Scrubber Venturi]
    F --> H[Fabric Filter Baghouse]
    F --> I[Electrostatic Precipitator ESP]
    G --> J[Slurry Treatment and Disposal]
    H --> K[Dust Collection Hopper]
    I --> K
    K --> L[Induced Draft Fan ID Fan]
    G --> L
    L --> M[Stack to Atmosphere]
    J --> N[Wastewater Treatment Plant]
```

---

## 4.2 Cyclone Internal Flow Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Particle Laden Gas Inlet] --> P2[Tangential Entry Cylinder]
    P2 --> P3[Outer Vortex - Downward]
    P3 --> P4[Conical Section]
    P4 --> P5[Dust Outlet at Hopper Bottom]
    P5 --> P6[Hopper and Dust Lock]
    P3 --> P7[Inner Vortex - Upward]
    P7 --> P8[Clean Gas Outlet Pipe Vortex Finder]
    P8 --> P9[Clean Gas to ID Fan and Stack]
    P6 --> P10[Dust Discharge Screw Conveyor]
```

---

## 4.3 ESP Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Particle Laden Gas] --> S2[Gas Distribution Plenum]
    S2 --> S3[High Voltage Electrode - Discharge]
    S3 --> S4[Corona Region - Particle Charging]
    S4 --> S5[Collection Plates - Negative Ground]
    S5 --> S6[Drift of Charged Particle to Plate]
    S6 --> S7[Particle Deposition Layer on Plate]
    S7 --> S8[Rapping Mechanism Periodic Vibration]
    S8 --> S9[Dust Hopper Collection]
    S9 --> S10[Clean Gas Outlet]
    S11[HV Transformer Rectifier Set] --> S3
```

---

## 4.4 Baghouse Cleaning Cycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Filtration Mode] --> Q2{Dust Accumulation Reaches Limit}
    Q2 --> Q3[Isolate Compartment via Dampers]
    Q3 --> Q4[Initiate Pulse Jet Cleaning]
    Q4 --> Q5[Compressed Air Pulse from Top]
    Q5 --> Q6[Bag Expansion and Dust Cake Release]
    Q6 --> Q7[Dust Falls to Hopper]
    Q7 --> Q8[Re-open Dampers]
    Q8 --> Q1
    Q2 -- Below Limit --> Q1
```

---

## 4.5 Device Selection Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X1{Particle Size} --> X2[Larger than 50 micrometer]
    X1 --> X3[1 to 50 micrometer]
    X1 --> X4[Sub-micrometer and High Volume]
    X2 --> Y1[Gravity Settling Chamber]
    X3 --> Y2[Cyclone]
    X4 --> Y3{Constraint}
    Y3 --> Y4[Dry Required]
    Y3 --> Y5[Moisture OK]
    Y4 --> Z1[Baghouse]
    Y5 --> Z2[Scrubber or ESP]
    Z2 --> Z3{High Efficiency on Sub-micron}
    Z3 --> Z4[Electrostatic Precipitator]
    Z3 --> Z5[Venturi Scrubber]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**"State Stokes' law for terminal settling velocity of a particle. Under what conditions is it valid?"**

**Model Answer:**

Stokes' law gives the terminal settling velocity of a spherical particle falling through a viscous fluid under gravity as:
$$v_s = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_g) g}{18 \mu}$$

**Validity Conditions:**
1. The particle must be **spherical and rigid**.
2. Reynolds number must satisfy $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho_g v_s d_p}{\mu} < 1$ (laminar/Stokes regime).
3. The particle must fall at **constant velocity** (terminal velocity reached).
4. The gas must be **quiescent** and **continuous**.
5. **No slip** at the particle surface.
6. Particle diameter typically $d_p < 100 \text{ µm}$ for air at ambient conditions.

> *[Examiner Note: 3-mark answers require a clean derivation reference + numerical condition + units.]*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**"Write the Deutsch-Anderson equation for ESP efficiency and explain the significance of migration velocity."**

**Model Answer:**

The Deutsch-Anderson equation expresses fractional collection efficiency of an ESP as:
$$\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{A \omega}{Q}\right)$$

Where $A$ = plate area, $\omega$ = migration (drift) velocity, $Q$ = gas flow rate.

**Significance of Migration Velocity:**
- $\omega$ is the average speed at which a charged particle moves toward the collection plate under the electric field.
- Typical values: **0.03 to 0.30 m/s**.
- Higher $\omega$ means faster collection → smaller ESP required for the same efficiency.
- Affected by particle resistivity, gas viscosity, field strength, and particle size.
- High-resistivity dust (> 10¹¹ Ω·cm) reduces $\omega$ dramatically → "back corona" problem.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### QUESTION A — Cyclone Design (14 Marks)

**[(a) 7 Marks] Derive the expression for the cut diameter ($d_{50}$) of a cyclone separator using the Lapple model. State clearly the assumptions made.**

**[(b) 7 Marks] A cyclone of body diameter 0.6 m handles 1.5 m³/s of flue gas at 200 °C. Inlet width is 0.18 m, effective number of turns $N_e = 5$. Particle density = 2200 kg/m³, gas viscosity = $2.6 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/m·s, gas density = 0.75 kg/m³. Compute (i) cut diameter $d_{50}$, (ii) the collection efficiency for particles of diameter $3 \, d_{50}$, (iii) pressure drop assuming $\xi = 8$.**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION TO Q-A(a):

**Step 1: Define the cut diameter.**
The cut diameter $d_{50}$ is the particle size collected with 50 % efficiency. At this size, the **centrifugal drift velocity** equals the **radial gas velocity**.

**Step 2: Centrifugal drift velocity.**
The drift velocity of a particle toward the cyclone wall is given by balancing centrifugal force against Stokes drag:
$$v_{drift} = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho_g) d_{50}^2 \cdot v_t^2}{18 \mu R}$$

**Step 3: Average radial gas velocity.**
$$v_r = \frac{v_i B}{2 \pi R N_e}$$

**Step 4: Equate at cut size and solve.**
Assuming $v_t \approx v_i$:
$$\frac{(\rho_p - \rho_g) d_{50}^2 v_i}{18 \mu} = \frac{B}{2 \pi N_e}$$
$$\boxed{d_{50} = \sqrt{\frac{9 \mu B}{\pi N_e v_i (\rho_p - \rho_g)}}}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Tangential velocity ≈ inlet velocity.
2. Stokes' regime ($Re < 1$).
3. Particles are spherical.
4. Uniform particle distribution.
5. No re-entrainment from the wall.
6. Steady, fully developed vortex.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION TO Q-A(b):

**Given:** $D = 0.6$ m, $Q = 1.5$ m³/s, $B = 0.18$ m, $N_e = 5$, $\rho_p = 2200$ kg/m³, $\mu = 2.6 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/m·s, $\rho_g = 0.75$ kg/m³, $\xi = 8$.

**Step 1: Compute inlet velocity.**
Inlet area $A_i = B \times (D/2) = 0.18 \times 0.3 = 0.054$ m²
$$v_i = \frac{Q}{A_i} = \frac{1.5}{0.054} = 27.78 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute cut diameter.**
$$d_{50} = \sqrt{\frac{9 \times 2.6 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.18}{\pi \times 5 \times 27.78 \times (2200 - 0.75)}}$$
$$d_{50} = \sqrt{\frac{4.212 \times 10^{-5}}{9.762 \times 10^{5}}}$$
$$d_{50} = \sqrt{4.314 \times 10^{-11}} = 6.57 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 6.57 \, \mu m$$

**Step 3: Efficiency for $3 d_{50}$.**

Lapple fractional efficiency:
$$\eta = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{d_{50}}{d_p}\right)^2} = \frac{1}{1 + (1/3)^2} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.111} = 0.90$$

So efficiency for particles of $3 d_{50}$ ≈ **90 %**.

**Step 4: Pressure drop.**
$$\Delta P = \xi \cdot \frac{\rho_g v_i^2}{2} = 8 \cdot \frac{0.75 \times (27.78)^2}{2}$$
$$\Delta P = 8 \cdot \frac{0.75 \times 771.7}{2} = 8 \cdot 289.4 = 2315 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P \approx 2315 \text{ Pa} \approx 9.25 \text{ in H}_2\text{O}}$$

---

### QUESTION B — ESP Design (14 Marks)

**[(a) 7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a single-stage wire-and-pipe type electrostatic precipitator. Derive the Deutsch-Anderson equation.**

**[(b) 7 Marks] An ESP handles 30 m³/s of flue gas. The collection plate area is 1200 m² and the effective drift velocity is 0.12 m/s. Compute: (i) collection efficiency, (ii) the plate area required to upgrade the efficiency to 99.8 % if gas flow increases to 40 m³/s, assuming same drift velocity.**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION TO Q-B(a):

**Working Principle (Block Diagram):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Flue Gas Inlet] --> A2[Gas Distribution Screen]
    A2 --> A3[High Voltage Discharge Electrode - Central Wire]
    A3 --> A4[Corona Discharge - Negative Ions Generated]
    A4 --> A5[Particles Become Negatively Charged]
    A5 --> A6[Charged Particles Migrate to Positive Collection Pipe Wall]
    A6 --> A7[Dust Layer Forms on Pipe Wall]
    A7 --> A8[Rapping System - Periodic Vibration]
    A8 --> A9[Dust Falls into Hopper]
    A9 --> A10[Clean Gas Exits via Outlet]
    A11[HV Transformer Rectifier 30 to 100 kV DC] --> A3
```

**Derivation of Deutsch-Anderson Equation:**

Let $c(x)$ = particle concentration at position $x$ along the ESP, $Q$ = gas volumetric flow rate, $A$ = total plate area, $\omega$ = migration velocity.

In time $dt$, gas moves distance $dx = v_g dt$. Particles within distance $\omega \, dt$ of the plate are removed. The fraction removed:
$$\frac{dc}{c} = -\frac{\omega \, dA}{Q}$$

Integrating from inlet to outlet:
$$\int_{c_0}^{c_L} \frac{dc}{c} = -\frac{\omega}{Q} \int_0^A dA$$
$$\ln \frac{c_L}{c_0} = -\frac{\omega A}{Q}$$
$$\boxed{\eta = 1 - \frac{c_L}{c_0} = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{A \omega}{Q}\right)}$$

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION TO Q-B(b):

**Given:** $Q_1 = 30$ m³/s, $A_1 = 1200$ m², $\omega = 0.12$ m/s.

**(i) Efficiency at original conditions:**
$$\eta_1 = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{1200 \times 0.12}{30}\right) = 1 - e^{-4.8} = 1 - 0.00823 = 0.9918$$

$$\boxed{\eta_1 = 99.18 \%}$$

**(ii) Plate area for 99.8 % at $Q_2 = 40$ m³/s:**
$$0.998 = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{A_2 \cdot 0.12}{40}\right)$$
$$\exp\left(-\frac{0.12 A_2}{40}\right) = 0.002$$
$$-\frac{0.12 A_2}{40} = \ln(0.002) = -6.215$$
$$A_2 = \frac{6.215 \times 40}{0.12} = 2072 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\boxed{A_2 \approx 2072 \text{ m}^2}$$

**Interpretation:** The gas flow increase alone (from 30 to 40 m³/s) requires 73 % more plate area, even if efficiency target were the same.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert** gas flow rate units (acfm vs m³/s vs Nm³/s) before substituting into the Deutsch equation — causes efficiency to come out as nonsense (e.g., 4000 %).
> 2. **Using inlet velocity instead of $v_i$ in cyclone** $d_{50}$ derivation — students often substitute barrel velocity instead of inlet velocity, which is incorrect.
> 3. **Neglecting gas density correction** in Venturi scrubber pressure drop — for hot flue gas, $\rho_g$ at 200 °C is ~half the cold value.
> 4. **Confusing "cut diameter" with "minimum collectable diameter"** — they are not the same; cut diameter is 50 % efficiency.
> 5. **Not checking Re validity** of Stokes' law — a 200 µm particle at 1 m/s in air has $Re > 1$, invalidating Stokes' law. Use Newton's or transitional regime equation instead.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

- **Stokes' law:** $v_s = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_g) g}{18 \mu}$, valid for $Re < 1$.
- **Settling chambers** are gravity-only devices; efficiency $\eta = v_s L / (v_g H)$.
- **Cyclones** use centrifugal force; $d_{50}$ given by Lapple model: $\sqrt{9 \mu B / (\pi N_e v_i (\rho_p - \rho_g))}$.
- **Cyclone pressure drop** = $\xi \rho_g v_i^2 / 2$ with $\xi \in [4, 8]$.
- **Wet scrubbers** rely on inertial impaction; higher $Stk$ → higher efficiency.
- **Venturi scrubbers** can capture submicron particles at the cost of high $\Delta P$.
- **Baghouses** filter via cake build-up; pulse-jet cleaning is modern standard.
- **ESP migration velocity** is the key design parameter; influenced by particle resistivity.
- **Deutsch-Anderson:** $\eta = 1 - \exp(-A\omega / Q)$ — the most important ESP formula.
- **Particle resistivity** governs ESP performance: $10^{10}$ to $10^{11}$ Ω·cm is optimal.
- **Back corona** occurs when resistivity > $10^{11}$ Ω·cm — causes efficiency collapse.
- **Comparison metric:** Efficiency ranking ESP ≈ Baghouse > Venturi > Cyclone > Settling.
- **Pressure drop ranking:** Settling < ESP < Cyclone < Baghouse < Venturi (general).
- **Hot-gas applications** favor ESP and cyclone (no liquid); fabric limited by fiber material.
- **Sticky/explosive dust** favors dry ESP or baghouse (no water; inert medium).
- **Re-entrainment** is a key loss mechanism in both cyclones and ESPs — controlled by hopper design and rapping intensity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control devices for gaseous contaminants: adsorption, absorption, condensation, combustion, automobile emission control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT613 - PROCESS DESIGN FOR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Control devices for gaseous contaminants: adsorption, absorption, condensation, combustion, automobile emission control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Gaseous Contaminant Control Devices

**Gaseous contaminant control devices** are engineered systems designed to capture, transform, or destroy airborne pollutants (VOCs, SO₂, NOₓ, CO, H₂S, Hg vapor, hydrocarbons, etc.) released from stationary or mobile sources before they enter the atmosphere. The five principal technologies covered under the KTU 2024 syllabus are **Adsorption, Absorption, Condensation, Combustion (Incineration), and Automobile Emission Control** (typically via catalytic converters).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A *control device* is a unit operation or process that alters the physical, chemical, or biological character of a gaseous waste stream to reduce the emission of regulated pollutants to levels stipulated by the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** or the **MoEFCC** norms.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine the polluted flue gas leaving a factory as a **"dirty river"** carrying invisible chemical "garbage." Just as you would purify a river by:

- **Passing it through a sponge (Adsorption)** – sticky surface traps molecules.
- **Pouring it through a water-shower tower (Absorption)** – water chemically dissolves gases.
- **Cooling it down so droplets form (Condensation)** – like a cold drink glass collecting water on a hot day.
- **Burning the garbage in a furnace (Combustion)** – destroying organics into CO₂ + H₂O.
- **Putting a catalytic "muffler" on a car's tailpipe (Automobile Emission Control)** – converts toxic CO/HC/NOₓ to harmless gases.

> [!NOTE]
> The universal performance metric for these devices is the **Removal Efficiency (η)**, defined as:
> 
> $$\eta = \frac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100\ \%$$
> 
> where $C_{\text{in}}$ and $C_{\text{out}}$ are the inlet and outlet pollutant concentrations (typically in **mg/m³** or **ppmv**).

## 1.3 Selection Logic — Why Different Devices?

| Pollutant Type | Preferred Technology | Key Driver |
|----------------|---------------------|------------|
| VOCs, hydrocarbons | Adsorption / Combustion | High recovery vs. destruction |
| SO₂, HCl, NH₃ | Absorption (scrubbing) | Chemical reactivity with water |
| High-boiling organics | Condensation | Recoverable as liquid product |
| Toxic organics, odors | Combustion (Thermal/ Catalytic) | Complete destruction to CO₂ |
| Vehicle exhaust (CO, HC, NOₓ) | Three-way catalytic converter | Simultaneous redox reactions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Recap:** Always express gas-phase concentrations in standard conditions: **0 °C, 1 atm (STP) = 1.01325 × 10⁵ Pa** OR **25 °C, 1 atm (SATP)**. Conversion uses the **Ideal Gas Law** $PV = nRT$ with $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Adsorption

**Adsorption** is a surface phenomenon where gas molecules (adsorbate) adhere to the surface of a solid (adsorbent) due to **van der Waals forces** (physisorption) or **chemical bonds** (chemisorption).

- **Common adsorbents:** Activated carbon (most common for VOCs), zeolites (for NOₓ), silica gel (for moisture), activated alumina.
- **Operation:** Two-bed system — one adsorbs while the other is regenerated (desorbed) by hot steam or hot gas.
- **Key design parameter:** *Breakthrough curve* — plots $C_{\text{out}}/C_{\text{in}}$ vs. time. The breakthrough point is typically taken at $C_{\text{out}}/C_{\text{in}} = 0.05$ (5 %).

**Why?** Activated carbon has a surface area of **500–1500 m²/g**, providing enormous capacity for VOC capture.

## 2.2 Absorption (Gas Absorption / Scrubbing)

**Absorption** transfers a soluble gas component from the gas phase into a liquid solvent. It follows **two-film theory** by Whitman and Lewis.

- **Equipment:** Packed tower (Pall rings, Raschig rings) or sieve-plate tower.
- **Solvent selection:** Water (for SO₂ with alkali), NaOH solution, amines (for H₂S, CO₂), etc.
- **Equilibrium relation:** *Henry's Law* at low concentrations.

**Why?** Highly efficient (90–99 %) for water-soluble acid gases; operates continuously without regeneration cycles (if solvent is spent).

## 2.3 Condensation

**Condensation** recovers vapors by cooling below their **dew point** or by increasing pressure. The recovered condensate is a usable product (e.g., solvent recovery).

- **Types:** Surface condensation (shell-and-tube) and Contact condensation (direct mixing).
- **Applicability:** VOCs with high boiling points (>38 °C), e.g., toluene, xylene, mercury vapor.

**Why?** Often used as a *pre-treatment* before adsorption/absorption to reduce load on downstream devices.

## 2.4 Combustion (Incineration)

**Combustion** oxidizes combustible VOCs/CO to **CO₂ and H₂O** at high temperature.

- **Thermal incinerator:** 700–1100 °C, residence time 0.5–2 s.
- **Catalytic incinerator:** 300–500 °C (with Pt/Pd catalyst), residence time ~0.1 s.
- **Key metric:** Destruction and Removal Efficiency (DRE) ≥ **99.99 %** for hazardous waste incinerators (per CPCB).

**Why?** Complete destruction; ideal for concentrated, non-recoverable organics.

## 2.5 Automobile Emission Control

A modern gasoline vehicle uses a **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)** containing:
- **Reduction catalyst:** Rhodium / Platinum (reduces NOₓ → N₂).
- **Oxidation catalyst:** Palladium / Platinum (oxidizes CO → CO₂, HC → CO₂ + H₂O).

The engine is operated near the **stoichiometric air–fuel ratio (A/F ≈ 14.6)** to enable simultaneous oxidation and reduction. An **oxygen sensor (lambda sensor)** in the exhaust maintains this ratio via closed-loop feedback to the ECU.

> [!NOTE]
> **Lambda (λ) = (Actual A/F) / (Stoichiometric A/F)**. For gasoline, λ = 1 at stoichiometry; λ > 1 = lean (excess air); λ < 1 = rich (excess fuel). The TWC window for efficient operation is **0.99 ≤ λ ≤ 1.01**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Variables / Units | Application |
|---|----------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | $C$ in mg/m³ or ppmv | All devices |
| 2 | $q = \dfrac{q_{\max} K_L C}{1 + K_L C}$ (Langmuir) | $q$ = loading (g/g), $C$ = equilibrium conc. (mg/L) | Adsorption isotherm |
| 3 | $q = K_F \, C^{1/n}$ (Freundlich) | $K_F$, $n$ are empirical constants | Adsorption isotherm |
| 4 | $y = m \, x$ (Henry's Law) | $y, x$ = mole fractions in gas & liquid; $m$ = Henry's constant | Absorption equilibrium |
| 5 | $N_A = k_G (y - y^*) = k_L (x^* - x)$ (Two-film) | $k_G$, $k_L$ = mass-transfer coefficients | Absorption rate |
| 6 | $\ln P^{\text{sat}} = A - \dfrac{B}{T + C}$ (Antoine) | $P^{\text{sat}}$ in mmHg, $T$ in °C | Condensation |
| 7 | $H_u = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ (Heat of combustion) | $m$ = mass flow (kg/s), $C_p$ in kJ/kg·K | Combustion |
| 8 | $\text{DRE} = \dfrac{W_{\text{in}} - W_{\text{out}}}{W_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | $W$ = mass of toxic compound | Combustion |
| 9 | $\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | dimensionless | Automobile |
| 10 | $Z = H_{\text{OG}} \cdot N_{\text{OG}}$ (Packed tower height) | $H_{\text{OG}}$ = HTU (m), $N_{\text{OG}}$ = NTU | Absorption design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion tip (KTU favorite):** 1 ppmv ≈ $\dfrac{M}{24.45}$ mg/m³ at 25 °C, where $M$ is the molecular weight (g/mol) and 24.45 L/mol is the molar volume at 25 °C.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Two-Film Theory Mass-Transfer Flux (Absorption)

The **two-film model** (Lewis-Whitman, 1924) assumes stagnant gas and liquid films at the interface, with steady-state molecular diffusion through each.

**Step 1 — Define flux through gas film:**

$$N_A = -D_G \, \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

where $D_G$ = gas-phase diffusivity (m²/s), $C_A$ = concentration of A (kmol/m³).

**Step 2 — Integrate across gas film of thickness $\delta_G$:**

Assuming linear concentration drop across the film:

$$N_A = \frac{D_G}{\delta_G} (C_{A,b} - C_{A,i}) = k_G (y - y_i)$$

where $k_G = D_G / (\delta_G \cdot C_T)$ is the gas-side mass-transfer coefficient and $C_T$ is total molar concentration.

**Step 3 — Repeat for liquid film:**

$$N_A = \frac{D_L}{\delta_L} (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b}) = k_L (x_i - x)$$

**Step 4 — Combine using overall coefficient $K_G$:**

By adding resistances in series analogy:

$$\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}$$

The overall driving force equation becomes:

$$N_A = K_G (y - y^*) = K_G (y - m x)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into plug-flow design equation:**

For a packed tower of cross-section $S$ and height $Z$, the differential absorption rate is:

$$d(G \cdot y) = -K_G (y - y^*) \, a \, S \, dZ$$

where $G$ = molar gas flow (kmol/s), $a$ = interfacial area per unit volume (m²/m³).

**Step 6 — Integrate to obtain HTU-NTU form:**

After algebraic manipulation (introducing $G_s = G/S$ and $L_s = L/S$ molar fluxes):

$$Z = \int_{y_2}^{y_1} \frac{G_s \, dy}{K_y \, a \, (1 + m \cdot G_s/L_s) (y - y^*)} = H_{\text{OG}} \cdot N_{\text{OG}}$$

where the **Height of a Transfer Unit** is:

$$H_{\text{OG}} = \frac{G_s}{K_y \, a \, (1 + m G_s/L_s)}$$

and the **Number of Transfer Units** is:

$$N_{\text{OG}} = \int_{y_2}^{y_1} \frac{dy}{(1 + m G_s/L_s)(y - y^*)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** $H_{\text{OG}}$ is determined experimentally for a given packing; $N_{\text{OG}}$ is computed from operating and equilibrium lines (graphical or numerical integration). This is the *core design equation* KTU examiners love to test.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Langmuir Isotherm from Statistical Mechanics (Adsorption)

The Langmuir isotherm assumes:
- Monolayer coverage on a homogeneous surface.
- Each site holds one adsorbate molecule.
- No interaction between adsorbed molecules.

**Step 1 — Rate of adsorption $\propto$ bare sites and gas pressure:**

$$r_{\text{ads}} = k_a \, P \, (1 - \theta)$$

where $\theta$ = fractional surface coverage.

**Step 2 — Rate of desorption $\propto$ occupied sites:**

$$r_{\text{des}} = k_d \, \theta$$

**Step 3 — At equilibrium, $r_{\text{ads}} = r_{\text{des}}$:**

$$k_a \, P \, (1 - \theta) = k_d \, \theta$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\theta$:**

$$k_a P - k_a P \theta = k_d \theta$$

$$k_a P = \theta (k_d + k_a P)$$

$$\theta = \frac{k_a P}{k_d + k_a P} = \frac{K_L P}{1 + K_L P}$$

where $K_L = k_a / k_d$ is the Langmuir equilibrium constant (atm⁻¹).

**Step 5 — Express as loading $q$:**

Since $q = q_{\max} \theta$:

$$\boxed{q = \frac{q_{\max} K_L P}{1 + K_L P}}$$

When $P \to 0$: $q \approx q_{\max} K_L P$ (linear, Henry-like). When $P \to \infty$: $q \to q_{\max}$ (saturation).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Automobile)

For complete combustion of gasoline approximated as **iso-octane C₈H₁₈**:

**Step 1 — Write balanced equation:**

$$\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + a\,\text{O}_2 + 3.76\,a\,\text{N}_2 \rightarrow 8\,\text{CO}_2 + 9\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 3.76\,a\,\text{N}_2$$

**Step 2 — Balance oxygen:** O on right = $16 + 9 = 25$; O on left = $2a$.

$$2a = 25 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = 12.5$$

**Step 3 — Molecular weights:**

- Fuel (C₈H₁₈): $M_f = 8(12) + 18(1) = 114\ \text{g/mol}$
- Air: $M_a = 28 \times 0.79 + 32 \times 0.21 = 28.84\ \text{g/mol}$

**Step 4 — Mass of air required per kg fuel:**

$$\text{Air mass} = a \times (1 + 3.76) \times M_a = 12.5 \times 4.76 \times 28.84 = 1716\ \text{g}$$

**Step 5 — Stoichiometric A/F ratio:**

$$\boxed{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{1716}{114} \approx 15.05}$$

For *real gasoline* (paraffinic average C₇H₁₆), the textbook value is **14.6–14.7** (slight variation due to aromatic content and additives like MTBE).

**Step 6 — Lambda relationship:**

$$\lambda = \frac{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}{14.6}$$

If actual A/F = 12.5, then $\lambda = 0.856$ (rich mixture — high CO and HC in exhaust, low NOₓ).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Control Technologies

| Parameter | Adsorption | Absorption | Condensation | Combustion | Auto Cat. Conv. |
|-----------|------------|------------|--------------|------------|-----------------|
| Phase change | No | No | Yes (gas→liq) | No | No |
| Operating T (°C) | 20–50 | 20–60 | −20 to 40 | 700–1100 | 400–800 |
| Removal η (%) | 90–95 | 90–99 | 50–90 | 95–99.99 | 90–98 |
| Best for | VOCs | SO₂, HCl, H₂S | High-b.p. VOCs | Toxic organics | CO, HC, NOₓ |
| Capital cost | High | Medium | Medium | High | Low (mass prod.) |
| Recovers material? | Yes | No (spent liq) | Yes | No | No |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Adsorption — Two-Bed Regenerative System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InletFlue[Inlet Flue Gas] --> ValveA[Inlet Valve A]
    ValveA --> Bed1[Adsorber Bed 1 ACTIVE]
    Bed1 --> ValveB[Outlet Valve B]
    ValveB --> CleanOut[Clean Gas to Stack]
    
    SteamIn[Steam In for Regeneration] --> ValveC[Inlet Valve C]
    ValveC --> Bed2[Adsorber Bed 2 REGENERATING]
    Bed2 --> Cond[Condenser]
    Cond --> Sep[Liquid Product Separator]
    Sep --> ValveD[Outlet Valve D]
    ValveD --> RecycleStream[Recycle/Recovered Solvent]
    
    Bed1 -.saturated.-> Swap1[Switchover Logic]
    Swap1 -.triggers.-> Bed2
    Bed2 -.regenerated.-> Swap1
```

## 4.2 Absorption — Counter-Current Packed Tower

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GasIn[Polluted Gas In] --> Bottom[Gas Inlet at Bottom]
    Bottom --> PackedSection[Packed Section with Raschig Rings]
    PackedSection --> MistEl[Mist Eliminator]
    MistEl --> CleanGas[Clean Gas Out to Stack]
    
    LiqIn[Fresh Liquid Solvent In] --> TopDist[Liquid Distributor at Top]
    TopDist --> PackedSection
    PackedSection --> BottomSump[Tower Sump]
    BottomSump --> PumpOut[Rich Solvent Pump Out]
    PumpOut --> Regen[Solvent Regeneration Unit]
    Regen --> LeanBack[Lean Solvent Return]
    LeanBack --> TopDist
```

## 4.3 Condensation — Surface Condenser with Refrigeration Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HotGas[Hot Vapor Stream In] --> PreCool[Pre Cooler]
    PreCool --> SurfaceCond[Shell and Tube Surface Condenser]
    SurfaceCond --> RefrigLoop[Refrigerant Loop at 5 deg C]
    SurfaceCond --> LiqOut[Condensed Liquid Recovery]
    LiqOut --> Decanter[Decanter or Distillation]
    Decanter --> Product[Pure Recovered Solvent]
    
    SurfaceCond --> NonCond[Non Condensable Gas to Adsorber or Incinerator]
```

## 4.4 Combustion — Thermal Incinerator with Heat Recovery

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VOCStream[VOC Laden Air In] --> Burner[Natural Gas Burner or Electric Heater]
    Burner --> CombChamber[Combustion Chamber 750 to 1100 deg C]
    CombChamber --> ResTime[Residence Time 0.5 to 2 s]
    ResTime --> HX[Primary Heat Exchanger Heat Recovery]
    HX --> Quench[Quench Section to 100 deg C]
    Quench --> Stack[Exhaust to Stack]
    
    CombChamber --> Secondary[Secondary Burner if LEL based]
    HX --> HotAir[Hot Air Pre Heats Inlet Stream]
    HotAir --> VOCStream
```

## 4.5 Automobile Emission Control — Three-Way Catalytic Converter with Closed-Loop ECU

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AirIn[Air Intake] --> Throttle[Throttle Body]
    Throttle --> Engine[Internal Combustion Engine]
    Engine --> ExhMan[Exhaust Manifold]
    ExhMan --> O2Sens1[Oxygen Sensor 1 Pre Cat]
    O2Sens1 --> ECU[Engine Control Unit ECU]
    ECU -.feedback adjusts fuel.-> FuelInj[Fuel Injector]
    FuelInj --> Engine
    
    ExhMan --> TWC[Three Way Catalytic Converter]
    TWC --> Reduction[Reduction Catalyst Rh Pt NO to N2]
    TWC --> Oxidation[Oxidation Catalyst Pd Pt CO HC to CO2 H2O]
    Reduction --> Oxidation
    Oxidation --> O2Sens2[Oxygen Sensor 2 Post Cat]
    O2Sens2 --> ECU
    O2Sens2 --> Muffler[Muffler]
    Muffler --> TailPipe[Tail Pipe to Atmosphere]
```

## 4.6 Master Comparison — Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Identify Pollutant Type] --> Q1{Is it water soluble acid gas?}
    Q1 -->|Yes SO2 HCl NH3| Abs[Use Absorption Scrubbing]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is it a high value VOC to recover?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Boliling point above 38 C?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Cond[Use Condensation first]
    Q3 -->|No| Ads[Use Adsorption Activated Carbon]
    Q2 -->|No destruction preferred| Q4{Concentration above LEL?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| Comb[Use Thermal Incinerator with heat recovery]
    Q4 -->|No| Comb2[Use Catalytic Incinerator at 350 to 500 C]
    Start --> Q5{Is it vehicle exhaust CO HC NOx?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| Auto[Use Three Way Catalytic Converter lambda 1]
```
