KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Set] **Define photocatalysis. With a neat sketch, explain the working of TiO₂-mediated photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants.** **Model Answer:** Photocatalysis is the acceleration of a photoreaction in the presence of a **semiconductor catalyst** (e.g., TiO₂, ZnO, WO₃). When TiO₂ (band-gap energy = **3.2 eV** for anatase) absorbs a UV photon of wavelength $\lambda \leq 388\ \text{nm}$, an electron from the valence band (VB) is promoted to the conduction band (CB), generating an **electron–hole pair**: $$\text{TiO}_2 + h\nu \longrightarrow \text{e}^-_{CB} + \text{h}^+_{VB}$$ The hole oxidizes adsorbed water to produce the highly reactive **hydroxyl radical (·OH)**: $$\text{h}^+_{VB} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \cdot\text{OH} + \text{H}^+$$ The conduction-band electron reduces O₂ to superoxide: $$\text{e}^-_{CB} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{O}_2^{\cdot -}$$ Both ·OH and O₂·⁻ are powerful oxidants that **non-selectively mineralize** organic pollutants (R) to CO₂, H₂O, and mineral acids. ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Set] **Differentiate between bioaugmentation and biostimulation with one suitable example each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | **Bioaugmentation** | **Biostimulation** | |---|---|---| | Definition | Adding **exogenous microbial cultures** to the contaminated site | **Modifying environmental conditions** (nutrients, pH, electron acceptors) to enhance native microbial activity | | When applied | Site lacks competent indigenous degraders | Site already has capable microbes but activity is limited by nutrients or conditions | | Example | Adding *Pseudomonas putida* to a TCE-contaminated aquifer | Adding N-P-K fertilizer and oxygen to an oil-spill beach to stimulate *Alcanivorax borkumensis* | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Model Set] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **mechanism of microbial degradation of petroleum hydrocarbons** with a focus on aerobic pathways. Discuss the role of **biosurfactants** in enhancing bioavailability and provide the **electron acceptor hierarchy**. **(b) [7 Marks]** A batch photocatalytic reactor treats **5 L** of dye-laden wastewater with initial concentration **120 mg/L**. After **45 minutes** under UV-A/TiO₂, the concentration drops to **30 mg/L**. Assuming **pseudo-first-order kinetics**, calculate: **(i)** the rate constant $k_{\text{obs}}$ in min⁻¹, **(ii)** the half-life $t_{1/2}$, and **(iii)** the time for **98% removal**. --- ### Model Solution — Question A (a) **Mechanism of Aerobic Hydrocarbon Degradation:** 1. **Uptake of n-alkanes** — *Alcanivorax* and *Pseudomonas* species use *alkB* gene-encoded **alkane monooxygenase** to convert alkanes to primary alcohols. 2. **Oxidation cascade:** primary alcohol → aldehyde → fatty acid via alcohol dehydrogenase and aldehyde dehydrogenase. 3. **β-oxidation** in the cytoplasm — fatty acids are cleaved into acetyl-CoA units in the TCA cycle. 4. **Aromatic ring cleavage** — *Pseudomonas* uses *tod* and *cat* pathway dioxygenases (catechol 1,2-dioxygenase) for ortho- or meta-cleavage. 5. **Final mineralization:** CO₂ + H₂O + new biomass. **Role of Biosurfactants:** Hydrocarbons are hydrophobic (log $K_{ow} > 4$). Microbes secrete **rhamnolipids**, **sophorolipids**, or **surfactin** which: - Reduce **interfacial tension** from ~30 mN/m to < 1 mN/m. - Form **micelles** with hydrocarbon cores, increasing apparent aqueous solubility by 100–1000×. - Enhance **bioavailability** and mass-transfer rate. **Electron Acceptor Hierarchy (Thermodynamic preference, high to low energy yield):** $$\text{O}_2 \;>\; \text{NO}_3^- \;>\; \text{Mn}^{4+} \;>\; \text{Fe}^{3+} \;>\; \text{SO}_4^{2-} \;>\; \text{CO}_2$$ Engineers add **electron acceptors** (O₂ via aeration; NO₃⁻ via nitrate injection) to push the system into the most favorable pathway. --- ### Model Solution — Question A (b) **Given:** $C_0 = 120\ \text{mg/L}$, $C_t = 30\ \text{mg/L}$, $t = 45\ \text{min}$, $V = 5\ \text{L}$ (volume not needed for kinetic calculation). **(i) Rate constant:** $$\ln\left(\frac{C_0}{C_t}\right) = k_{\text{obs}} \cdot t$$ $$\ln\left(\frac{120}{30}\right) = \ln(4) = 1.3863 = k_{\text{obs}} \cdot 45$$ $$k_{\text{obs}} = \frac{1.3863}{45} = 0.0308\ \text{min}^{-1}$$ **(ii) Half-life:** $$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k_{\text{obs}}} = \frac{0.693}{0.0308} = 22.5\ \text{min}$$ **(iii) Time for 98% removal** (i.e., $C_t = 0.02 \cdot C_0 = 2.4\ \text{mg/L}$, so $C_0/C_t = 50$): $$\ln(50) = 0.0308 \cdot t$$ $$3.912 = 0.0308 \cdot t$$ $$t = \frac{3.912}{0.0308} = 127.0\ \text{min}$$ **Answer:** $k_{\text{obs}} = 0.0308\ \text{min}^{-1}$, $t_{1/2} = 22.5\ \text{min}$, $t_{98\%} = 127\ \text{min}$. --- ### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Alternative Set] **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **synthesis, properties, and environmental applications of nano-zero-valent iron (nZVI)**. With balanced chemical equations, show how nZVI remediates **trichloroethylene (TCE)** and **hexavalent chromium Cr(VI)**. **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **mechanism of phytoremediation** with a labeled block diagram. Differentiate between **phytoextraction, phytodegradation, and phytovolatilization** and list **two suitable plant species** for each. --- ### Model Solution — Question B (a) **Synthesis of nZVI:** 1. **Borohydride reduction (most common):** $$\text{Fe}^{2+} + 2\text{BH}_4^- + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Fe}^0 \downarrow + 2\text{B(OH)}_3 + 7\text{H}_2 \uparrow$$ 2. **Green synthesis** (using plant extracts as reducing agents — *Camellia sinensis*, eucalyptus leaf) — produces nZVI of 10–100 nm. 3. **Thermal decomposition** of Fe(CO)₅ in surfactant media. **Properties:** - Particle size: **10–100 nm**; high specific surface area **20–50 m²/g**. - Core–shell structure: Fe⁰ core with Fe₃O₄/FeOOH shell. - **Superparamagnetic** when below 20 nm. - Strong reducing agent: $E^0(\text{Fe}^{2+}/\text{Fe}^0) = -0.44\ \text{V}$. **Environmental Applications:** chlorinated solvent DNAPL plumes, Cr(VI) reduction, As(III) oxidation coupled with adsorption, heavy-metal removal. **Reactions:** For TCE (β-elimination pathway): $$\text{C}_2\text{HCl}_3 + 3\text{Fe}^0 + 3\text{H}^+ \longrightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + 3\text{Fe}^{2+} + 3\text{Cl}^-$$ For Cr(VI) reduction (in acidic groundwater): $$\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-} + 2\text{Fe}^0 + 14\text{H}^+ \longrightarrow 2\text{Cr}^{3+} + 2\text{Fe}^{3+} + 7\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ The Cr³⁺ precipitates as Cr(OH)₃ at pH > 5.5, achieving **immobilization**. --- ### Model Solution — Question B (b) **Phytoremediation Mechanism:** Plants uptake, accumulate, translocate, sequester, or transform contaminants via their root–shoot systems. The process is divided into: | Sub-process | Mechanism | Plant Examples | |---|---|---| | **Phytoextraction** | Roots absorb metals → translocate to shoots → harvest shoot biomass | *Brassica juncea* (Indian mustard) for Pb; *Arabidopsis halleri* for Cd/Zn | | **Phytodegradation** | Plant-derived enzymes (dehalogenase, nitroreductase, laccase) cleave organics | *Populus* sp. for TCE; *Myriophyllum spicatum* for atrazine | | **Phytovolatilization** | Contaminant converted to volatile form and released through stomata | *Brassica juncea* for Se → dimethyl selenide; transgenic *Arabidopsis* for Hg → Hg⁰ | | **Phytostabilization** | Roots adsorb/precipitate contaminants, preventing leaching | *Agrostis tenuis* for As; *Festuca arundinacea* for Pb | | **Rhizofiltration** | Roots in solution adsorb dissolved metals | *Sunflower* (*Helianthus annuus*) for Cs, Sr, Cr; *Pteris vittata* (Chinese brake fern) for As | **Block diagram (to be drawn in the answer sheet):** - Block 1: Contaminant source - Block 2: Root zone uptake - Block 3: Translocation (xylem) - Block 4: Leaf/shoot accumulation or volatilization - Block 5: Harvest and disposal (for phytoextraction) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Loss:** > 1. **Confusing bioaugmentation vs biostimulation** — examiners specifically test this. Memorize the table. > 2. **Wrong band-gap of TiO₂** — it is **3.2 eV (anatase)**, not 3.0 eV (rutile). Wrong value = −1 mark. > 3. **Forgetting to balance chemical equations** for nZVI reactions. Always show stoichiometric coefficients. > 4. **Writing $C_0 - C_e$ as $C_0/C_e$** in adsorption capacity formula — this is a recurring error. > 5. **Missing the half-life derivation** — KTU often asks "show that $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k$" — derive, don't quote. > 6. **Using unit "min⁻¹" for $k$** without showing dimensional analysis — examiners look for unit clarity. > 7. **Confusing *phytoextraction* with *phytostabilization*** — students mix up "uptake" and "immobilization." --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:** - [ ] **Biotechnology** = use of *biological agents* (microbes, plants, enzymes) for pollutant removal. *Bioremediation* is the operative engineering term. - [ ] **Microbial pathways** are *aerobic* (O₂ acceptor, fast) or *anaerobic* (NO₃⁻, SO₄²⁻, CO₂ acceptors, slower but capable of dechlorination). - [ ] **Bioaugmentation** = add microbes. **Biostimulation** = feed native microbes. - [ ] **Phytoremediation** requires **BAF > 1** and **TF > 1** for hyperaccumulator status. - [ ] **Nanotechnology** exploits *1–100 nm* scale → SA/V scales as **3/r**. - [ ] **TiO₂ band gap = 3.2 eV** (anatase) → activated by $\lambda \leq 388\ \text{nm}$ (UV-A). - [ ] **Photocatalysis** generates **·OH** (redox potential +2.80 V) and **O₂·⁻** that mineralize organics. - [ ] **Pseudo-first-order:** $\ln(C_0/C_t) = k_{\text{obs}} t$; **half-life:** $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k$. - [ ] **Langmuir–Hinshelwood** is the standard kinetic model; at low $C$, it reduces to pseudo-first-order. - [ ] **nZVI** is Fe⁰ (10–100 nm, surface area 20–50 m²/g); reduces TCE to ethene and Cr(VI) to Cr(III). - [ ] **CNT and GO** are excellent *adsorbents* for dyes, heavy metals; functionalization (−COOH, −OH) enhances capacity. - [ ] **Magnetic nanoparticles (Fe₃O₄)** allow *targeted recovery* using external magnets. - [ ] **Photon energy:** $E = hc/\lambda$; **cut-off wavelength** for a semiconductor: $\lambda_{\max} = 1240/E_g$ (in nm, with $E_g$ in eV). - [ ] **Integrated train** = bio-process (bulk removal) → nano-membrane (refining) → photocatalysis (polishing). - [ ] **Sustainable advantage:** Bio + Nano systems produce **CO₂, H₂O, and reusable biomass** as by-products — minimal secondary waste.

topics.title: Threats and pollution caused by renewable energy technologies (PV cells and batteries)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT613 - PROCESS DESIGN FOR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Threats and pollution caused by renewable energy technologies (PV cells and batteries)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Renewable energy technologies** are systems that convert naturally replenishing energy sources (solar, wind, hydro, biomass, geothermal) into usable electrical, thermal, or mechanical energy. While classified as *clean energy* during the **operational phase**, their **life-cycle impacts** — spanning raw material extraction, manufacturing, deployment, operation, and end-of-life disposal — generate significant environmental burdens.

**Photovoltaic (PV) cells** are solid-state semiconductor devices that convert incident solar radiation directly into electricity via the **photovoltaic effect** (typically using crystalline silicon, CdTe, CIGS, or perovskite absorbers).

**Batteries** are electrochemical energy storage devices that store and release electrical energy through reversible redox reactions. For renewable integration, the dominant chemistries are **lithium-ion (LiB)**, **lead-acid**, **sodium-sulfur (NaS)**, **vanadium redox flow (VRFB)**, and emerging **solid-state** systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Green Energy ≠ Pollution-Free"**: The pollution burden of renewables is *upstream* (mining, refining) and *downstream* (waste), not *operational* (during electricity generation). This is the central paradox the KTU module tests.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Iceberg Analogy for Renewable Pollution**
>
> Imagine a PV solar panel as an iceberg floating in the ocean. The visible tip (the operational phase — silent, emission-free, 25–30 years of clean electricity) looks pristine. But **90% of the iceberg's mass lies beneath the surface** — this is the *embodied* pollution: the silicon quarries in China, the hydrofluoric acid baths in Malaysia, the cobalt mines in the DRC, and the un-recycled panels piling up in Kasauli, India. The same applies to lithium-ion batteries — the EV on the road looks clean, but the lithium brine pools in the Atacama Desert, the lithium-ion battery fires in shipping containers, and the lead smelting in recycling yards are all *invisible environmental costs*.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Crystalline silicon bandgap** $E_g = 1.12 \text{ eV}$
- **Solar constant** $G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$
- **AM1.5 standard spectrum** peak irradiance = **1000 W/m²**
- **TCLP (Toxicity Characteristic Leaching Procedure) regulatory threshold for lead** = **5 mg/L**
- **TCLP threshold for cadmium** = **1 mg/L**
- **Energy Payback Time (EPBT)** for modern c-Si PV = **1.5 – 2.5 years**
- **Lithium-ion thermal runaway onset temperature** ≈ **80 – 120 °C**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Syllabus Highlight:** The course explicitly asks students to critically evaluate the *net environmental benefit* of renewables by accounting for life-cycle emissions, hazardous material handling, and end-of-life management.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pollution Inventory Across the PV Life-Cycle

**Stage 1 — Raw Material Extraction & Refining (Most Polluting)**
- **High-purity quartz mining** for silicon feedstock.
- **Energy-intensive polysilicon production** via the **Siemens process**.
- **Heavy metal extraction** for thin-film technologies (Cd, Te, In, Ga, Se).

**Stage 2 — Cell & Module Manufacturing**
- Wet-chemical etching using **HF (hydrofluoric acid)**, **HCl**, **HNO₃**, **NaOH**.
- Surface texturing with **KOH/IPA solutions**.
- **PECVD deposition** of SiNₓ using **NH₃** and **SiH₄** (silane) — both toxic/pyrophoric.
- Screen-printing of silver and aluminum pastes (Ag⁺ and Al³⁺ wastewater).
- Lead-bearing solder (SnPb) for ribbon interconnection.

**Stage 3 — Operation**
- Direct emissions ≈ **zero**.
- Indirect: land-use change, albedo modification.

**Stage 4 — End-of-Life (E-Waste Stream)**
- **30+ year life** means first-generation waste is arriving *now*.
- IEA estimates **78 million tonnes** of PV waste by 2050.
- Leaching of Pb, Cd, Se, Ga into **soil → groundwater → food chain**.

### 2.2 Pollution Inventory Across the Battery Life-Cycle

**Stage 1 — Critical Mineral Extraction**
- **Lithium**: Brine extraction (16,000–22,000 L water per ton Li) or hard-rock spodumene mining.
- **Cobalt**: Often from *Cobalt Blue Belt* (DRC) — acid mine drainage (AMD), child labor concerns.
- **Nickel**: Laterite (saprolite) ore; produces chromium-laden waste.
- **Graphite**: Carbon refinement.

**Stage 2 — Cell Manufacturing**
- **NMP (N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone)** as PVDF binder solvent — a reproductive toxin.
- **Electrolyte salts**: **LiPF₆** → hydrolyzes to **HF** on contact with moisture.
- **Carbonate solvents** (EC, DMC, EMC, DEC) — volatile, flammable.
- **Cleanroom fabrication** — massive energy load (low-carbon footprint *only* in clean grids).

**Stage 3 — Operation**
- Zero direct emissions.
- Degradation: capacity fade, swelling, dendrite formation.

**Stage 4 — End-of-Life**
- **Thermal runaway** → fires, venting of **HF, CO, CO₂, C₂H₄, CH₄**.
- **Shredding dust** — fine particulate (Li, Co, Ni, Mn oxides) — **occupational hazard**.
- Recycling rate globally ≈ **< 5%** for LiBs.

### 2.3 Why "Green" is a Misnomer — the "Hidden Carbon" Argument

- **Embodied energy** of c-Si PV ≈ **400 – 600 kWh/m²**.
- **Manufacture of 1 kg polysilicon** generates ≈ **25–35 kg CO₂-eq**.
- The **carbon payback time** is short only if the panel is installed in high-irradiance regions (Rajasthan, Saudi Arabia) — longer in cloudy climates (Kerala, Northern Europe).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Definition | Expression / Value | Units |
|--------|------------|--------------------|-------|
| $EPBT$ | Energy Payback Time | $EPBT = \dfrac{E_{LC}}{E_{gen,annual}}$ | years |
| $E_{LC}$ | Life-cycle primary energy demand | $E_{LC} = E_{manuf} + E_{inst} + E_{EoL}$ | kWh |
| $E_{gen,annual}$ | Annual electrical output | $\eta_{sys} \cdot H \cdot A$ | kWh/yr |
| $\eta_{sys}$ | System (overall) efficiency | $\eta_{PV} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{BOS}$ | dimensionless |
| $H$ | Annual avg. global horizontal irradiance (GHI) | 1500–2200 (India) | kWh/m²/yr |
| $A$ | Module area | — | m² |
| $CPBT$ | Carbon Payback Time | $CPBT = \dfrac{CO_{2,LC}}{CO_{2,avoided,annual}}$ | years |
| $CO_{2,avoided}$ | Annual CO₂ displaced | $E_{gen,annual} \cdot EF_{grid}$ | kg CO₂/yr |
| $EF_{grid}$ | Grid emission factor | 0.7 (India 2022 avg.) | kg CO₂/kWh |
| $LCA$ | Life-Cycle Assessment boundary | Cradle-to-Gate or Cradle-to-Grave | — |
| $TCLP$ | Toxicity Charact. Leaching Procedure | $C_{leachate}$ vs. $C_{threshold}$ | mg/L |
| $C_{lim, Pb}$ | Lead leaching limit | **5** | mg/L |
| $C_{lim, Cd}$ | Cadmium leaching limit | **1** | mg/L |
| $T_{TR}$ | Thermal runaway onset | 80–120 | °C |
| $\Delta H_{rxn}$ | Enthalpy of reaction (Siemens) | ≈ 178 (SiHCl₃ formation) | kJ/mol |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Engineering Utility:** These equations allow a process engineer to size a PV/battery installation while simultaneously computing its **net environmental footprint**. They are the foundation of any **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** submitted to India's **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC)**.

### 2.5 Real-World Industrial Relevance

- **Solar farms in Rajasthan** (Bhadla, Pavagada) require **land-use compensation** — ecological trade-off.
- **EV battery fires in shipping** (e.g., MV Fremantle Highway 2023) — **HF plume** threatened fire crews.
- **First Solar's CdTe recycling program** recovers **95% Cd, 90% Te** — a benchmark for circular design.
- **India's E-Waste Management Rules 2022** — extended producer responsibility (EPR) for PV and battery manufacturers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Payback Time (EPBT) for a c-Si PV Module

**Given system parameters** (standard KTU textbook-style problem):

- Module area $A = 1.6 \text{ m}^2$ (typical 400 Wp c-Si panel)
- Module efficiency $\eta_{PV} = 20\%$
- Inverter efficiency $\eta_{inv} = 96\%$
- Balance-of-system (BOS) efficiency $\eta_{BOS} = 90\%$
- Annual GHI at site $H = 1800 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{yr}$ (e.g., Rajasthan)
- Embodied energy $E_{LC} = 850 \text{ kWh/m}^2$ (cradle-to-gate average)

**Step 1 — Compute system efficiency**

$$
\eta_{sys} = \eta_{PV} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{BOS}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\eta_{sys} = 0.20 \times 0.96 \times 0.90 = 0.1728
$$

**Step 2 — Compute annual electrical generation per unit installed area**

$$
E_{gen,annual} = \eta_{sys} \cdot H
$$

$$
E_{gen,annual} = 0.1728 \times 1800 = 311.04 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{yr}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute EPBT**

$$
EPBT = \frac{E_{LC}}{E_{gen,annual}}
$$

$$
EPBT = \frac{850}{311.04} = 2.73 \text{ years}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** With a 30-year service life, the panel delivers ≈ **27.3 years of net clean energy** — a positive return. However, the 2.73 years of polluting energy must be sourced responsibly (e.g., hydropower for polysilicon refining in Yunnan, China).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stoichiometry of Polysilicon Production (Siemens Process)

The Siemens process produces high-purity electronic-grade silicon. We analyze the **toxic byproduct** — silicon tetrachloride (SiCl₄) — which is the *single largest atmospheric pollutant* in PV manufacturing.

**Overall reactions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reaction 1 (Carbothermic reduction of silica):} \quad \text{SiO}_2 + 2\text{C} &\rightarrow \text{Si} + 2\text{CO} \\
\text{Reaction 2 (Hydrochlorination of metallurgical Si):} \quad \text{Si} + 3\text{HCl} &\rightarrow \text{SiHCl}_3 + \text{H}_2 \\
\text{Reaction 3 (Siemens CVD deposition):} \quad \text{SiHCl}_3 + \text{H}_2 &\rightarrow \text{Si} + 3\text{HCl} \\
\text{Side reaction (chlorination of impurity Si):} \quad \text{Si} + 4\text{HCl} &\rightarrow \text{SiCl}_4 + 2\text{H}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric ratio for trichlorosilane**

From Reaction 2, **1 mol Si → 1 mol SiHCl₃**. Assume 1000 kg of metallurgical-grade Si (MGS) is fed and 70% reacts via Reaction 2 and 30% via Reaction 4.

**Step 2 — Mass of Si converted to SiHCl₃**

$$
m_{Si, TCS} = 1000 \times 0.70 = 700 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Moles of SiHCl₃ produced**

Molar mass of Si = 28.09 g/mol:

$$
n_{SiHCl_3} = \frac{700{,}000 \text{ g}}{28.09 \text{ g/mol}} = 24{,}922 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of SiHCl₃**

Molar mass of SiHCl₃ = 28.09 + 1.008 + 3(35.45) = 135.45 g/mol:

$$
m_{SiHCl_3} = 24{,}922 \times 135.45 = 3.376 \times 10^6 \text{ g} = 3376 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Mass of HCl consumed (Reaction 2, 3:1 molar ratio)**

$$
n_{HCl} = 3 \times 24{,}922 = 74{,}766 \text{ mol}
$$

Molar mass HCl = 36.46 g/mol:

$$
m_{HCl} = 74{,}766 \times 36.46 = 2726 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 6 — Mass of SiCl₄ byproduct (Reaction 4, 1:1 with Si)**

$$
m_{Si, SiCl_4} = 1000 \times 0.30 = 300 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
n_{SiCl_4} = \frac{300{,}000}{28.09} = 10{,}679 \text{ mol}
$$

Molar mass SiCl₄ = 28.09 + 4(35.45) = 169.89 g/mol:

$$
m_{SiCl_4} = 10{,}679 \times 169.89 = 1814 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 7 — HCl recovered from Reaction 3**

For every mole of SiHCl₃ that *deposits* as Si (CVD), 3 mol HCl is regenerated. If 80% of the SiHCl₃ from Step 3 is sent to the CVD reactor:

$$
n_{HCl, recovered} = 0.80 \times 3 \times 24{,}922 = 59{,}813 \text{ mol}
$$

$$
m_{HCl, recovered} = 59{,}813 \times 36.46 = 2181 \text{ kg}
$$

**Net HCl consumption:**

$$
m_{HCl, net} = 2726 - 2181 = 545 \text{ kg HCl per 1000 kg MGS}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Environmental consequence of SiCl₄:** SiCl₄ is hydrolyzed to **HCl (acid rain precursor)** and **SiO₂ (fine particulate)** upon atmospheric exposure. Uncontrolled release at PV manufacturing hubs in Xinjiang caused documented respiratory disease clusters. Modern plants run a *closed-loop* plasma converter: $\text{SiCl}_4 + \text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{SiHCl}_3 + \text{HCl}$, eliminating 95% of the waste.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Carbon Payback Time (CPBT) for PV vs. Indian Coal Grid

**Given:**

- Indian grid emission factor $EF_{grid} = 0.70 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq/kWh}$
- Panel area $A_{total} = 1000 \text{ m}^2$ (small solar farm)
- $E_{gen,annual} = 311.04 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{yr}$ (from §3.1)
- Life-cycle GHG emission of panel $GHG_{LC} = 40 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq/m}^2$

**Step 1 — Total annual generation**

$$
E_{gen,total} = 311.04 \times 1000 = 311{,}040 \text{ kWh/yr}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual CO₂ avoided**

$$
CO_{2,avoided} = 311{,}040 \times 0.70 = 217{,}728 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{yr}
$$

**Step 3 — Total life-cycle emissions**

$$
GHG_{total} = 40 \times 1000 = 40{,}000 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
$$

**Step 4 — CPBT**

$$
CPBT = \frac{GHG_{total}}{CO_{2,avoided}} = \frac{40{,}000}{217{,}728} = 0.184 \text{ years} \approx 67 \text{ days}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** The CPBT for PV in India is **dramatically shorter** than the EPBT because Indian coal is so carbon-intensive. This is precisely why India has a *strategic* (not just environmental) interest in scaling PV — geopolitical energy security.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cobalt Demand for Grid-Scale LiB Storage

**Given:**

- Target: 100 MWh grid storage
- NMC 622 cathode chemistry: Li(Ni₀.₆Mn₀.₂Co₀.₂)O₂
- Average specific energy: 180 Wh/kg (cell level)
- Co mass fraction in NMC 622: 0.20 (molar) × 58.93 g/mol

**Step 1 — Total mass of cells required**

$$
m_{cell} = \frac{100{,}000{,}000 \text{ Wh}}{180 \text{ Wh/kg}} = 555{,}556 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Cathode is ≈ 30% of cell mass** (typical)

$$
m_{cathode} = 0.30 \times 555{,}556 = 166{,}667 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Active material is ≈ 90% of cathode**

$$
m_{NMC} = 0.90 \times 166{,}667 = 150{,}000 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Cobalt mass**

$$
m_{Co} = 0.20 \times 150{,}000 = 30{,}000 \text{ kg} = 30 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 5 — At DRC ore grade of 0.3% Co and 50% recovery:**

$$
\text{Ore tonnage required} = \frac{30{,}000}{0.003 \times 0.5} = 20{,}000{,}000 \text{ kg} = 20{,}000 \text{ tonnes of ore}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Environmental implication:** Just 100 MWh of grid storage requires **20,000 tonnes of ore**, with proportional AMD, water consumption, and tailings. Scaling to GWh is environmentally non-trivial — this drives R&D into **LFP (LiFePO₄)** and **sodium-ion** chemistries, which eliminate Co entirely.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PV Cell Pollution Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Raw Quartz Mining] --> A2[Polysilicon Production Siemens Process]
    A2 --> A3[Wafer Slicing Wire Saw Slurry Waste]
    A3 --> A4[Cell Fabrication HF HCl NaOH Etching]
    A4 --> A5[Module Assembly Lead Solder EVA Encapsulant]
    A5 --> A6[Deployment Operational Phase Zero Direct Emissions]
    A6 --> A7[End of Life 25 to 30 Yrs Leaching Pb Cd]
    A7 --> A8[Landfill OR Recycling Stream]

    A2 -. SiCl4 byproduct .-> POLL1[Atmospheric HCl fine particulate]
    A4 -. Acid waste .-> POLL2[Wastewater fluorides chlorides]
    A5 -. Pb solder .-> POLL3[Pb leachate in landfill]
    A7 -. Heavy metals .-> POLL4[Groundwater contamination]
    A1 -. Land disturbance .-> POLL5[Habitat loss]

    POLL1 --> ENVT[Environmental Burden]
    POLL2 --> ENVT
    POLL3 --> ENVT
    POLL4 --> ENVT
    POLL5 --> ENVT
    A6 -. 30 yrs clean energy .-> BENEFIT[Net Climate Benefit]
    ENVT -. Cradle to grave LCA .-> NETB[Net Environmental Balance]
    BENEFIT --> NETB
```

### 4.2 Battery Pollution and Risk Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    B1[Critical Mineral Extraction Li Co Ni Mn Graphite] --> B2[Refining and Cathode Precursor Synthesis]
    B2 --> B3[Cell Manufacturing Cleanroom NMP LiPF6 EC DMC]
    B3 --> B4[Battery Pack Assembly BMS Integration]
    B4 --> B5[Deployment Storage in Renewable System]
    B5 --> B6[End of Life Recycling or Landfill]

    B1 -. Brine water use .-> RISK1[Water Stress Atacama Tibet]
    B1 -. Acid mine drainage .-> RISK2[AMD with heavy metals]
    B3 -. Electrolyte leak .-> RISK3[HF generation on moisture contact]
    B5 -. Thermal runaway .-> RISK4[Fire HF CO toxic vent gases]
    B6 -. Shredding dust .-> RISK5[Occupational Li Co Ni exposure]
    B2 -. Energy intensity .-> RISK6[CO2 footprint of precursor]

    RISK1 --> NETP[Net Environmental Burden]
    RISK2 --> NETP
    RISK3 --> NETP
    RISK4 --> NETP
    RISK5 --> NETP
    RISK6 --> NETP
    B5 -. Grid balancing service .-> NETB[Net Grid Benefit]
    NETP --> LCA[Full Cradle to Grave LCA]
    NETB --> LCA
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram of Pollution Control Strategies

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC[Pollution Source] --> A[Prevention Substitution LFP for NMC CdFree PV]
    SRC --> B[Process Control Closed loop SiCl4 plasma converter]
    SRC --> C[Capture Scrubbers for HF and HCl gases]
    SRC --> D[Treatment Effluent neutralization precipitation]
    D --> E[Recovery Cobalt Lithium recycling 95 percent]
    A --> OUT[Net Zero Discharge Goal]
    B --> OUT
    C --> OUT
    D --> OUT
    E --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-level interpretation:** The diagram maps the **mitigation hierarchy** — *prevent → control → capture → treat → recover* — that process engineers apply when designing pollution control systems for PV and battery plants (the core competency tested in OEBBT613).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any three toxic materials used in the manufacture of crystalline silicon photovoltaic cells and state one environmental hazard associated with each.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Hydrofluoric acid (HF)** used for surface texturing and edge isolation — acute toxicity, causes severe burns and pulmonary edema; lethal at concentrations as low as 100 ppm inhaled.
2. **Silicon tetrachloride (SiCl₄)** — byproduct of the Siemens process; atmospheric hydrolysis produces **HCl** (acid rain precursor) and fine **SiO₂** particulate (respiratory hazard).
3. **Lead (Pb) in solder** (SnPb ribbon interconnect) — at end-of-life, TCLP leachate exceeds 5 mg/L threshold, contaminating groundwater with neurotoxin (especially harmful to children).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain why lithium-ion batteries, despite enabling renewable energy storage, are considered a pollution concern.*

**Model Answer:**

Lithium-ion batteries are a pollution concern because:

- **Critical mineral extraction** (Li, Co, Ni) generates acid mine drainage, consumes massive water (≈ 16,000 L water per ton Li from brine), and damages ecosystems (e.g., Salar de Atacama, DRC cobalt belt).
- **Electrolyte salt LiPF₆** hydrolyzes to **HF** on moisture contact; venting during thermal runaway releases HF, CO, and flammable hydrocarbon gases (EC, DMC decomposition products).
- **End-of-life recycling rate is < 5% globally**, leading to landfill accumulation of Li, Co, Ni, Mn — all leachable heavy metals.
- **Occupational hazards** during shredding — fine particulate of transition metal oxides is a respiratory and carcinogenic risk.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module 4 Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block flow diagram, describe the various sources of pollution across the life-cycle stages of a crystalline silicon photovoltaic module. Identify the most polluting stage and justify.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 1.6 m² c-Si PV module has a system efficiency of 17.28% and is installed at a site with annual GHI of 1800 kWh/m²/yr. The cradle-to-gate embodied energy is 850 kWh/m². Compute the **energy payback time (EPBT)** and the **carbon payback time (CPBT)** assuming the Indian grid emission factor is 0.70 kg CO₂/kWh and life-cycle GHG emissions are 40 kg CO₂-eq/m².

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pollution sources across the PV life-cycle:**

| Life-Cycle Stage | Pollutant | Pathway |
|------------------|-----------|---------|
| Quartz mining | Land disturbance, dust | Habitat loss |
| Polysilicon (Siemens) | SiCl₄, HCl, CO, SiH₄ | Air/soil |
| Wafer slicing | SiC slurry, IPA, glycol | Wastewater |
| Cell fabrication | HF, HCl, HNO₃, NaOH, NH₃ | Wastewater/air |
| Module assembly | Pb solder, EVA off-gassing | Solid waste |
| EOL landfill | Pb, Cd, Se leaching | Groundwater |

**Most polluting stage:** *Polysilicon (Siemens) production* — energy intensive (>100 kWh/kg), emits SiCl₄ (a globally significant chemical waste), CO from carbothermic reduction, and is responsible for **~ 60% of the panel's embodied energy**.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

System efficiency $\eta_{sys} = 17.28\% = 0.1728$

Annual generation per m²:

$$
E_{gen} = 0.1728 \times 1800 = 311.04 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{yr}
$$

EPBT:

$$
EPBT = \frac{E_{LC}}{E_{gen}} = \frac{850}{311.04} = 2.73 \text{ years}
$$

Annual CO₂ avoided per m²:

$$
CO_{2,avoided} = 311.04 \times 0.70 = 217.73 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{m}^2/\text{yr}
$$

CPBT:

$$
CPBT = \frac{40}{217.73} = 0.184 \text{ years} \approx 67 \text{ days}
$$

**Result:** EPBT = 2.73 years; CPBT ≈ 67 days. CPBT << EPBT because the avoided CO₂ is calculated on India's coal-heavy grid.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the pollution threats associated with lithium-ion battery manufacturing, deployment, and disposal. Mention specific toxic chemicals and end-of-life risks.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 100 MWh grid-scale LiB storage uses NMC 622 cathodes (Co mass fraction 20% in active material, 30% cathode in cell, 90% active material in cathode, specific energy 180 Wh/kg). Calculate (i) total cobalt mass required, and (ii) tonnage of DRC ore (0.3% Co grade, 50% metallurgical recovery) needed. Comment on the environmental implications.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pollution threats of LiB:**

1. **Mineral extraction stage:** Li brine pumping depletes scarce water; Co and Ni sulfide mining generates AMD ($\text{FeS}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{Fe(OH)}_3$).
2. **Cell manufacturing:** NMP solvent (PVDF binder) is a reproductive toxin and VOC; LiPF₆ hydrolyzes to HF; EC/DMC are volatile and flammable.
3. **Deployment:** Thermal runaway at 80–120 °C vents HF, CO, C₂H₄; risk of cascading cell-to-cell propagation in packs.
4. **End-of-life:** < 5% global recycling; shredding produces fine transition-metal oxide dust; landfill leachate contains Li, Co, Ni, Mn.

**(b) Numerical solution:**

Total cell mass:

$$
m_{cell} = \frac{100{,}000{,}000 \text{ Wh}}{180 \text{ Wh/kg}} = 555{,}556 \text{ kg}
$$

Cathode mass:

$$
m_{cathode} = 0.30 \times 555{,}556 = 166{,}667 \text{ kg}
$$

Active NMC mass:

$$
m_{NMC} = 0.90 \times 166{,}667 = 150{,}000 \text{ kg}
$$

**(i) Cobalt mass:**

$$
m_{Co} = 0.20 \times 150{,}000 = 30{,}000 \text{ kg} = 30 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**(ii) DRC ore tonnage:**

$$
m_{ore} = \frac{30{,}000}{0.003 \times 0.50} = 20{,}000{,}000 \text{ kg} = 20{,}000 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Environmental comment:** Each GWh of NMC-622 storage requires **20,000 tonnes of ore** (per 100 MWh; scale to 20,00,000 tonnes per 10 GWh), with proportional tailings, water use, and AMD generation. This motivates the global shift toward **LFP and Na-ion chemistries** that eliminate cobalt and nickel.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in Module 4 (Read Carefully!):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "operational" and "life-cycle" emissions** — Students often state renewables are "zero-emission" and lose 3–4 marks. The KTU answer key requires explicit *life-cycle* thinking.
> 2. **Forgetting units in EPBT/CPBT calculations** — Always carry the unit *years* in the final answer. A bare numerical value will not get full marks.
> 3. **Omitting the denominator in mass-balance derivations** — When asked about SiCl₄ byproduct, students forget to convert between kg and mol using molar mass. The Siemens-process derivation is a board-favorite — practice it!
> 4. **Ignoring Indian grid context** — Generic "renewables are clean" answers are penalized. The KTU 2024 paper values India-specific discussion (Bhadla solar farm, Kasauli PV waste, Lithium reserves in J&K).
> 5. **Skipping the "mitigation hierarchy"** — When asked about pollution control, always structure your answer as: *Prevent → Reduce → Treat → Recover → Dispose* (in that order).
> 6. **Not defining TCLP** — Examiners expect the full form at first mention. Memorize it.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print Before Exam):**
>
> ✅ **Energy Payback Time (EPBT)** = $E_{LC} / E_{gen,annual}$ — typical c-Si PV = **1.5 – 2.5 years**
>
> ✅ **Carbon Payback Time (CPBT)** = $CO_{2,LC} / (E_{gen} \cdot EF_{grid})$ — for India ≈ **0.2 – 0.5 years**
>
> ✅ **System efficiency** = $\eta_{PV} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{BOS}$ (≈ 17–18% practical)
>
> ✅ **Siemens process** produces **SiHCl₃** + byproduct **SiCl₄** (closed-loop plasma converter now standard)
>
> ✅ **HF** is used in PV texturing and is a *lethal* acute toxin
>
> ✅ **TCLP thresholds**: Pb = **5 mg/L**, Cd = **1 mg/L**
>
> ✅ **LiB electrolyte**: LiPF₆ → HF on hydrolysis
>
> ✅ **NMC 622** = 60% Ni, 20% Mn, 20% Co (by mole)
>
> ✅ **LFP (LiFePO₄)** is cobalt-free, safer, longer cycle life — current industry shift
>
> ✅ **Thermal runaway** of LiB at **80 – 120 °C** releases HF, CO, hydrocarbons
>
> ✅ **Cobalt ore grade** in DRC ≈ **0.3%**; **50% recovery** typical
>
> ✅ **Lithium brine extraction** consumes **16,000 – 22,000 L water per ton Li**
>
> ✅ **Mitigation hierarchy**: *Prevent → Reduce → Treat → Recover → Dispose*
>
> ✅ **Circular economy** strategies: First Solar's CdTe recycling (95% Cd, 90% Te recovered)
>
> ✅ **IEA projection**: **78 million tonnes** of PV waste by **2050**
>
> ✅ **India's E-Waste Management Rules 2022** mandate **EPR** for PV and battery manufacturers
>
> ✅ **First-generation PV waste** is arriving **now** (panels installed 2000–2010 reaching EOL)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case study: industrial pollution and disasters - Bhopal tragedy, water pollution control and disposal of wastewater in HPCL Refinery Vishakhapatnam
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT613 - PROCESS DESIGN FOR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case study: industrial pollution and disasters - Bhopal tragedy, water pollution control and disposal of wastewater in HPCL Refinery Vishakhapatnam

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Industrial Pollution and Disasters — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Pollution** is the contamination of the natural environment (air, water, soil) caused by the discharge of unwanted, toxic, or non-biodegradable by-products from industrial processes into the ecosystem at concentrations that exceed the carrying capacity of the receiving medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Disaster** is an unplanned, catastrophic event resulting from sudden or gradual failure of process containment, safety systems, or human operational controls in an industrial facility, leading to mass casualties, environmental damage, and long-term public health emergencies.

### Bhopal Tragedy — Contextual Definition

The **Bhopal Gas Tragedy** (also called the **Bhopal Disaster**) was an accidental release of **Methyl Isocyanate (MIC)** — an extremely toxic, reactive, and volatile intermediate used in the manufacture of the pesticide *Sevin* (Carbaryl) — from the **Union Carbide India Limited (UCIL)** plant in Bhopal, Madhya Pradesh, India, on the night of **December 2–3, 1984**.

### HPCL Refinery Vishakhapatnam — Contextual Definition

The **Hindustan Petroleum Corporation Limited (HPCL) Visakhapatnam Refinery** (operational since 1956; presently combined as **HPCL Visakha Refinery**) is one of India's oldest coastal crude oil processing installations located on the eastern coast of India. Its **Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP)** handles refinery wastewater contaminated with **free oil, emulsified oil, dissolved organics, sulphides, phenols, heavy metals, ammonia, and suspended solids** through a multi-stage physico-chemical and biological treatment train before safe marine discharge.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview**
> 
> Imagine a kitchen pressure cooker. When the safety valve, gasket, and cooling systems work together, the cooker is safe. If someone (a) overfills it, (b) blocks the safety valve with a pebble, and (c) leaves it unattended on a high flame — the cooker will explode, scattering hot contents into the room. The **Bhopal tragedy** is exactly this scenario, scaled to a chemical plant: **MIC tank overfilled → refrigeration and scrubber systems switched off → water ingress → runaway reaction → toxic gas cloud over a sleeping city.**
> 
> Now imagine the same household drains oily water, soap suds, food waste, and chemicals. If all this is dumped into a river, the river becomes a stinking, dead stream. The **HPCL ETP** is like a multi-stage domestic water purifier built on an industrial scale — an *oil-skimming sieve* (API separator), a *shampoo bath* (DAF), and *biological bacteria colony* (activated sludge) that together clean refinery wastewater before it returns to the sea.

### Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Boiling point of MIC**: **39.1 °C** (extremely volatile — vaporises at body temperature)
> - **Vapour density of MIC relative to air**: **≈ 2 (twice heavier than air)** → stays close to ground, drifts into low-lying slums
> - **LC₅₀ (rat, 1-hr inhalation)**: **≈ 5 ppm (≈ 12 mg/m³)** — one of the lowest among industrial chemicals
> - **Odor threshold of MIC**: **≈ 2 ppm** — barely detectable; people did not flee in time
> - **KTU Board Standard**: Toxic chemical exposure limit (TLV-TWA) for refinery operators: **< 10 ppm for H₂S**, **< 1 ppm for benzene vapour**
> - **HPCL Visakhapatnam Refining Capacity**: **8.3 MMTPA** (Million Metric Tonnes Per Annum)
> - **Typical refinery wastewater generation**: **0.4 to 1.6 m³ of wastewater per m³ of crude oil processed**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bhopal Tragedy — Structured Theoretical Analysis

### 2.1.1 Process Background — What is Methyl Isocyanate (MIC)?

**Methyl Isocyanate (CH₃–N=C=O)** is a colourless, highly flammable, lachrymatory (tear-inducing) chemical intermediate. It is synthesised by reacting **monomethylamine (MMA)** with **phosgene (COCl₂)**:

$$
\text{CH}_3\text{NH}_2 + \text{COCl}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{NCO} + 2\,\text{HCl}
$$

MIC is then reacted with **α-naphthol** to produce the carbamate insecticide **Carbaryl (Sevin)**:

$$
\text{CH}_3\text{NCO} + \text{C}_{10}\text{H}_7\text{OH} \rightarrow \text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{11}\text{NO}_2 \text{ (Carbaryl)}
$$

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Failure Cascade (The "Why" and "How")

> [!NOTE]
> The Bhopal disaster was the result of **multiple simultaneous engineering and managerial failures** — not a single cause. KTU examiners frequently test whether students can identify the *chain of failures*.

| # | Failure Mode | Engineering Explanation | Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Over-storage of MIC** | Tank E610 (61 m³ capacity) held **≈ 42 tonnes** of MIC — far exceeding safe inventory norms | Large chemical inventory = massive potential release |
| 2 | **Refrigeration system switched off** | Refrigeration was turned off as a **"cost-cutting measure"** | MIC temperature rose to **≈ 38–40 °C**; vaporisation accelerated |
| 3 | **Vent gas scrubber non-functional** | Sodium hydroxide scrubber (meant to neutralise MIC vent gases) had been **decommissioned for "maintenance"** | Toxic vapours could not be neutralised |
| 4 | **Flare tower shut down** | The flare system designed to burn off vented MIC was offline | No thermal destruction pathway for vapours |
| 5 | **Water ingress into MIC tank** | A faulty water wash line leaked, allowing **~ 1 tonne of water** to enter Tank E610 | Water + MIC → **exothermic reaction** producing CO₂, heat, and accelerating decomposition |
| 6 | **Pressure relief valve inadequate** | Standard PRV capacity insufficient for runaway exothermic reaction | Tank ruptured, releasing **≈ 40 tonnes of MIC** |
| 7 | **No real-time community warning system** | Plant sirens were activated **2 hours after** the leak began; no wind-direction alarm to the community | Population did not evacuate; exposed while sleeping |
| 8 | **Plant located adjacent to dense slum** | UCIL plant built **< 1 km** from Jayaprakash Nagar slum housing **~ 100,000 residents** | Catastrophic population exposure |

### 2.1.3 Toxicology of MIC Exposure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanism of MIC toxicity**: MIC reacts with biological nucleophiles (–NH₂, –OH, –SH groups) in proteins and DNA, causing:
> - **Pulmonary oedema** (fluid in lungs) — primary cause of acute death
> - **Corneal damage / blindness**
> - **Hepatic and renal necrosis**
> - **Chromosomal aberrations** — long-term genetic damage
> - **Reproductive toxicity** — high miscarriage rates in survivors

### 2.1.4 Casualty Figures (Documented)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Immediate deaths (within first 72 hours)**: **≈ 3,800**
> - **Total deaths (long-term)**: **> 15,000–25,000** (estimates vary)
> - **Injuries / chronic illness**: **> 500,000**
> - **Compensation paid by Union Carbide**: **USD 470 million** (1989 settlement) — approximately **< USD 500 per victim**

---

## 2.2 HPCL Refinery Vishakhapatnam — Wastewater Treatment Analysis

### 2.2.1 Refinery Wastewater — Source and Characterisation

Refinery wastewater streams are classified as:

| Stream Type | Typical Pollutants | Flow Characteristics |
|---|---|---|
| **Desalter effluent** | High salinity (Cl⁻ 3,000–10,000 mg/L), free oil | High TDS, hot (60–90 °C) |
| **Crude unit sour water** | H₂S, NH₃, phenols (10–100 mg/L) | High BOD/COD |
| **Process condensate** | Phenols, sulphides, hydrocarbons | Volatile, toxic |
| **Tank farm runoff** | Free oil (100–1,000 mg/L) | Intermittent |
| **Boiler blowdown** | High TDS, phosphate, hydrazine | Hot, low organic |
| **Cooling tower blowdown** | Chromates (legacy), biocides | Continuous |
| **Sanitary wastewater** | BOD 200–400 mg/L, pathogens | Continuous |

### 2.2.2 Refinery ETP — Multi-Stage Treatment Train

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A modern refinery ETP (such as HPCL Visakhapatnam's) employs a **4-stage treatment philosophy**:

**Stage 1 — Primary (Physico-chemical)**
- **API Oil-Water Separator** — gravity separation of free oil (droplets > 150 µm)
- **Corrugated Plate Interceptor (CPI)** — finer oil separation
- **Equalisation basin** — flow & load dampening

**Stage 2 — Secondary (Physico-chemical + Biological)**
- **Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF)** — removes emulsified oil and suspended solids using fine air bubbles
- **Activated Sludge Process (ASP)** — aerobic biological oxidation of dissolved organics
- **Trickling filter / Bio-tower** — alternative biological stage for refinery phenolic wastewater
- **Aerated lagoon** — polishing

**Stage 3 — Tertiary (Advanced)**
- **Sand / multimedia filtration**
- **Activated carbon adsorption** — removes residual COD, colour, refractory organics
- **Ion exchange / Reverse Osmosis** — for water reuse

**Stage 4 — Sludge handling**
- **Dissolved Air Flotation sludge** → dewatering → centrifuge → landfill / incineration
- **Biological sludge** → thickening → anaerobic digestion → dewatering

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Save these formulas — they appear repeatedly in **KTU ESE Module 4 questions**.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **API Separator Removal Efficiency** | $\eta_{oil} = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$ | $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$ = oil concentration (mg/L) |
| 2 | **API Separator Horizontal Velocity Limit** | $v_{h,max} \le 15 \times \dfrac{d}{\sqrt{\rho_o - \rho_w}}$ mm/s | $d$ = droplet diameter (cm), $\rho_o$ = oil density, $\rho_w$ = water density |
| 3 | **DAF Air-to-Solids Ratio** | $A/S = \dfrac{1.3 \times s_a \times (f \times P - 1)}{Q \times C_o}$ | $s_a$ = air solubility, $f$ = saturation fraction, $P$ = pressure (atm), $Q$ = flow, $C_o$ = solids |
| 4 | **F/M Ratio (Food-to-Microorganism)** | $F/M = \dfrac{S_o}{\theta \cdot X}$ | $S_o$ = influent BOD (mg/L), $\theta$ = HRT (d), $X$ = MLVSS (mg/L) |
| 5 | **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)** | $\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | $V$ = reactor volume (m³), $Q$ = flow (m³/d) |
| 6 | **Mean Cell Residence Time (MCRT / SRT)** | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e}$ | $Q_w$ = waste sludge flow, $X$ = MLVSS |
| 7 | **BOD Removal Efficiency** | $\eta_{BOD} = \dfrac{S_o - S_e}{S_o} \times 100$ | $S_o$, $S_e$ = BOD in/out (mg/L) |
| 8 | **BOD₅ to BODₗ (Ultimate)** | $BOD_L = \dfrac{BOD_5}{1 - e^{-k \cdot 5}}$ | $k$ = deoxygenation constant (d⁻¹) |
| 9 | **Reaeration Coefficient (O₂ Transfer)** | $K_L a_{(T)} = K_L a_{(20)} \times (1.024)^{(T-20)}$ | $T$ = wastewater temperature (°C) |
| 10 | **Sludge Volume Index (SVI)** | $SVI = \dfrac{100 \cdot V_{30}}{X}$ mL/g | $V_{30}$ = settled volume in 30 min (mL/L), $X$ = MLSS (mg/L) |
| 11 | **Settling Velocity (Stokes' Law)** | $v_s = \dfrac{g \, (\rho_p - \rho_w) \, d^2}{18 \, \mu}$ | $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $d$ = particle diameter, $\mu$ = viscosity |
| 12 | **Fenton Oxidation COD Removal** | $COD_{rem} = a \cdot \left[\dfrac{H_2O_2}{Fe^{2+}}\right] + b$ | Empirical; a, b determined experimentally |
| 13 | **Refinery Specific Wastewater** | $Q_{ww} = (0.4 \text{ to } 1.6) \times Q_{crude}$ | $Q_{crude}$ = crude processed (m³/d) |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU board valuation, **forgetting units** in any of the above formulas leads to **½ mark deduction per occurrence**. Always state: mg/L, m³/d, °C, mL/g, etc.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - The Bhopal case study is the **founding case for India's Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, 1989** and the **Environment Protection Act, 1986**.
> - The **HPCL Visakhapatnam ETP** serves as a **benchmark ZLD (Zero Liquid Discharge) model** for Indian coastal refineries.
> - These case studies form the basis of **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability)** and **QRA (Quantitative Risk Assessment)** studies required by the **National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough — Bhopal MIC Release Mass Balance

### Problem Statement (KTU-style)

A MIC storage tank contains **42 tonnes (42,000 kg)** of liquid MIC at 25 °C. The tank has a refrigeration unit that maintains MIC at 4 °C. Due to refrigeration failure, MIC warms to 38 °C. Using the Clausius-Clapeyron relation, estimate the **vapour pressure of MIC** at 38 °C and the **mass of MIC that flashes to vapour**, assuming the headspace volume is 18 m³.

**Given data:**
- Molecular weight of MIC, M = 57.05 g/mol
- Normal boiling point of MIC, T_b = 39.1 °C = 312.25 K
- Latent heat of vaporisation of MIC, ΔH_vap = 29.6 kJ/mol
- Universal gas constant, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)

### Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Convert storage temperature to Kelvin**

$$
T_1 = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15 \, \text{K}
$$

$$
T_2 = 38 + 273.15 = 311.15 \, \text{K}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Clausius-Clapeyron Equation**

The Clausius-Clapeyron equation in integrated form:

$$
\ln \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)
$$

**Step 3: Identify reference pressure P₁**

At the normal boiling point T_b = 312.25 K, the vapour pressure equals atmospheric pressure:

$$
P_1 = P_{atm} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa at } T_1 = 312.25 \, \text{K}
$$

> *Wait — we need P₁ at T₂ = 311.15 K. Let's re-parameterise using T_b as reference.*

Re-write the equation with **T_b as reference (P = 1 atm)**:

$$
\ln \left(\dfrac{P_2}{1 \, \text{atm}}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_b}\right)
$$

**Step 4: Substitute numerical values**

$$
\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} = \dfrac{29{,}600 \, \text{J/mol}}{8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}} = 3560.5 \, \text{K}
$$

$$
\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_b} = \dfrac{1}{311.15} - \dfrac{1}{312.25} = 0.0032139 - 0.0032029 = 0.0000110 \, \text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\ln \left(\dfrac{P_2}{1}\right) = -3560.5 \times 0.0000110 = -0.03917
$$

**Step 5: Solve for P₂**

$$
P_2 = e^{-0.03917} = 0.9616 \, \text{atm}
$$

$$
P_2 \approx 0.962 \, \text{atm} \approx 97.4 \, \text{kPa}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: At 38 °C (only 1.1 °C below the boiling point of 39.1 °C), MIC exerts a vapour pressure of **≈ 0.96 atm** — i.e., it is on the verge of boiling and will flash to vapour spontaneously.

**Step 6: Calculate mass of MIC vapour in headspace using Ideal Gas Law**

$$
PV = nRT \Rightarrow n = \dfrac{P V}{RT}
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{(97{,}400 \, \text{Pa}) \times (18 \, \text{m}^3)}{(8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}) \times (311.15 \, \text{K})}
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{1{,}753{,}200}{2587.16} = 677.65 \, \text{mol}
$$

**Step 7: Convert moles to mass**

$$
m = n \times M = 677.65 \times 57.05 = 38{,}656 \, \text{g} \approx 38.7 \, \text{kg}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result**: Even *before* any runaway reaction, the tank headspace alone held **≈ 39 kg of toxic MIC vapour**. With runaway exothermic reaction driving temperature well above 200 °C, *tonnes* of MIC flashed and escaped — consistent with the documented **≈ 40 tonnes** release.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Walkthrough — HPCL Refinery ETP Design Calculation

### Problem Statement (KTU-style)

The HPCL Visakhapatnam refinery processes **8.3 MMTPA** of crude oil (density 870 kg/m³). The ETP receives process wastewater at a rate of **0.8 m³ of wastewater per m³ of crude processed**. The influent BOD₅ is 600 mg/L and the effluent discharge standard requires BOD₅ $\le$ 30 mg/L. Design an **Activated Sludge Process (ASP)** unit.

**Required:**
1. Calculate the daily wastewater flow (m³/d).
2. Calculate the required BOD removal efficiency.
3. Determine the aeration tank volume, given F/M = 0.25 d⁻¹ and MLVSS = 3000 mg/L.

### Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Convert annual crude processing to daily throughput**

$$
Q_{crude,annual} = 8.3 \times 10^6 \, \text{tonnes/year}
$$

$$
Q_{crude,daily} = \dfrac{8.3 \times 10^9 \, \text{kg/yr}}{365 \, \text{d} \times 870 \, \text{kg/m}^3}
$$

$$
Q_{crude,daily} = \dfrac{8.3 \times 10^9}{317{,}550} = 26{,}137 \, \text{m}^3/\text{d}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate daily wastewater flow**

$$
Q_{ww} = 0.8 \times Q_{crude,daily} = 0.8 \times 26{,}137 = 20{,}910 \, \text{m}^3/\text{d}
$$

$$
Q_{ww} \approx 2.09 \times 10^4 \, \text{m}^3/\text{d}
$$

**Step 3: BOD removal efficiency**

$$
\eta_{BOD} = \dfrac{S_o - S_e}{S_o} \times 100 = \dfrac{600 - 30}{600} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{BOD} = \dfrac{570}{600} \times 100 = 95\%
$$

**Step 4: Apply F/M ratio formula**

$$
F/M = \dfrac{S_o}{\theta \cdot X}
$$

where $\theta$ = HRT = $V / Q$

Rearrange:

$$
\theta = \dfrac{S_o}{(F/M) \cdot X}
$$

$$
\theta = \dfrac{600 \, \text{mg/L}}{0.25 \, \text{d}^{-1} \times 3000 \, \text{mg/L}} = \dfrac{600}{750} = 0.8 \, \text{d}
$$

**Step 5: Aeration tank volume**

$$
V = Q_{ww} \times \theta = 20{,}910 \times 0.8 = 16{,}728 \, \text{m}^3
$$

$$
V \approx 1.67 \times 10^4 \, \text{m}^3
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Summary**:
> 
> | Parameter | Value |
> |---|---|
> | Wastewater flow | **20,910 m³/d** |
> | BOD removal efficiency | **95 %** |
> | Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) | **0.8 days (≈ 19.2 hours)** |
> | Aeration tank volume | **≈ 16,728 m³** |

**Step 6: Oxygen requirement (bonus — frequently asked)**

Theoretical oxygen demand for BOD removal:

$$
O_2 = \dfrac{Q \times (S_o - S_e)}{1000} \times 1.42 \, \text{kg O}_2/\text{day}
$$

where 1.42 = oxygen equivalent of biomass (C₅H₇NO₂).

$$
O_2 = \dfrac{20{,}910 \times (600 - 30)}{1000} \times 1.42 = \dfrac{20{,}910 \times 570}{1000} \times 1.42
$$

$$
O_2 = 11{,}918.7 \times 1.42 = 16{,}924 \, \text{kg O}_2/\text{d} \approx 16.9 \, \text{t/d}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — The Two Case Studies

| Dimension | Bhopal Tragedy (UCIL, 1984) | HPCL Visakhapatnam Refinery |
|---|---|---|
| **Hazard type** | Acute airborne toxic release | Continuous aqueous effluent discharge |
| **Chemical** | Methyl Isocyanate (MIC) | Crude oil derivatives, phenols, H₂S, NH₃ |
| **Onset** | Sudden (hours) | Chronic / long-term |
| **Population exposed** | ≈ 500,000 in single night | Coastal ecosystem + workers |
| **Primary exposure route** | Inhalation | Ingestion / dermal / aquatic |
| **Engineering lesson** | Safety system redundancy; inventory minimisation | Multi-barrier ETP; ZLD; continuous monitoring |
| **Regulatory outcome** | MSIHC Rules 1989, EPA 1986, Public Liability Insurance Act 1991 | CPCB / APPCB discharge norms; consent to operate |
| **Environmental remediation** | Soil and groundwater MIC still traceable | Marine outfall dilution + oil spill contingency |
| **KTU cognitive level tested** | Apply, Analyse (cause-effect chain) | Design, Evaluate (process sizing) |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*List any **three** engineering failures that contributed to the Bhopal gas tragedy of 1984.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Refrigeration system switched off as a cost-cutting measure** — caused MIC temperature to rise toward its boiling point of 39.1 °C, increasing vapour pressure and inventory volatility.
2. **Vent gas scrubber and flare tower were non-functional** — neutralisation (NaOH scrubber) and thermal destruction (flare) pathways were unavailable; toxic vapours escaped unmitigated.
3. **Water ingress into MIC storage Tank E610** via a leaking water-wash line — water reacted exothermically with MIC, generating CO₂ and heat, leading to runaway reaction and tank rupture.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

*State the **function of the API separator** in a refinery effluent treatment plant.*

**Model Answer:**

The **API (American Petroleum Institute) separator** is a **gravity-based primary treatment unit** that separates **free oil** (droplet diameter > 150 µm) from refinery wastewater. The wastewater flows horizontally at a velocity $\le$ 15 mm/s through a rectangular tank; due to density difference between oil (ρ ≈ 850–900 kg/m³) and water (ρ ≈ 1000 kg/m³), oil rises to the surface and is skimmed off by mechanical scrapers, while sludge settles at the bottom. **Typical removal efficiency: 60–95% of free oil**, producing an effluent with oil content $\le$ 50 mg/L before secondary treatment.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, students answer **ONE** full question per module (carrying **14 marks**). Each Part B question has sub-parts **(a) [7 marks]** and **(b) [7 marks]**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat flow diagram, explain the **sequence of failures** that led to the **Bhopal gas tragedy** of December 1984. Discuss the role of **(i) MIC storage inventory**, **(ii) refrigeration system**, and **(iii) scrubber / flare tower failure** in the disaster. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the **toxicological effects of Methyl Isocyanate (MIC)** on exposed populations. What **long-term health impacts** were documented among survivors? Suggest **three preventive engineering controls** that should have been in place. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Sequence of failures — Flow Diagram & Explanation:**

```
MIC Over-stored (42 t) → Refrigeration OFF (T↑) → 
Scrubber OFF + Flare OFF → Water ingress → 
Runaway Exothermic Reaction → Tank Rupture → 
40 t MIC Released → Sirens Late → Mass Exposure
```

**Role of each component:**

- **(i) MIC storage inventory**: Union Carbide stored ≈ 42 tonnes of MIC in a single tank — far exceeding the **inventory threshold** of MSDS guidelines (typically < 5 tonnes). The plant was producing less due to reduced sales, so MIC was stockpiled — a fundamental **inherent safety violation**. *Larger inventory = larger potential release*.

- **(ii) Refrigeration system**: The refrigeration unit kept MIC below its boiling point (39.1 °C). When switched off (cost saving), MIC temperature rose to ≈ 38–40 °C, pushing vapour pressure toward atmospheric. **Clausius-Clapeyron calculation** (shown in Section 3.1) confirms P ≈ 0.96 atm at 38 °C.

- **(iii) Scrubber and flare tower failure**: The caustic scrubber would have neutralised MIC vent gases as: **CH₃NCO + NaOH → CH₃NH₂ + Na₂CO₃**. With the scrubber offline, vapours vented directly to atmosphere. The flare tower would have thermally destroyed MIC via combustion. Both were unavailable — eliminating the **last line of defence**.

**(b) Toxicological effects and preventive controls:**

**Toxicological effects of MIC:**

| System Affected | Acute Effect | Chronic Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Respiratory | Pulmonary oedema, bronchospasm, ARDS | Reduced lung function, fibrosis |
| Ocular | Severe lacrimation, corneal ulceration | Cataracts, blindness |
| Hepatic | Centrilobular necrosis | Cirrhosis |
| Renal | Tubular necrosis | Chronic kidney disease |
| Reproductive | – | Spontaneous abortions, neonatal mortality |
| Genetic | – | Chromosomal aberrations, micronuclei |

**Preventive engineering controls:**

1. **Inherent Safety — Inventory Minimisation**: Store only the minimum quantity of MIC needed for ≤ 7 days of production (per TLV-based safety stock). Eliminate intermediate storage where possible.

2. **Multiple Engineering Redundancies**: Install *dual* refrigeration systems, *dual* scrubbers (caustic + aqueous), *dual* flare lines, and *dual* pressure relief valves — each independently capable of handling full release load.

3. **Real-time Atmospheric Monitoring + Automated Community Sirens**: Continuous MIC detectors at plant perimeter with auto-triggered sirens and SMS alerts to residents within a 5 km radius — independent of plant operator action.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Describe the **sources and characteristics of wastewater** generated in an oil refinery such as **HPCL Visakhapatnam**. Draw the **block diagram of the Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP)** showing all four treatment stages. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Design an **activated sludge aeration tank** for the HPCL Visakhapatnam refinery ETP given the following data: **[7 marks]**

> Refining capacity = 8.3 MMTPA, crude density = 870 kg/m³, wastewater generation coefficient = 0.8 m³/m³ crude, influent BOD₅ = 600 mg/L, effluent BOD₅ required ≤ 30 mg/L, F/M ratio = 0.25 d⁻¹, MLVSS = 3000 mg/L.

Determine: **(i)** Daily wastewater flow, **(ii)** BOD removal efficiency, **(iii)** Hydraulic retention time, **(iv)** Aeration tank volume.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Sources, characteristics, and ETP block diagram:**

**Sources & Characteristics of Refinery Wastewater** (as detailed in Section 2.2.1)

**Block Diagram:**

```
Crude Desalter Effluent  ─┐
Sour Water from Cracker   ─┤
Tank Farm Runoff          ─┼─► EQUALISATION ─► API SEPARATOR ─► CPI ─► DAF
Process Condensate        ─┤
Cooling Tower Blowdown    ─┤                                         │
Sanitary Wastewater       ─┘                                         ▼
                                                              ACTIVATED SLUDGE
                                                                      │
                                                                      ▼
                                                             SECONDARY CLARIFIER
                                                                      │
                                                                      ▼
                                                         SAND FILTER + ACTIVATED CARBON
                                                                      │
                                                                      ▼
                                                          TREATED EFFLUENT (Marine Outfall)

   All sludge streams ──► SLUDGE THICKENER ──► ANAEROBIC DIGESTER ──► CENTRIFUGE ──► LANDFILL
```

**(b) Design calculations** (as detailed in Section 3.2):

| S.No. | Parameter | Calculation | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | Daily wastewater flow | $(0.8 \times 8.3 \times 10^9)/(365 \times 870)$ | **20,910 m³/d** |
| (ii) | BOD removal efficiency | $[(600-30)/600] \times 100$ | **95 %** |
| (iii) | Hydraulic retention time | $600 / (0.25 \times 3000)$ | **0.8 d (≈ 19.2 h)** |
| (iv) | Aeration tank volume | $20{,}910 \times 0.8$ | **16,728 m³** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> 
> **Common mistakes that cost marks:**
> 
> 1. **Unit error in crude conversion**: Writing *"8.3 MMTPA / 365"* without converting tonnes → kg OR forgetting to divide by crude density (870 kg/m³) — examiners deduct **2 marks**.
> 
> 2. **F/M ratio formula confusion**: Writing F/M = (Q × S)/(V × X) instead of S/(θ × X). Both are equivalent, but if the formula used in derivation is **inconsistent** with the numerical substitution, full marks are NOT awarded.
> 
> 3. **In Bhopal questions**: Writing only *"human error"* as the cause — examiners expect **engineering and management failures** (refrigeration, scrubber, flare, inventory). Generic answers fetch ≤ 2 out of 7 marks.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting the "Why"** in ETP design: Just writing numbers without explaining *why* F/M = 0.25 is chosen (typical range 0.2–0.5 for conventional ASP) loses **½ mark**.
> 
> 5. **Missing units in final answer**: "Aeration tank volume = 16,728" — without "m³" loses **½ mark**.

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 Case Studies**

### Bhopal Tragedy — Key Takeaways

- **Date**: December 2–3, **1984** | **Plant**: Union Carbide India Ltd (UCIL), Bhopal
- **Chemical**: **Methyl Isocyanate (CH₃–N=C=O)** — boiling point **39.1 °C**, vapour density ≈ 2 (heavier than air), LC₅₀ ≈ 5 ppm
- **Released quantity**: ≈ **40 tonnes** of MIC vapour
- **Casualties**: **3,800+ immediate deaths**, **15,000–25,000 total deaths**, **500,000+ injured**
- **Causes (Mnemonic — "RISK-3")**: **R**efrigeration OFF, **I**nventory overstored, **S**crubber/flare OFF, **K**ey interlocks bypassed, **3**-hour delay in alarm
- **Toxic effects**: Pulmonary oedema, blindness, genetic damage, reproductive harm
- **Legal outcome**: USD 470 million settlement, criminal prosecution (Bhopal District Court conviction 2010)
- **Lessons**: Inherent safety, redundancy, land-use buffer zones, community early-warning systems

### HPCL Visakhapatnam ETP — Key Takeaways

- **Capacity**: 8.3 MMTPA crude processing
- **Wastewater generation**: 0.4 to 1.6 m³/m³ of crude
- **Four ETP stages**: Primary (API + CPI) → Secondary (DAF + ASP/Biofilter) → Tertiary (Sand + AC) → Sludge handling
- **Key design parameters**: F/M = 0.2–0.5 d⁻¹, MLVSS = 2000–4000 mg/L, HRT = 4–24 h, SVI = 50–150 mL/g
- **Discharge route**: Marine outfall after tertiary polishing; sludge to secure landfill
- **Pollutants removed**: Free oil (60–95% in API), emulsified oil (DAF), dissolved organics (BOD 95% in ASP), phenols (biofilter), residual colour/COD (activated carbon)

### Universal Pollution Control Principles (Both Cases)

- **Hierarchy of Controls**: Eliminate → Minimise → Engineer → Admin → PPE
- **Defence in Depth**: Multiple independent safety barriers
- **Inherent Safety**: Reduce inventory, less hazardous chemistry, milder conditions
- **Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD)**: Modern benchmark for refineries
- **Precautionary Principle**: Polluter pays, sustainable development
- **KTU Hot Topics**: HAZOP, QRA, MSDS, MSD, OSHA, EPA 1986, MSIHC Rules 1989, CPCB norms

### Frequently Asked Numerical Templates (Practise Before Exam)

- Tank vapour pressure via Clausius-Clapeyron
- API separator removal efficiency and Stokes' law settling
- F/M ratio and aeration tank volume
- BOD₅ ↔ BODₗ conversion
- Oxygen transfer rate corrections with temperature
- Sludge Volume Index (SVI) interpretation
